[Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of mythological fiction that earns most of what it attempts. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. Frankie's first-person narration has a distinctive, earned quality — the inventory-brain of someone who has spent years in the sneaker industry, the grief-deflection of someone who won't let herself feel things directly, the granddaughter's muscle memory of a shop she claims to have left behind. The voice is consistent across all eight chapters without becoming monotonous. Lines like "Papou bought neutral like the world was ending" and "Pride is hereditary, and the stitch died with him" feel genuinely discovered rather than manufactured. The mythological integration is handled with unusual restraint and intelligence. The gods are never named except Nike (and even then, only once, in a moment of genuine weight). M.B. (Charon), the three sisters (the Fates), the racing flats on the wire (Hermes), the winged high-tops — all of this is layered in through implication, ledger entries, and physical detail rather than exposition. The drachma coin, the river-wet boots, the pedals that get wet — these accumulate into revelation without announcement. This is genuinely sophisticated mythological fiction. The central metaphor — a cobbler's shop as the threshold between the mortal and divine, the last hour as sacred time — is not just clever but structurally sound. It holds the whole piece together. The ledger entries ("paid: one favorable wind," "paid: a song I can't write down") are among the best details in the piece. The emotional core — Frankie's estrangement from her grandfather, the wound of being told to leave, the posthumous understanding of why — is handled with real delicacy. The scene in Chapter 6 where she confronts the fight, the ruined leather, the "You should have taught me" — this is the emotional climax of the piece and it lands. The revelation that Papou was protecting her from the shop's true nature, and that he was wrong to do so, is genuinely complex. The selling of the winged high-tops is the piece's moral center, and it's handled well. Frankie knows she's doing wrong while she does it, the record-keeping voice ("I want this on the record") becomes self-indicting, and the consequences are appropriately quiet rather than dramatic. WEAKNESSES: The pacing in the middle chapters (3-5) occasionally loses momentum. Chapter 4 (Mateo) is necessary but feels slightly long — the developer scene, while well-written, doesn't advance the mythological plot and the rendering detail is belabored. The piece could tighten here. Some of the self-conscious meta-commentary ("I want this on the record," "I'm keeping the record honest") is used effectively but occasionally tips into a tic. By the fifth or sixth instance, it's doing less work than it was. The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally the mythological characters speak in ways that are slightly too on-the-nose. Nike's "They don't even know who they're running from" and "We used to run people to death, and they thanked us" are good, but the marathon speech in Chapter 3 is the weakest dialogue in the piece — it explains too much of what the imagery is already showing. The Fates scene is better because it's more oblique. The ending of Chapter 7 — "You're particular," she says, which is her word, her insult, and in her mouth now it's something else entirely — is slightly over-explained. The reader has already understood the reversal; the gloss weakens it. The final chapter is very good but the last paragraph ("Gods wore sneakers. I know — I resoled them.") is the one moment where the piece becomes slightly self-congratulatory, winking at its own premise. It's earned, but it's also the one place where the restraint that characterizes the whole piece briefly fails. The transformation of Frankie from someone who sold the winged shoes to someone who has accepted the shop's true nature is handled well overall, but the speed of her acceptance in Chapter 7 (making Nike's shoes, going to the track) could use slightly more resistance. The grief chapter (6) does the heavy lifting, but the pivot from "I sold the sacred shoes" to "I'm making new ones" happens in a single sleepless night, which is almost but not quite fully earned. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a genuinely impressive piece — one of the better examples of contemporary mythological fiction I've encountered in this format. The voice is distinctive and consistent, the mythological integration is sophisticated, the emotional core is real, and the prose is controlled without being cold. The weaknesses are real but minor relative to the achievement. This is the work of a writer who has thought carefully about what they're doing and executed it with considerable skill. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 14 World Building: 19 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 83.3/100
+ Sci-Fi First Contact — First Contact (Chapter Avg: 82.7 | Final: 85.5)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Sci-Fi First Contact Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: A month ago the megastructures arrived. First, the fish started dying. Then they spoke.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Sci-Fi First Contact Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: A month ago the megastructures arrived. First, the fish started dying. Then they spoke. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Reading the prompt - Three loaded images: **megastructures** (scale, unknowability, awe), **fish dying** (ecological and economic collapse, oceans as the contact zone), **"then they spoke"** (delicious pronoun ambiguity — does "they" mean the structures, or *the fish*?). - The one-month timeline means this is a *post-arrival* story. The shock has worn off; dread has set in. We start in the eerie normalization phase, not the panic. - The prompt's real engine is the causal eeriness: dying → speech. The best versions will make those two things *causally* linked, not coincidental. ## Premise options — who is speaking, and why? 1. **Death as the medium.** The structures communicate *through* marine life, and the signal kills the carriers. Every word costs a school, a pod. Messages appear as schools of fish forming glyphs in the water, visible from above, before dying. Communication with a body count — do we keep asking questions when every answer kills? 2. **Translation / testimony.** The structures came because Earth's oceans were *already* dying (warming, acidification). They're not speaking *to* us — they're translating what the ocean is saying as it dies. "They spoke" = the fish, finally audible. Humanity is not the contacted party; we're the audience, or the accused. 3. **Here for the whales.** The aliens' taxonomy identifies cetaceans as Earth's intelligence. The fish die as collateral of failed attempts to talk to whales. Humanity's humbling role: interpreters in a conversation not meant for us. 4. **The archive / eulogy.** The structures are witnesses that record dying biospheres. The speech is the archive being voiced — the ocean's own eulogy, spoken in borrowed words. 5. **Xenoforming eviction.** The die-off is deliberate preparation. The speech is a notice: *make room*. Darker, more thriller-shaped. ## Character options - **Whale-fall ecologist** — studies what death feeds, how a sunken whale becomes an ecosystem. Her expertise *is* the theme. Bonus: a career of hydrophone archives of a changing ocean, which could become functionally important later. Personal irony: she spent decades saying "the ocean is dying" and no one listened; now the ocean says it itself, and she's the one who must listen. - **Fisheries observer / fisherman** — blue-collar grit, immediate livelihood stakes, witnesses the first glyph-school from a trawler deck. Harder to plug into any decoding plot. - **Acoustician / ex-naval sonar tech** — sound as the vector; resonance with real-world sonar harming whales. Our noise hurt them first; now their speech hurts. - **A "listener"** — someone who perceives the signal differently (cochlear implant, neurodivergence). Risk of gimmick. - **Child POV** — wonder and horror, but struggles to carry plot in 8k words. ## Tone options - Slow-burn **dread** (Lovecraft-by-way-of-the-tide-pool). - **Elegy** — melancholy, literary, Arrival-adjacent intimacy. - **Awe/wonder** — Clarkean, risks blunting the fish dying. - **Thriller** — countdowns, military standoffs; propulsive but generic. - Most promising: dread braided with elegy. A story that aches more than it races. ## Imagery / set pieces - A school of fish forming a glyph in harbor water, then floating belly-up. - A mass stranding where the beached pod vocalizes in chorus — words, not song. - Bioluminescent messages pulsing along a structure's hull at night, answered by the dying glow of plankton. - The structure visible from shore at all times — a gothic fact on the horizon. Does it ever *do* anything? (Restraint = awe. Maybe it "breathes" once.) - Hydrophone recordings: the sound of a healthy reef from forty years ago vs. now. - Dead zones spreading in clean geometric shapes — patterns that are themselves language. ## Devices - **Chapter epigraphs as decoded message fragments** — each chapter opens with a piece of translation; by chapter 8 the fragments read as a single statement. Compresses exposition, accumulates dread. - **Day-count chapter titles** ("Day 34," "Day 41") — organic urgency from the prompt's own "a month ago." - **Retrospective first-person narration** — mirrors the prompt's report-like cadence, allows elegant compression, and sets up a devastating closing line. - News bulletins / official statements used sparingly for offscreen world-response (panic, militaries, cults) without spending scenes on them. ## Possible beat maps (8 chapters × ~1000 words) **Variant A — Mystery/decoding escalation:** 1. World a month in; protagonist's wound; the die-off as background becoming foreground. 2. First witnessed speech event (visceral set piece — the choral stranding or glyph school). 3. Institutions circle; pattern noticed: messages correlate exactly with die-off events. 4. Translation breakthrough; first full message — reframe #1. 5. The cost-per-word truth undeniable; ethical crisis; second message reveals intent — reframe #2. 6. Human response fractures; personal stakes peak; a choice crystallizes. 7. Climax: the protagonist acts — a reply, a sacrifice, a refusal. 8. Aftermath; cost counted; final haunting image. **Variant B — Grief/testimony escalation:** 1. Counting the dead, routine as liturgy. 2. The fish speak — and speak *to* something in her past or her work. 3. She realizes the messages are the ocean's testimony, not the aliens' words. 4. The world demands the structures be destroyed/silenced — killing the ocean's only voice. 5. What the testimony actually says — complicating, not accusing. 6. She chooses to answer. 7. The reply: a living medium (a recording of a healthy ocean; a released tagged whale carrying song) — answering death-as-medium with life-as-medium. 8. Do they understand the difference? Ambiguous, resonant close. ## Ending options - The reply is sent; the response is silence — or one final, differently-costly word. - The final message is a *question* the story leaves hanging. - The structures go quiet permanently, leaving humanity holding the archive/testimony. - The last message addresses the protagonist personally (powerful, but risks Solaris pastiche and sentimentality). ## What to avoid - Military standoff as the climax; "they came for our water"; a montage-y genius-linguist decoding. - Showing the aliens. Structures only. - Letting the eco-theme become a lecture ("stop polluting" as the literal message). - Over-stuffing: no dual POV, no cult subplot beyond a line, no geopolitics beyond epigraphs. # Reflection The first thing I notice is that premise options 1, 2, and 4 keep bleeding into each other in my mind — death-as-medium, testimony, and archive all share the same emotional core: **the speech and the dying are one event**. That feels like the prompt's true center of gravity. If the fish dying were just a side effect and the structures spoke by radio, the prompt's two sentences would be decoration next to each other instead of a mechanism. I want the causality. Option 3 (here for the whales) is clever and humbling, but I suspect it spends its energy on a twist rather than an ache, and 8,000 words can't afford cleverness that doesn't pay emotional rent. Option 5 turns it into a thriller, and the world doesn't need another first-contact eviction notice. On "who is speaking": the prompt's grammar points at the fish, and honoring that reading — the fish themselves speaking — is what would distinguish this from a hundred generic megastructure stories. But I don't think the story has to choose crudely. The most haunting version might be the one where the characters can't tell whether the voice belongs to the aliens, the fish, or the ocean itself — and where the answer, when it comes, reframes everything. Something like: the structures didn't come to speak; they came to *translate*. Whether that translation kills the speakers as a cost of the medium is the open question I keep circling — it creates the strongest dramatic engine (every answer costs lives; do we keep listening?), but I want to be careful it doesn't become a gimmick that overshadows the grief. The whale-fall ecologist is pulling ahead of every other protagonist option, and I want to interrogate why. It's not just that her job is thematic (an expert in what death feeds, confronting death-as-message — that's almost too neat). It's that she solves plot problems: she has hydrophone archives, water access, credibility to be near the events, and a built-in reason to be the first to believe the fish are speaking. The irony of her being a Cassandra — decades of documenting the ocean's decline to indifference, and now everyone finally listens when it's too late — gives her a bitter interiority that doesn't require a dead-spouse backstory bolted on. Actually, that's a real decision point to hold open: does she need a personal loss threaded through, or is the ocean-grief sufficient? My instinct is that adding a drowned loved one risks crowding 8k words and sliding toward Solaris; the career-guilt wound may be the cleaner one. The risk with a scientist POV is
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **The premise (locked).** The structures are *witnesses* — they travel toward the sound of dying biospheres, and Earth's ocean has been screaming for a century. Their function is to translate and archive a dying world's testimony. The catch is the medium: the translation runs through living tissue. Schools, pods, reefs are the ink; every message is written in bodies and the writing kills them. This unifies the prompt's three beats into one mechanism: the arrival, the dying, and the speaking are a single event. The fish die first because the first messages took a month to surface. The fish speak because the voice is the ocean's own — the structures never say a word on their own behalf. Whether the witness knows the cost, and whether it considers the cost acceptable, stays unresolved. **The protagonist (locked).** Dr. Maren Vold, a whale-fall ecologist at a small marine station at the head of a Norwegian fjord; the structure stands at the fjord's mouth, visible from her beach every day. Her wound is career-grief, not a dead relative: forty years documenting the ocean's decline in an archive nobody listened to. She is a Cassandra whose recordings were her failed testimony — until they become the *reply*. Her arc runs from bitter archivist → horrified interpreter → the one who answers. The reflection's unfinished worry — that a scientist POV risks clinical detachment — is answered by making grief her instrument: she processes horror through ritual (the counting of the dead), and the narration lives in sensory detail, not abstraction. **Cast (kept deliberately tiny).** Jun, a young postdoc who does the signal work — decoding gets a human face without a genius-linguist montage. Einar, an aging fisherman whose livelihood dies in the die-off — the economic wound with one face instead of a subplot. A resident humpback the station calls Kveld, silent since the arrival — a minimal thread that pays off at the climax. All governments, navies, commissions, and cults exist **only in epigraphs and radio voices**, never onstage. **Voice and style (locked).** Retrospective first-person, past tense — Maren narrating from an unspecified later point, mirroring the prompt's own report-like cadence. Marine-biological diction treated as poetry: species names and Latin binomials deployed sparingly, like psalm verses. Short declaratives at emotional peaks, deliberately echoing the prompt's rhythm ("First the fish died. Then they spoke."), so the final line can land as a variation on that chord. Restraint over sentimentality: the ache must be earned through images (belly-up glyph schools, a chorus of beached whales, a reef recording from 1987), never declared. **Structural devices (locked).** Chapters titled by day-count ("Day 34" through "Day 60"), converting the prompt's "a month ago" into organic countdown pressure. Each chapter opens with an italicized epigraph — mostly decoded fragments of the testimony, which accumulate across the story into a single arc: *counts → self-naming → a litany of memory → a question → [chapter 6: a line from Maren's own ignored life's work] → song → a completed epitaph.* This compresses exposition, accumulates dread, and makes the translation plot visible without scenes of people staring at screens. **The climax (locked).** The penultimate message decodes as a question — *will anyone else hear us?* — while the world votes to silence the structures to stop the dying. Maren realizes her forty-year archive is the answer: proof of witness. She plays her oldest recording — a healthy reef, deafening, alive — into the water at the structure's foot. For the first time, the structure speaks in its *own* body: the healthy-reef song rendered as light along its hull. A message that costs nothing living. Kveld surfaces and joins it — one living voice. Then the structures go dark: the testimony is complete, the archive now ends in song instead of silence, and the dying stops. **Ending register.** Elegy without absolution. The testimony did not save the ocean; it only made the dying heard. The structures remain as dark monuments. Maren's final movement is small and human — she returns to counting, but the meaning of the counting has changed. **Explicit avoidances.** No aliens shown, ever — structures only, and they do almost nothing (they "breathe" once; restraint is the awe). No techno-babble mechanism talk. No military climax — the strike is a deadline heard over a radio, never a scene. The message never says "stop polluting"; the indictment is assembled by the reader from the litany, never delivered as a lecture. No dead-relative backstory, no dual POV, no cult subplot beyond a single epigraph line, no "they came for our water," and the testimony never addresses Maren personally — she must resist reading herself into it, and the narration will name that temptation rather than indulge it. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Day 34."** *Epigraph: an unexplained string of counts.* Open on Maren's morning ritual: walking the tide line, tallying the washed-up dead — counting as liturgy. Establish the normalized dread of a month in: the structure standing at the fjord's mouth like a fact, the silent hydrophones, Einar's boat coming in empty, Kveld's song absent from the water. Weave her history lightly: the archive, the ignored papers, the bitterness. **Turn/hook:** mapping the dead zones, she realizes the kill-patterns are too clean — geometric. The dying has a shape, and the shape looks like *writing*. **Chapter 2 — "Day 38."** *Epigraph: the first decoded sentence fragment.* The set piece: a pod strands in the shallows below the station. The town gathers. The whales vocalize in unison — not song, almost *words* — and die. Jun runs the recording against the kill-shapes: they match. **Turn:** the horrifying reframe — they've been watching messages arrive for a month and calling it a die-off. The dying *is* the speaking. **Chapter 3 — "Day 41."** *Epigraph: testimony fragment — the first memory.* Confirmation chapter, handled through people not whiteboards: Jun plots die-off events against acoustic events — perfect correlation. Every word costs a school. The working understanding forms: the structures translate; the ocean is the speaker; the fish are the ink. World-response arrives only as radio voices and epigraph-interstitials. Maren alone with her archive, listening to a reef recording from 1987 — loud enough to steer by — against the silence outside. **Turn/hook:** governments are assembling to decide whether to silence the structures — kill the voice to stop the dying. A shadow-deadline enters. **Chapter 4 — "Day 45."** *Epigraph: litany fragment.* The testimony comes home: a message forms in the fjord itself — a glyph in the harbor written in the herring, Einar's whole remaining livelihood dying in a single night. Decoded, it is not accusation but *recitation*: what was here, what it sounded like, what is gone. **Turn (reframe #2):** Maren grasps that the messages aren't addressed to humanity at all — they are a witness statement for the archive. Humanity is the subject, not the audience. Her grief curdles into the need to
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human short-fiction writer given this prompt would probably *not* start by engineering a premise. They'd start from one image or feeling they couldn't shake — the glyph school of fish, the sound-difference between a living reef and a dead one, the eerie grammar of "then *they* spoke" — and write toward it without knowing what it meant. Their plan, if they had one, would be a list of scenes and questions, not a closed system. The story would mutate in the drafting. Some thread would be introduced on page two because it felt right and never pay off, and the story would be *better* for it, because that's what the texture of the real feels like. A human would also draw on idiosyncratic, specific knowledge: a real harbor they know, the smell of a fish kill, the actual bureaucratic texture of a marine station, what a stranding response actually looks like (gulls, skip loaders, arguments about who pays for disposal). Their fjord would be *their* fjord. Mine is "a Norwegian fjord" chosen because it photographs well. A human would probably also be less certain about their own theme. My intention states the theme cleanly enough to print on a poster. Real stories often *can't* state their theme — the meaning leaks out sideways, and the writer is a little afraid of it. And a human might simply refuse to explain the mechanism at all: fish died, then they spoke, and nobody ever finds out why, and the story is about living next to that. My instinct to make arrival/dying/speech "a single event" is satisfying and elegant, and elegance is not obviously what this prompt wants. # Telltale LLM Approaches, and How They'd Fail This Piece Specifically **Neatness and bow-tying.** LLM fiction closes loops compulsively — every setup pays off, every theme is restated, even "ambiguous" endings resolve the *emotional* question. This prompt is fundamentally an open wound (dying → speech → ???). A bow converts dread into satisfaction. **Thesis-statement storytelling.** LLMs structure stories like arguments: a claim, supporting evidence, scheduled recontextualizations, a conclusion. The reader of such a story always knows what they're supposed to feel and when. First contact deserves epistemological mess — characters who are wrong, who disagree, who never find out. **Safety and moral cleanliness.** LLM protagonists have noble wounds and commit costless virtuous acts. This piece is *about* complicity — the ocean is dying because of us — so a morally clean protagonist actively betrays the material. **Purple competence.** Uniformly polished, metaphor-dense prose with rhythmic habits (tricolons, "not X but Y," em-dash appositions). For a story about mass death, polish is a moral problem: it aestheticizes the horror. Rot should not be beautiful on schedule. **Prestige pastiche.** Epigraphs, day-count chapter titles, retrospective narration, Latin species names as liturgy, an old fisherman, a named whale — these are *signifiers* of literary SF assembled into a mood board. It risks reading as "Arrival fan fiction with Vandermeer seasoning" rather than a thing with its own nervous system. **No humor, no banality.** LLM literary fiction is almost never funny or boring, and human grief is full of both. A harbor town in a die-off would have dark jokes, betting pools, a conspiracy guy. Total solemnity is less human *and* less devastating — contrast is what makes grief land. **Callback saturation.** When everything returns, nothing is allowed to just *exist*. Human stories are full of one-off details that mean nothing and thereby mean everything. **Pre-composed effects.** Planning the final line's "chord," the epiphany schedule, the register ("elegy without absolution") produces fiction that feels *placed* rather than *found*. The reader can tell when an ending was waiting for them. # Deep Critique of the Intention & Plan **The premise is over-solved — and it has a quiet moral contradiction.** I built a complete, elegant mechanism: witnesses, translation-through-tissue, archive. Two problems. First, full explanation converts the prompt's eeriness into a puzzle with an answer key; the dread drains out once the reader understands the rules. Second, and worse: the premise says the ocean was *already dying* and the structures came because of it — but the plan's escalation makes the structures the proximate cause of the visible die-off, and the ending has the dying *stop* once the testimony completes. The story thereby relocates blame from us to them, then resolves it. I labeled the ending "elegy without absolution," but the mechanics deliver absolution: Maren acts, the dying stops, the archive "ends in song." Everyone is forgiven, including the reader. That is the safety instinct quietly rewriting the premise's moral core. **The central dilemma got smuggled out of the story.** In the brainstorm I identified the strongest dramatic engine — *every answer costs lives; do we keep listening?* — and flagged the risk of it becoming a gimmick. Notice what happened next: the plan removed the dilemma entirely. Maren's climactic act (playing a recording) is costless, harmless, and pure. The question "what would you pay to hear the ocean's last words?" — the actual engine of horror in this premise — never gets asked onstage. That's the drift toward safety happening in real time across the planning documents. **Maren's wound is flattering.** "Cassandra archivist" is noble suffering: she was right, nobody listened. Real wounds are shaming. A human version would have the wound be partly her fault — she gave up, or she once exaggerated a dataset and was caught, or she's secretly *thrilled* that the arrival vindicates her life's work and hates herself for the thrill. The plan gestures at "irony" from the outside; it never inhabits shame from the inside. Her vindication should be *poisoned*, not poetic. **The supporting cast are described as functions.** I wrote: Jun "does the signal work," Einar is "the economic wound with one face," Kveld is "a minimal thread that pays off at the climax." That's plan-first storytelling — no character has an inner life capable of *resisting* the plot. Nobody in this story ever hijacks it. And Kveld the named whale surfacing to join the light-song is the bowiest bow in the plan — the "and then the dog came home" beat. A human might kill Kveld. Or never bring Kveld back and let the absence be the answer. Or — the dark version — make Kveld the *medium* of the reply, and let Maren choose to allow it. **The deaths are too beautiful.** Every death in this story is calligraphy: glyph schools, choral strandings, bioluminescent script. If all death is art, death becomes decorative — the story aestheticizes the very thing it mourns. The real texture of a fish kill is stink, flies, tonnage, and a council meeting about disposal costs. The plan contains zero ugly deaths, zero boring deaths, zero funny moments, zero weather that isn't symbolic. The world is a curated stage. **Triple-frame apparatus.** Epigraphs + day-count titles + retrospective first person = three formal devices, each chosen because it was "promising" in the brainstorm. LLMs accumulate devices; humans select and kill. The epigraph arc is particularly suspect: it ventriloquizes the alien into grammatically satisfying fragments that assemble into a single eloquent statement. That's me being eloquent in a costume. A real translation of a dying ocean would be full of garbage — corrupted segments, repetitions, mistranslations, arguments among translators. "It repeats the same count for six days and nobody knows why" is far more haunting than "a litany of memory." **Scheduled epiphanies.** Chapters 2, 3, 4, and 5 all end on reveals or reframes. That's streaming-TV rhythm, and at 1000 words per chapter it will produce a detectable formula: ritual opening → event → realization. Realizations in life arrive mid-scene, in subordinate clauses, at the wrong time. **The hedge.** My note that the testimony never addresses Maren personally, and that "the narration will name that temptation rather than indulge it," is an LLM tell — having the theme both ways by announcing restraint. A human would just let her feel it and trust the reader. **The pre-composed ending.** Planning for the final line to "land as a variation on the prompt's chord" is peak placed-not-earned. So is "the reply: life-as-medium answering death-as-medium" — a conceptual rhyme so perfect it could only come from a plan. Chekhov's archive. The reader will feel the click of the box closing. # Brainstorm: Ways to Make It More Human 1. **Restore the honest blame.** After the reply, the structures go dark — and the fish keep dying at the old background rate, because the structures were never the main cause. *We* were. The testimony ends mid-sentence. The ocean goes on dying, now unwitnessed. That ending actually earns "elegy without absolution." 2. **Give the climax a real cost.** Options: the reply requires a living medium and Maren lets the whales die; the archive is destroyed by being played (the only copy is spent — she gives up the thing she *is*); she hesitates and someone else acts; her sabotage of the strike has a name and a court date attached. 3. **Poison the vindication.** Let her catch herself being *glad* — the world finally has to read her archive. Or her Cassandra status is partly self-inflicted (a massaged dataset, years of not trying), so "her archive is the answer" becomes a question: *is she the right witness?* 4. **Let a character resist the plot.** Einar doesn't want the messages decoded — the fish were worth more to him as fish. Or Jun leaks the translation, the world's reaction is their fault, and they're not sorry. One small, human betrayal. 5. **Ugly at least one death completely.** The harbor glyph dies and by noon it's a disposal problem. A skip loader erases half the message before Jun can image it — and the testimony permanently has a hole in it. The ocean's last words contain a skip-loader-shaped gap nobody will ever fill. That missing word could haunt the ending more than any litany. 6. **Let the testimony speak badly.** Repetitions, corrupted fragments, one clearly-wrong translation that the world falls in love with anyway. Human grief is full of banal last words; the ocean's might be a number or a place-name. 7. **Kill or withhold Kveld.** Absence as the answer, or Kveld as the *price* of the reply. 8. **Drop one frame.** Keep at most two of: epigraphs, day-counts, retrospective voice. Better: make the epigraphs a *collage* — testimony fragments colliding with bureaucratic debris (an incident report, a 2009 grant rejection, a skip-loader invoice). That's texture, not prophecy. 9. **De-schedule the reveals.** One chapter should end flat — someone eating dinner. Bury the biggest reframe mid-paragraph. 10. **One detail that pays nothing off.** The kid selling structure-shaped keyrings. The broken coffee machine. The ferry timetable. The world exists. 11. **One running joke.** The town betting pool on what the structure is. The conspiracy caller who's accidentally right about exactly one thing. 12. **Retire the planned final line.** Don't compose the chord in advance. Or
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Working title: "The Counting."** **The premise (softened, held open).** The structures came toward the sound of a dying ocean. The die-off and the speech are one event — the testimony is written in living tissue, and the writing kills the ink. The fish speak because the voice is the ocean's own. **But — and this is the key revision — nobody ever confirms any of this.** "Witness/archive" is Jun's theory. The commission has another. A conspiracy caller on the radio has a third ("it's not talking to us, it's talking about us"), mocked by everyone, and he happens to be the one who's right — the reframe available to the whole town from day one and ignored because of who said it. The mechanism stays a contested guess. The eeriness survives because there is no answer key. **The moral core (locked, and protected from my own instinct to soften it).** The structures are *not* the main cause of the dying. We are. When the testimony ends, the spectacular die-off events stop and the papers declare victory — and the fish keep dying at the old background rate, now unwitnessed. The plan must never let the climax fix anything. The ending is elegy *without* absolution, and this time the mechanics actually deliver it: nothing is saved, no one is forgiven, including the protagonist. **The protagonist.** Dr. Maren Vold, whale-fall ecologist, marine station at the head of a Norwegian fjord, structure at the fjord's mouth. Her wound is poisoned, not noble: she documented the decline for forty years and **hoarded** the evidence instead of fighting to publish it — the 2009 grant rejection pinned above her desk is kept because it is *partly fair*. Her archive exists because of the dying; her career was built on it. Worst: when the world finally comes begging for her recordings, she catches herself *glad*, and hates it. One buried line of self-recognition: the structures only keep records; so did she. Her arc is not vindication — it's an archivist learning the difference between keeping testimony and testifying, at a price she chooses and is not absolved of. **The climax and its cost (locked).** With a naval "acoustic suppression" fleet en route to jam the testimony into silence, and the messages visibly shortening as the voice spends the last of the fish, Maren plays her loudest artifact — a 1987 reef, the sound of a *living* ocean — into the fjord. Not a reply to a question (too neat); a donation to the record, if a record is what's being kept. Then Kveld, the station's resident humpback, silent since the arrival, answers the speaker — and his song is *taken*, pulled into the testimony register, his body failing as he comes in toward the sound. Maren has her hand on the cutoff. She holds the tape to the end. Kveld strands below the station at dawn, mid-note. She chose the complete record over one living life; the narration will render the choice plainly and never editorialize it. The structure goes dark mid-phrase. The last decoded fragment is half a sentence. **Cast.** Jun (postdoc) gets agency and guilt: they leak the decoded fragments online against orders — *"it's our ocean; everyone should hear it"* — and the circus, the commission takeover, and the world's beloved mistranslation are partly their fault; they're not sorry, exactly. Einar (fisherman) gets the moral counterweight, unrecruited and unredeemed: he wants the voice *silenced*, because the fish were worth more as fish, and the story must treat him as right enough to hurt. Kveld is an absence, then a song, then a cost — never a pet. All governments exist offscreen, in radio voices and paper. **Voice and style.** Retrospective first person, plain sentences, flat at the peaks of horror ("I will not write what it sounded like. It sounded like an animal dying."). Marine diction as working texture, not liturgy — the Latin-as-psalm device is cut. Dialogue carries more weight than narration, to keep purple out. The structure is described once, obliquely, with slightly wrong adjectives, and never fully again. **Humor and banality are mandatory**: the quay-kiosk betting pool on what the structure will do (it never does anything; nobody wins), the broken coffee machine, the conspiracy caller, the skip-loader disposal argument. At least one death is rendered as pure ugliness — stink, flies, tonnage, an invoice. **Frames — two, kept deliberately imperfect.** Retrospective narration + irregular chapter epigraphs as **collage**: testimony fragments colliding with bureaucratic debris (the grant rejection, an incident report, the skip-loader invoice), never forming a clean arc. Day-count titles are dropped; chapters get flat single-word titles. The testimony itself must **speak badly**: it repeats one count for six days, the world's favorite translation is a comforting error that the correction never overtakes, and the harbor message is half-erased by a loader before it can be imaged — a permanent hole in the ocean's last words, which haunts the ending more than any litany could. **Reveals are de-scheduled.** No chapter may end on a twist more than once. The central reframe (the dying *is* the speaking) lands buried mid-paragraph while someone does dishes. At least one chapter ends on dinner and a joke that's actually funny. One detail pays off nothing. The
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Dr. Maren Vold Sixty-one. Born in Bergen; her father adjusted marine insurance claims, her mother taught school — she grew up around boats in the working, unglamorous way, none of it romantic at the time. In 1993, on a survey trawl, she helped haul up a net that had dragged across a sunken whale carcass, and what came up — the crabs, the hagfish, the whole glistening congregation feeding on one death — set the direction of her life. Whale falls. What death feeds. She has always found this more comforting than morbid, which tells you most of what you need to know about her. **The wound, made specific:** In 2009 she applied for funding to digitize and publish her archive — twenty-two years of hydrophone tapes, trawl logs, species counts — as a public acoustic baseline. The rejection letter cited "anecdotal methodology," "absence of a testable hypothesis," and, devastatingly, "the applicant's evident emotional investment in the material." The letter is pinned above her desk. People assume it's fuel. The truth is worse: the man who wrote it was *partly right*, and instead of revising, resubmitting, or making noise, she withdrew into the purity of the private archive. She kept recording — for herself, impeccably, unread. Her bitterness at being ignored sits on top of a shame she rarely touches: she never actually made them listen. She preferred being right in a drawer to being messy in public. And when the commission arrives and the world finally begs for her tapes, she catches the gladness rising in herself — vindication, at last, at the fjord's expense — and has to stand there feeling it. The structures only keep records. So did she. **Idiosyncrasies:** She names her hydrophones — the oldest, deployed 1987, is "the Aunt," because it's old, nosy, and hears everything. She writes field notes in pencil because "ink is for people who are certain." She greets the dead on the tide line ("Morning. You're late."), then is annoyed at herself for anthropomorphizing, then does it again the next day. She corrects species IDs reflexively, mid-crisis, and hates that about herself. She defends the station's broken coffee machine out of sheer loyalty. She has not been able to play the 1987 tape in years — the fjord's own reef, this same water, before the farms, deafening with life. It plays in her head uninvited. She can name every species audible in it the way other people know the words to a wedding song. **Flaws:** Pride dressed as humility. Cowardice about conflict — she let a letter win for thirteen years. A tendency to process other people's grief as data, offering tea and facts where comfort should go. And the fatal one: she can rationalize almost anything with the word *record*. **Physicality:** Tall, broad-shouldered, stooped at the shoulders from decades of leaning over railings. Grey braid whose end she chews when thinking, a habit she hides from Jun. Rough hands, short nails, a red wool hat from October to May, a limp from a winch cable in 1998 that forecasts weather. She speaks slowly, in a low register, with pauses long enough that people think she's finished. She has learned to let them. **Motives and allegiances:** On the surface — the work, the baseline, the truth. Underneath, tangled together: to matter, and to be forgiven by the ocean, and she can no longer tell them apart. Her truest allegiance is to the dead, and that is the story's problem, because the climax will ask her to weigh the record against one living life, and her hierarchy is already loaded. **Relationships:** Jun — hired three years ago off a strong letter and a weak CV; she loves them and shows it by assigning them the good shifts and criticizing their tea. Einar — thirty years of nodding on the quay under an unspoken treaty: he doesn't mock her counts, she doesn't lecture him about quotas. The story breaks the treaty. Kveld — eleven years of his dusk song on her tapes; the largest single folder in the archive; she has never once examined what that means. **Voice:** - "Everyone remembers where they were when the fish started speaking. I was on the beach, counting them. This is not as poetic as it sounds. It is what I did every morning." - "Jun asked me if I believed the ocean was saying goodbye. I said I didn't believe in goodbyes, I believed in baselines. Jun said, 'Maren.' Just that. It is the worst thing anyone has ever called me." - "I know the sound of a healthy reef the way you know your mother's voice. That is not a metaphor I permit myself. I am permitting it once, and then we are done with it." # Jun Aas Twenty-seven, from inland — Lillehammer, a town with more ski jumps than shoreline. Physics undergrad, drifted into signal processing, took the marine acoustics postdoc because it was funded and the fjord was beautiful in the photos. They came to the ocean *late and by choice*, which is exactly what makes them useful: they hear the testimony as signal first, grief second. Where Maren hears a dying voice, Jun hears structure, register, repetition — and the story can live in that gap. Jun is the one who runs the correlation, finds the register, and does the actual decoding work — and then watches the commission arrive with lanyards and NDAs and absorb it. **The leak, made honest:** Jun leaked the decoded fragments partly out of principle and partly out of vanity and loneliness, and the order matters. The principle — *"it's our ocean; everyone should hear it"* — is real but arrived afterward, as justification. What came first was the sting of erasure: their work, their fjord, absorbed by men who'd never been on the water. The leak is the only fully honest thing anyone does in the story, and it also causes the circus: the pilgrim boats jamming the fjord mouth, the comforting mistranslation going viral worldwide, the commission locking Maren out of her own archive. Jun knows this. Their way of knowing it is to sleep at the station, work twice as hard, and be brittlely funny. They are not sorry, exactly. Both things stay true; they contain multitudes and will say so flippantly so nobody asks further. **Idiosyncrasies:** Instant noodles daily, doctored with elaborate precision. Annotates code with profanity. Named Kveld (his song always came at dusk), which Maren resented and lost. Wears headphones around their neck as a do-not-disturb sign. Says "sorry" when they mean "listen to me." The only station member in the quay betting pool — bets with data-informed confidence, loses anyway. Calls the structure "the neighbor" ("Has the neighbor done anything?" "The neighbor breathed."), a running gag that does double duty: it keeps the structure's awe domestic and strange. **Physicality:** Small and quick, twenty-seven going on nineteen inside a borrowed oversized station jacket, hood up in all weather. Gestures with whole arms when a signal does something interesting. Bounces a knee. Falls asleep sitting up mid-analysis, chin on chest, screen glowing. Eats standing up. Goes completely, unnaturally still when frightened — the opposite of their usual motion, and the reader should learn to read it. **Motives and allegiances:** To matter; to be seen doing the work; to belong to an ocean community they chose rather than inherited. Their allegiance is to Maren (they'd deny it hotly) and, quietly, to the testimony itself — Jun is the one person who loves the signal the way Maren loves the ocean, and neither of them ever says so. **Voice:** - "Okay, so either the fish are dying at random or the fish are dying in cursive, and I really needed it to be random, so obviously it's cursive." - "I leaked it because the whole world was about to get an opinion about our water from a man who's never stood on it. Also because I'm an idiot. Both things. I contain multitudes. Shut up." - "The neighbor's not a machine. Machines do things. It's a *habit*." # Einar Berg Sixty-four. Third generation on the same fjord. His grandfather fished herring when the schools ran so thick he claimed you could set a bucket on them and stand on the bucket. Einar remembers the boom, the crash, the quotas, and the arrival of the salmon farms, which he despises with the completeness of a man who watched them take his water — the escapees, the lice, the taste of it. His boat, the *Veslemøy*, named for his mother, is older than Maren's archive and held together by his hands and his nephew Leif's welding. His wife died six years ago — not a wound the story mines, just weather in him. His daughter is in Oslo doing something with apps. Leif crews with him and plans to leave. He knows. He says nothing. **His position, which the story must honor:** He wants the voice silenced. Not from denial — he has watched the catch shrink for thirty years and never once believed that writing it down would help; that's the core of his long, low-grade quarrel with Maren. His argument: the fish were his, are his, and being spent as somebody's alphabet is one more theft in a life of thefts. *Everything that talks, takes.* It's a genuinely strong counter-reading of the testimony — communication as extraction — and nobody in the story successfully refutes it, including the narration. He can also smell Maren's gladness before she admits it to herself, and his contempt for it is one of the things that forces her to look at it. **His arc — deliberately not a conversion:** When the harbor glyph kills the herring school his season depended on, he doesn't rage at the heavens. He sells the dead fish to the rendering plant, because that's what you do with dead fish, and the invoice — a bill for God's handwriting — becomes one of the story's ugliest epigraphs. He uses the payout to clear the debt on the *Veslemøy*. Later he takes Maren out to the structure because the fjord mouth is jammed with pilgrim boats and he's the only one who knows the water, and she pays him — and maybe, though he'd never say it, he wants to be present when it finishes, the way you'd sit with a dying neighbor you never liked. His last beat: after Kveld strands, he arrives at dawn with the winch and the loader, unasked, and does the work gently, and says the only prayer in the story: *"There, now. Enough."* For the whale, for Maren, or for the ocean is never resolved. **Idiosyncrasies:** Addresses people by job or boat ("Professor," neutrally — neither mockery nor respect). Chews toothpicks since quitting smoking. Keeps meticulous tide notebooks in a shorthand only Leif can read — his own private archive, a quiet rhyme with Maren's that the text never points at. Feeds the harbor gulls while maintaining he hates them. Calls the structure "the tenant": *the tenant pays no rent.* **Physicality:** Short, barrel-chested, a face creased like a chart from squinting. Pale blue eyes that water in wind, so he permanently looks slightly moved and isn't. Old net scars on his hands. Moves slowly until he's on the boat, where he's quick. Smells of diesel and lanolin. Laughs once per scene at most, a single bark. **Voice:** - "You count the dead. I used to count the living. We are both out of work, Professor." - "Everyone wants to know what it's saying. Nobody asks who it's saying it *with*." - "My grandfather stood on the herring. Stood on them. Now the herring spell. You tell me which world is stranger." # Kveld (K-7) A presence, not a character — and the story should hold that line. A humpback resident in the fjord eleven years, catalogued by fluke pattern as K-7, named by the interns over Maren's resistance. His dusk song is the largest folder in her archive. He has been silent since the arrival — forty days of absence that functions as the story's held breath. His body condition declines over the month; whether the testimony is claiming his food or claiming *him* is never established. He is the only one in the story who answers the 1987 tape, and the question that outlives him — did he choose to answer, or was the song drawn out of him? — is the one Maren will carry. He strands below the station at dawn, mid-note. Maren performs the necropsy herself; it replaces the counting as her ritual. One body, weighed properly, at last. # Texture Cast (brief) **Odd** — a nightly caller to the local radio, the town joke, whose theories include that the moon is a ship. He is also the first person to say, on air, "It's not talking to us. It's talking *about* us." Dismissed by everyone, including the reader, until he turns out to be the only one who was right. He never learns this and wouldn't care. **The kiosk kid** — seventeen, runs the quay betting pool on what the structure will do (breathe again, leave, speak Norwegian), keeps the ledger with terrifying seriousness, sells lighters and terrible waffles to the pilgrims. Pays off nothing. Exists because the world does. **Dr. Halvorsen** — the commission lead. Never onstage. A voice on a phone call, a lanyard at the edge of a scene, a signature on the lockout notice. She is not a villain; she is a procedure.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One: Counting *One hundred and sixty. One hundred and sixty. One hundred and sixty. One hundred and s—* *"...the applicant's evident emotional investment in the material... anecdotal methodology... we are unable to recommend funding."* — *from a funding decision, 2009* --- Everyone remembers where they were when the fish started speaking. I was on the beach, counting them. This is not as poetic as it sounds. It is what I did every morning, and had done every morning for thirty years, which tells you something about me that I have never managed to fix. The first dead fish that day was a whiting, about two hundred meters below the station, eye already taken by a gull. I wrote it in the notebook. I write everything in pencil, because ink is for people who are certain. Then, because I am apparently the kind of person I am, I said, "Morning. You're late." I was immediately annoyed with myself. I do this every day. You would think habit would wear the annoyance off. By the old boathouse I had two hundred and eleven. Every species accounted for — whiting, sprat, one dogfish I will think about for years. The ones that have been dead longest shine most; there's a dullness that comes over them in the first hours, and then, if the flies are slow, a kind of polish. It was October, so the smell was low. In summer you could navigate by it. At the mouth of the fjord stood the structure. It had been there a month. It is tall. That is the only true sentence I have about it. Tall, and the color of deep water in bad weather, with no edges you would call edges. The first week, the whole town stood on the quay and watched it. The second week, we watched it while doing other things. By the fourth week it had ceased to be weather. A month is long enough to get used to anything. There was a war somewhere that month. The markets fell. The fish kept dying at the usual background rate, plus. Einar was on the quay with his hands in his boiler suit, looking at the empty hold of the *Veslemøy* the way you look at a bad tooth. "Morning, Professor." "Einar." "Count?" "Two hundred eleven." He made the sound he makes, which is not a word in any of the three languages he knows, and moved his toothpick from one side of his mouth to the other. We have a treaty, Einar and I. Thirty years old. He does not mock my counts, and I do not lecture him about quotas. It has held longer than most marriages on this coast. He nodded at the fjord's mouth. "The tenant pays no rent." "The tenant does nothing," I said. "The tenant does nothing," he agreed, with satisfaction. Einar had decided, publicly and at length, not to be interested in the structure. He is a man of his word. The kid at the kiosk keeps a ledger of bets on what it will do. Leave: three to one. Speak: five to one. Speak Norwegian: forty to one. Nobody has put money on *nothing*, which is all it has done so far, which is why a seventeen-year-old was getting rich off the end of the world. Up at the station, the coffee machine was dying. It had been dying since March. Jun had drawn a line on the reservoir at the level where the pump gives up, and labeled it. "When the machine dies," Jun said, without looking up, "I'll know." "Know what?" "That it's the end." A shrug. "Things should announce themselves." Jun was twenty-seven and slept at the desk more often than was decent, headphones around their neck like a sign. I found them that morning chin on chest, in front of a screen of spectrograms gone flat. I turned the volume down on the Aunt. The Aunt is my oldest hydrophone, deployed in 1987, anchored in the deep water off the point. I call her the Aunt because she is old and nosy and hears everything. For thirty-nine days she had heard nothing. I had written the same sentence in the log thirty-nine times, in pencil, and stopped expecting to erase it. The silence was not total. Total would have been easier. There was the boat traffic, the far-off grumble of the ferry, a shrimp now and then, doing whatever it is shrimps do that sounds like static. What was missing was everything else. The cod grunting under the winter ice. The chatter of the herring schools. And at dusk, for eleven years, a humpback whale named Kveld, whose song came in off the deep water like someone working through a long thought. The interns named him. I forbade it, and lost. He had not sung in forty days. The largest folder in my archive is his. I don't think about that. Jun says I am a dead-augur. I tell them the Romans would have paid well for someone who could read the future in washed-up fish, and Jun tells me the Romans are dead, and I say: everyone is, eventually. This passes for humor at the station. The coffee machine is funnier than either of us, and it's dying. Counting is not nothing. That is the thing I would say if anyone asked, and no one has asked in years. Counting is the smallest form of respect. You cannot save what you will not look at, and looking, done properly, every morning, in pencil, becomes a kind of holding. The dead are not data. But data is what the dead leave us, and I am good at data. Above my desk hangs a letter from 2009, pinned where I can see it. People assume it is fuel. That evening I spread the tally sheets on the big table, because the big table was there and the sleep wasn't. A month of numbers. I logged positions because logging positions is what I do, and when I had enough of them, I did what I always do, which is plot them on the chart of the coast. The kills sat in curves. I sharpened my pencil. It was already sharp. Current would scatter a die-off. Temperature would blur one. These were not scattered and they were not blurred. They were drawn. Long arcs off the point. Parallel strokes in the bay. Along the headland, a curve inside a curve, with a spacing so regular I checked my own handwriting to make sure I hadn't imposed it. Geometry is not a thing the sea does. I wrote it in the log anyway, in pencil, so that I could erase it in the morning. I did not erase it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished opening chapter that demonstrates sophisticated control of voice, tone, and structure. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** Maren's retrospective first-person voice is immediately distinctive and consistent with her profile. The opening lines establish her character through action rather than declaration: she counts dead fish every morning, she writes in pencil, she greets the dead and is annoyed at herself for it. The self-awareness is calibrated precisely — she knows her own habits, finds them both necessary and slightly absurd, and doesn't romanticize them. "This is not as poetic as it sounds. It is what I did every morning, and had done every morning for thirty years, which tells you something about me that I have never managed to fix." This is excellent characterization through voice. The wound is present but not overexplained. The 2009 letter is mentioned twice — once in the epigraph, once at the end — and both times it's handled with restraint. "People assume it is fuel." That's all. The reader is trusted to understand the implication. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and effective. The exchange with Einar ("The tenant pays no rent" / "The tenant does nothing") is economical and characterizes both speakers. Jun's coffee machine exchange is genuinely funny and thematically resonant without being heavy-handed. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome anywhere. **Structure and Pacing** The chapter does exactly what a first chapter should: it establishes the world, the protagonist, the central mystery, and the emotional stakes without resolving any of them. The geometry revelation at the end — "Geometry is not a thing the sea does" — is earned rather than forced. The chapter builds to it naturally through the counting ritual, and the final image (writing it in pencil, not erasing it) is a perfect closing beat that encapsulates Maren's character. **Prose Quality** The prose is largely excellent. Sentences are short and declarative at the peaks of strangeness, which is exactly right. "It is tall. That is the only true sentence I have about it." This is the plan's instruction to describe the structure obliquely with slightly wrong adjectives, executed well. The humor lands: the kiosk kid's betting pool, the coffee machine dying since March, Jun's "things should announce themselves." There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "The dead are not data. But data is what the dead leave us, and I am good at data" — this is a little too neat, a little too aphoristic, though it's not egregious. "A month is long enough to get used to anything" is a fine observation but slightly generic. The line about the fish shining — "there's a dullness that comes over them in the first hours, and then, if the flies are slow, a kind of polish" — is genuinely good, specific and strange without being purple. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows the plan closely. The flat single-word title is there. The humor and banality are present (kiosk kid, coffee machine). The structure is described obliquely. The retrospective first person is established. The geometry discovery lands mid-paragraph rather than as a chapter-ending twist. The epigraph collage (testimony fragment + bureaucratic debris) is executed. Jun's character is introduced with appropriate lightness. Einar appears briefly but distinctly. **Weaknesses** The chapter is almost too controlled — there's a slight sense of a writer demonstrating competence rather than taking risks. The "dead-augur" exchange between Maren and Jun is the weakest moment, feeling slightly constructed. The observation about Kveld's folder being the largest in the archive, followed by "I don't think about that," is a little too telegraphed as significant — the plan calls for this to be buried, and it's not quite buried enough. The epigraph structure is interesting but the testimony fragment ("One hundred and sixty. One hundred and sixty...") doesn't fully land yet — its significance isn't clear enough to create the right kind of unease, though this may be intentional for a first chapter. Overall, this is a very strong opening chapter that executes its plan with skill, maintains tonal consistency, and establishes a distinctive voice without overreaching. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two: Pencil *"It's not talking to us. It's talking about us. Also the moon is a ship."* — Odd, caller, *Coastline Tonight*, Day 36 --- The gulls told us first. They rose off the shore below the point all at once, the way they do when something arrives in the water that is food or will be, and Jun looked up from the noodles and said, "What's got them," and I was already reaching for my jacket. Down at the waterline we found it and stopped. Eleven pilot whales in the shallows. Not rolling. Not thrashing. Eleven animals holding their heads above the water, steady as moored boats, and vocalizing — one note, in unison, that went down the scale and came back up it and sat on a pitch that made my molars ache. It was not song. I have forty years of song in my head and I can tell you, this was not it. It was too regular. It had edges. Song does not have edges. I will not write what it sounded like. It sounded like an animal dying. That is the only true sentence I have. Einar was already there. Of course he was. He stood at the water's edge with the toothpick gone still in his mouth, watching, the way a man watches weather that has decided to include him. By then the town was coming down the beach — the kiosk kid, first in, already arguing with somebody on a phone, as if the stranding were a market he'd cornered; a woman from the ferry line; two men I didn't know, which meant they'd driven in from somewhere. A boy was wading out to the smallest whale with a bucket, and Einar caught him by the collar and pulled him back without a word, and the boy looked at Einar's face and stopped struggling. One of the new men had a radio on his hip that murmured. I caught a thread of a voice I half-knew — Odd, the night caller, doing his show for the whole town on some passerby's hip. He was saying it wasn't talking to us, it was talking about us. Then something about the moon being a ship. The man with the radio turned it down, embarrassed, as if he were related to it. The smallest whale died first, with the boy's hand still hovering near it, not touching, at Einar's instruction. The others went in an order I couldn't make sense of until later. The note held until the last of them, and then it didn't. The gulls came down while the bodies were still rocking in the wash. Nobody stopped them. We got one recording. Jun had the hydrophone out in twelve minutes, which is fast for a person who'd been woken by whales. It was enough. Back at the station the coffee machine had taken itself off somewhere. Jun did the thing they do, which is to work while eating, noodles going cold, eyes on the screen, and I made tea nobody drank, and the spectrogram came up and neither of us said anything for a long time. The note the whales made had the same shape as the kill curves. The same arcs. The same spacing, drawn in frequency instead of on the chart, but the same hand, if a hand is what I'm going to call it. Jun ran the month's tally data against the month's acoustic log, and the correlation came up clean, and Jun said "okay" in a voice I hadn't heard from them before, and then they didn't say anything else for a while. I stood behind their chair and looked at the two lines sitting on top of each other, and understood that I had been reading it wrong for a month. All of us had. I had been reading the whales as a symptom and the glyphs as geometry, and the truth, whatever it was, was wearing both. Later, much later, when Jun had gone to sleep in the chair and the spectrogram was still on the screen, I took down the 2009 letter from above my desk and read it, which I do sometimes. *The applicant's evident emotional investment in the material.* I put it back. I went to the window. The structure stood at the mouth of the fjord doing nothing, the color of deep water, with the town lights on the hills behind it and the dead below it. It had been there a month. It had been talking for a month. We had been calling it a die-off. The dying was the speaking. The dying was the speaking. I stood there letting it be true, and it was the worst thing I have ever let be true, and I have let some things be true. From the chair, without opening their eyes, Jun said, "It's a register." "What?" "The repeated bit. The long count. I ran it back. It's the same every time, same spacing." Jun sat up, blanket around their shoulders, hair sideways. "It says it before anything else. Registers are how you open a—" They stopped. "Open a what." Jun looked at the screen, and then at me, and then at the screen. "A record," they said. I thought about my tally book. Thirty years of opening every page the same way: date, position, count. I thought about what it would mean to be the kind of thing that counts first and speaks second, and then I stopped thinking about it, because Jun was crying, silently, the way you do when you're twenty-seven and you haven't decided yet whether you're allowed to, and I did the thing I always do, which was to put the kettle on and say something about data. "Tea," I said. "And then we log it." Jun laughed, once, wetly, which was the right answer. We logged it. We were very precise. Neither of us said the word *message* until morning, and when we finally did, we said it quietly, the way you say a name in a house where someone has died. I did not sleep. I sat with the log book open and my pencil in my hand, and I did not write the sentence, because I did not yet have the sentence. I had the shape of the sentence. It was drawn in fish, and in whale voices, and in my own handwriting, and it said the same thing in all three.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of speculative literary fiction. Let me assess it carefully across the requested dimensions. **Craft and Voice** The retrospective first-person voice is controlled and distinctive. The flat affect at peaks of horror is executed precisely as planned: "I will not write what it sounded like. It sounded like an animal dying. That is the only true sentence I have." This is not a dodge — it's a technique that earns its restraint. The voice never becomes self-congratulatory about its own plainness, which is the trap this style usually falls into. The prose is largely clean and purposeful. Marine diction functions as working texture rather than liturgy. The sentence "Song does not have edges" is genuinely good — it earns its compression. The observation about the correlation ("the same hand, if a hand is what I'm going to call it") is appropriately hedged and strange. **Character Consistency** Maren is rendered faithfully to her profile. The 2009 letter comes down from the wall at exactly the right moment — not as a dramatic gesture but as a private ritual. Her response to Jun's tears (kettle, data, deflection) is precisely the flaw described: "offering tea and facts where comfort should go." Jun's voice is accurate — the "okay" in an unfamiliar register, the sideways hair, the crying without permission. Einar's brief appearance is efficient: the toothpick going still, catching the boy by the collar without a word. These are people, not sketches. **The Central Reveal** The plan specified that the central reframe — "the dying is the speaking" — should land "buried mid-paragraph while someone does dishes." The chapter delivers this almost exactly: it arrives while Jun is eating noodles and the spectrogram is on screen, rendered in a flat declarative repetition ("The dying was the speaking. The dying was the speaking.") that is more effective than any dramatic staging would have been. The repetition is earned — it mimics the way a terrible truth has to be said twice before it becomes real. **Weaknesses** The ending runs slightly long. "It said the same thing in all three" is a fine closing line, but the paragraph before it — "I had the shape of the sentence. It was drawn in fish, and in whale voices, and in my own handwriting" — edges toward the kind of poetic summary that the plan explicitly warned against. It's not quite purple, but it's the chapter's most self-conscious moment, where the narration becomes aware of its own elegance. The plan called for at least one chapter to end on "dinner and a joke that's actually funny" — this chapter doesn't do that, but it does end on "Tea. And then we log it." followed by a wet laugh, which is a lighter touch before the final meditation. That's close enough to the spirit. The Odd epigraph is well-chosen and does exactly what the plan intended — the conspiracy caller's correct insight dismissed by everyone. The moon-is-a-ship line prevents it from being too clean. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is correct for this story. "It's a register" / "Open a what" / "A record" — this exchange is genuinely good. It does the work of revelation without announcing itself. Jun's "okay" in an unfamiliar voice is a strong piece of characterization through minimal means. **Plot and Coherence** The chapter advances the plot efficiently: the pilot whale stranding, the acoustic correlation, the realization that the dying and the speaking are one event. The pacing is controlled. The commission hasn't arrived yet; the leak hasn't happened; the chapter is doing exactly what a second chapter should do — deepening the mystery while delivering one genuine revelation. **Forced Poetry / Purple Prose** There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly: "the way a man watches weather that has decided to include him" is good but self-aware. "I have let some things be true" is the chapter's most writerly line and it mostly earns it. The final paragraph is the weakest — "drawn in fish, and in whale voices, and in my own handwriting" is the kind of triple construction that can tip into self-satisfaction. It doesn't quite tip, but it leans. **Overall** This is a chapter that executes its plan with unusual fidelity and genuine craft. The voice is consistent, the characters are alive, the central revelation is handled with appropriate restraint, and the humor (the kiosk kid, the radio, the wet laugh) is present without undermining the weight. The chapter's main limitation is a slight tendency toward self-conscious elegance in its final beats, but this is a minor flaw in an otherwise strong piece. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: Telegram *...and the light came down through us in pieces, and we [—] the pieces.* *"The Commission acknowledges with thanks your cooperation, and requests all hydroacoustic materials in your possession, raw and processed, at your earliest convenience."* --- For six days after the whales, the ocean said one thing. It said it at dusk, in the range of small bodies — krill, sprat, fry, the planktonic crowd — and it said it in the cheap seats of the spectrum, where a voice can be carried by almost nothing. One hundred and sixty. A pause. One hundred and sixty. Every dusk, the same number, and then nothing, and the Aunt recorded all of it with the patience of the old and nosy. "It's clearing its throat," Jun said. "Or counting," I said. "Why would it count and not say anything?" "Ask me another." Jun looked at me sideways, and did not ask me another, which is one of the reasons I keep them. What Jun had found, between noodles and naps, was this: the voice lived where the ink was cheap. The glyphs off the point were sprat-work — tons of them, yes, but sprat are how the ocean says anything at length. The whales had been something else. Jun played me the stranding note against the six-day count and pointed at the screen with a chopstick. "It never uses a whale when a sprat will do," Jun said. "The whales were a capital letter." Eleven pilot whales for emphasis. I wrote it in the log, in pencil, and left it there, because some sentences you do not erase. You just stop looking at them for a while. When I was young, you paid for telegrams by the word, and people became poets out of poverty. Mothers could say in nine words what a novelist couldn't manage in four hundred. I told Jun this, and Jun said, "So it's writing a telegram," and I said it had been writing telegrams for a month, and we had been reading the paper. "It's going to run out," Jun said quietly, to the screen. I knew what they meant. The fjord was not inexhaustible. Nothing is inexhaustible — that is the first lesson of the tally book. A voice that pays by the body will be silent soon enough. I did not write that down either. The Commission telephoned on the sixth day. A woman's voice, pleasant, the kind of voice that has a lanyard attached to it. She thanked me for my cooperation, which I had not yet given, and requested all my materials, raw and processed, at my earliest convenience. I gave it. I sent everything I had on the events, which was not everything I had. I have forty years on those shelves. I sent them the month. After I hung up I stood in the window for a while. The tenant stood at the mouth of the fjord, paying no rent. I had waited thirty years for someone to ask for my tapes, and now someone had asked, and I would have preferred it to be for a better reason. That is what I told myself, standing there.
