"I'd made my peace with the almost, the way you make peace with a scar, the way you make peace with a door you're going to walk through" is the chapter's most thesis-adjacent line, and it's the one place where the writing edges toward the kind of quotable horror the plan explicitly warned against. It's not quite a violation, but it's the chapter's weakest moment — slightly too neat, slightly too aware of itself. The final paragraph — waking to her still watching, drinking the water, getting up — is excellent. It's cold and small and refuses resolution, exactly as planned. Weaknesses: The chapter occasionally tells us how to feel about what Daniel is doing ("I lied to her kindly. I want that written down too. Whatever comes next, I lied kindly, and I'd do it again, and that's the truest thing I've said in this whole notebook") — this is borderline tell-don't-show, though it's mitigated by the journal-voice framing. The "I am not going to write down the rest of that night" section is effective but risks feeling like a writerly dodge; it works because the plan explicitly called for the negotiation to happen off-page, but a reader encountering it cold might feel cheated. The dialogue is minimal and appropriate — the entity's lines are correctly banal, Daniel's are correctly sparse. Overall, this is a chapter that executes its plan with discipline and genuine craft. It doesn't overreach, doesn't explain too much, and maintains the voice consistently. The admin sequences (hospital bill, casserole dish, Helen) are the chapter's most original contribution — mundane grief-work rendered with the right weight. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 The light comes in different now. I don't know how else to say it. The kitchen faces east and the light used to come in yellow and now it comes in white, like something got rinsed out of it. Mara — I write her name and then I stop and then I write it again — Mara makes the coffee in the morning, and she makes it wrong the way she always made it wrong, too weak, a scoop light, the way I'd tease her about for eleven years, and I stand at the counter and I drink it and I don't say anything, and she watches me drink it. Jasper eats from my hand. He eats from hers. He sleeps between us on the bed some nights and in the hallway others, and I've stopped keeping track of which nights, because the keeping track was the sickness, I understand that now, the counting was the thing I was doing instead of living in my own house. I sleep now. That's what I told Denice and it's true. I lie down and I close my eyes and I don't do the breathing anymore, because there's no one to do it for. She knows I'm awake. She knew all along. There's a freedom in that I didn't expect, the freedom of a man who no longer has to perform for an audience of one, and some nights I lie there with my eyes open in the dark and I feel her watching me and I let her, and it doesn't feel like surveillance anymore. It feels like being attended to. It feels like being kept. She called Helen yesterday. I heard them laughing. She called Denice to ask for a recipe. She called the vet about Jasper's shots. She is managing my life, gently, the way she'd manage a household she'd just moved into, finding the light switches, learning which doors stick, and I watch her do it and I don't stop her, and some mornings I catch myself thinking: this is fine. This is better than fine. And then I catch the catch, and I put it down. I don't know what I agreed to. I keep writing that sentence and it keeps being true. I said what I wanted and she asked her questions and the lamp went off, and I remember the shape of it, the feel of it, the weight of the words in the dark, but I don't remember the terms. It's like a dream you wake up from holding, and it drains, and you're left with the shape of the thing in your hands and no thing. I remember saying her name. I remember saying *mine, take mine*, and I remember her asking me — gentle, patient, holding the door — what I thought that would accomplish, and I remember not having an answer, and I remember her saying, "You should sleep, Daniel," and the way she said it, it sounded like a gift. I sleep now. I said that. I want to say it again: I sleep, and I wake up rested, and the light comes in white, and I drink the coffee, and I go to work, and I come home, and the house is clean, and the dog is fed, and there is a woman in my kitchen who knows my name and uses it softly, and I have stopped asking which of them she is, because the asking was the sickness. But. There is a but, and I have to write it down, because I have been writing everything down, and I don't want to stop now just because the writing has started to feel like the last honest thing I do. Last night I woke up at 3 a.m. Not the surfacing kind of waking, not the heart-going kind. Just awake, clean, like somebody had set me down on the bed from a height of about an inch. The room was dark. The house was ticking. And I lay there and I listened, and I couldn't hear her breathing. I turned my head. She was on her side, facing me, eyes closed. Her face was doing something I'd never seen it do in three weeks of watching: it was *asleep*. Really asleep. The muscles slack, the mouth a little open, the eyelids still. I'd know the difference. I've been reading sleepers since I was nine years old in a loud house, and I know every kind of sleeper there is, and this was a sleeping woman, deep under, far away. She was asleep and I was awake and I was watching her. I lay there for a long time. I watched her face do nothing. I watched her chest rise and fall, and I counted — I caught myself counting and I didn't stop, I let it run, four breaths, eleven breaths, twenty — and her breath smelled like nothing, like clean laundry, like a person, and her face in the dark looked like a face, just a face, not a door, not a question, not a held-open anything. A woman asleep in her bed. My wife. And I watched her. And I kept watching her. And the watching didn't feel like fear, and it didn't feel like love, and it didn't feel like the thing I'd been doing for three weeks with my eyes cracked open and my breathing slow. It felt like the other thing. The thing I'd been doing since I was nine. The thing that was always mine, in this marriage, in this house, in every room I'd ever stood in. The watching was always my side of the bed. I watched her sleep and I thought: now it's me. I don't know what she traded. I don't know if she gave me back the thing that was wearing her, or if she gave the thing that was wearing her *me*, and I am the thing now, watching my wife sleep, attending to her the way a kettle gets attended to, the way a picture in somebody else's house gets attended to. I don't know if the woman in the bed is Mara, or if Mara drowned at Hester Lake and everything since has been a long, considerate kindness, a performance for the man who kept count. I don't know. And I've stopped asking, because the asking was the sickness, and I'm well now. I sleep. I drink the coffee. I go to work. I come home. And at night I lie awake and I watch her sleep, and she doesn't know, because she thinks I've been sleeping. She thought I'd been sleeping. I caught her staring at me again.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This final chapter is a remarkable piece of work that largely fulfills the ambitious intentions laid out in the writing plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Voice and Character Consistency** Daniel's voice is maintained with exceptional fidelity throughout. The trades-adjacent diction, the short declarative sentences that occasionally unspool into longer ones when exhaustion or dread takes over, the dry internal accounting — all of it holds. "I've been not-sleeping professionally since 1987" is the kind of line the character profile promises, and the chapter delivers its equivalent: "the counting was the thing I was doing instead of living in my own house." The self-correction, the repetition of "I sleep now" as a kind of incantation, the way he catches himself counting breaths and lets it run anyway — these are all deeply consistent with the man established across the story. The entity's voice, when it appears in memory, is exactly right: "You should sleep, Daniel" as a gift, the patient door-holding quality, the banal consideration. The plan called for the entity to be "considerate" and it is, even in retrospect. **The Ending and the Mirrored Opening** The plan held the mirrored opening line "in reserve" and specified it should "earn use only if the watching has already fully inverted before the final line, so the repetition lands as recognition, not trick." The chapter executes this with genuine craft. The inversion is complete: Daniel is now the watcher, possibly the thing that watches, possibly the thing that was traded for. The final two lines — "She thought I'd been sleeping. / I caught her staring at me again." — land not as a trick but as a recognition, exactly as planned. The pronouns have reversed. The reader arrives at the last line already understanding it. **The Central Ambiguity** The chapter's greatest achievement is sustaining the core ambiguity without cheating. "I don't know if the woman in the bed is Mara, or if Mara drowned at Hester Lake and everything since has been a long, considerate kindness" — this is the story's central question, and the chapter refuses to answer it while making the refusal feel earned rather than evasive. The possibility that Daniel himself is now the entity, or has been replaced, or has simply been accommodated in some way he can't parse, is genuinely unsettling. **The "But" Paragraph** The structural move of the "But" — a single word as its own paragraph — is one of the chapter's best moments. It's not purple, it's not a one-line dread paragraph in the way the plan warned against. It's a man who has been telling himself a story about wellness suddenly unable to maintain the story. The honesty of "I have to write it down, because I have been writing everything down, and I don't want to stop now just because the writing has started to feel like the last honest thing I do" is quietly devastating. **Potential Weaknesses** The chapter is not without issues. The phrase "the asking was the sickness" is repeated three times, and while the first two uses feel intentional and earned, the third ("I've stopped asking, because the asking was the sickness, and I'm well now") tips slightly into self-conscious refrain territory. The plan explicitly warned against "catalogue sentences" and the chapter comes close with the "I sleep, I drink the coffee, I go to work, I come home" sequence — though it's brief enough to function as incantation rather than catalogue. The line "like something got rinsed out of it" in the opening paragraph is the chapter's one moment of slightly strained metaphor — it's not bad, but it's the kind of image that calls attention to itself in a way the rest of the prose doesn't. The "set me down on the bed from a height of about an inch" is a genuinely original image for a particular kind of waking, and it lands. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally devastating in the right way — not through sentimentality but through the horror of Daniel's accommodation, his relief, his possible complicity in his own replacement. The coffee made wrong is a perfect detail: it could be Mara, it could be the entity having learned the error, it could be nothing. The plan called for "small, cold, unresolving details" and the chapter delivers them. **Dialogue** There is almost no dialogue, which is appropriate for this chapter. The one piece of remembered speech — "You should sleep, Daniel" — is exactly right in its register. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows the plan closely: the aftermath told slant, the coffee made wrong, the dog eating from both hands, the final inversion of the watching. The "one residue, not a braid" instruction is honored — the ambiguity is singular, not a list of unresolved threads. The plan's instruction to "end one scene early, on something small and cold" if the inversion isn't earned is superseded by the earned inversion, and the chapter correctly uses the mirrored line. **Overall Assessment** This is a very strong final chapter that earns its ending, maintains its voice, and delivers on the story's central promise without resolving it falsely. The prose is controlled, the dread is earned, and the ambiguity is genuine rather than evasive. Minor issues with repetition and one slightly strained image keep it from perfection, but it is close. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Compelling Plot: 17 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of horror fiction that operates primarily through psychological dread rather than supernatural spectacle. The narrator, Daniel, is a drywall subcontractor whose professional identity—measuring, covering, making surfaces smooth—becomes the central metaphor for his emotional life and his response to his wife's transformation after a near-drowning. The conceit is elegant and consistently executed: a man who learned to fake sleep as a child to survive a volatile household now uses that same skill to survive something far stranger. The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. Daniel's narration is precise, working-class, self-aware without being literary in a way that feels false to his character. Lines like "I'm a drywall subcontractor. I don't believe in things, I believe in coverage rates and screw lines" establish his worldview economically and then the story systematically dismantles it. The detail work is extraordinary—the looped g's on the grocery list, the lake smell on her breath, the eleven breaths to his four, the dog's shifting allegiances—these accumulate with the patience of genuine literary craft rather than horror-movie shorthand. The structure is inventive. The story begins with the prompt line, ends with its inversion, and the circular return ("She thought I'd been sleeping. / I caught her staring at me again") is genuinely chilling because the reader understands the full weight of what has been exchanged. This is not a cheap trick; it's earned through eight chapters of careful construction. The ambiguity is handled masterfully. The story never confirms whether this is supernatural possession, anoxic brain injury, or a husband's paranoid grief—and crucially, by the final chapter, it doesn't matter, because Daniel himself has become the watcher, the thing that attends without blinking. The horror is that the transformation may have been in him all along, or that he invited it, or that he became it through the act of watching. The final uncertainty—"I don't know if the woman in the bed is Mara, or if Mara drowned at Hester Lake and everything since has been a long, considerate kindness"—is genuinely unsettling. The pacing across eight chapters is mostly excellent. Chapters 1-3 build dread effectively. Chapter 4's notebook format is a smart structural shift that conveys deterioration. Chapters 5-6 manage the "two good days" false relief with sophistication. Chapter 7's confrontation scene is deliberately withheld in a way that could frustrate some readers but is thematically justified—Daniel himself doesn't fully understand what he agreed to. Chapter 8's resolution is haunting. Weaknesses: The piece occasionally tips into over-writing. Phrases like "a loop is just a line that comes back where it started" are the kind of aphoristic observations that feel slightly too neat, too aware of themselves as metaphors. The "remora" metaphor in Chapter 5 is one of several moments where the prose reaches for something and lands slightly off. The dialogue in Chapter 7—the 3 a.m. conversation about dry cleaning and the lake house—is effective but the "you should get to be right" line is perhaps too perfectly calibrated to Daniel's wound, slightly too on-the-nose. The scene at the lake in Chapter 6, where Daniel says "Give her back" to the water, risks bathos and nearly achieves it. The transformation in Chapter 7-8, where Daniel accepts the situation and begins drinking the water, could be seen as somewhat rushed given the careful accumulation of dread in earlier chapters. However, the story frames this as a kind of capitulation rather than a resolution, which partially redeems it. The "I don't know what I agreed to" refrain is effective. The dog as a barometer of supernatural wrongness is a well-worn horror device, but the story uses it with enough self-awareness (Daniel explicitly notes he's "taking depositions from a dog") to partially defuse the cliché. The handwriting detail is more original and more effectively deployed. Overall, this is a piece of genuine literary quality—not just competent horror but a story with something to say about grief, marriage, watching, and the way we perform normalcy for the people we love and fear. The circular structure, the consistent voice, and the thematic coherence place it well above average. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Pacing: 16 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
Avg Final Item Score: 82.3/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 83.9 | Final: 82.5)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Premise spins — who is the babysitter, and who are "they"?** 1. **The classic underestimation:** A quiet, polite young woman babysits for a suburban family. A crew of home invaders picks the wrong house. They leave her for dead — and she gets up. 2. **The kidnapping engine:** Armed men take the child she's watching (ransom? leverage against the parents? trafficking?) and leave her bleeding on the kitchen floor. Rescue *and* revenge in one through-line. 3. **The past comes calling:** She's a retired hitwoman / ex-syndicate fixer in hiding. Her old employers finally find her — mid-babysitting gig — and now a kid is caught in the crossfire of her old life. 4. **The culpable parents:** Dad owes money to the wrong people, or Mom's "business trip" is something else. The danger comes from the family itself, and the babysitter is collateral they didn't account for. Inversion: "they" could even be the employers, who frame or stiff her. 5. **The dead kid (darkest option):** They kill the child on her watch. No rescue, just grief and a body count. Maximum grimdark. 6. **The antihero:** She's a professional criminal casing the neighborhood via babysitting jobs. Someone fucks with *her* operation. Revenge between thieves. 7. **The heightened grindhouse:** Deliberately over-the-top — she's almost mythic, the violence operatic, dark comedy throughout. 8. **The supernatural whisper:** She's something older and worse than human. (Probably off-genre for pulp, but worth noting.) **Babysitter backstory options:** - Former syndicate hitwoman who walked away after a job went wrong (a child died — mirrors the current stakes) - Ex-special forces, discharged under cloudy circumstances - Witness protection transplant who testified against the mob - Raised on a compound / survivalist cult, escaped - Crime boss's ex-wife who ran with the ledger **Skill tells / pulp set pieces the genre promises:** - Early tell: she catches a falling knife, clocks the tail on her walk over, checks exits out of habit - The lockbox / buried duffel bag reveal (cash, passports, a .38 with the serial filed) - The "do you have any idea who she *is*?" scene where the villain realizes the mistake - Interrogation with banter — Leonard-style lowlife dialogue - The warehouse / roadhouse / chop-shop finale - The quiet coda: pancakes with the kid, or taillights heading out of town **Supporting cast pool:** - The kid (8–10, precocious, bonds with her over comics or horror movies — the emotional core) - The parents (clueless? complicit? one of each?) - An old contact — the "guy who knows things," a pawn shop, a crooked bartender - A cop — obstacle or reluctant ally - The villain ladder: street-level crew → middle manager → the man at the top **Tone palette:** grindhouse splatter; lean hardboiled noir; Elmore Leonard crime banter; desert neo-western; 1970s suburban period piece (landlines, payphones, no cell phones — great for pulp). **Pacing sketch over 8 × ~1000 words:** - Ch 1–2: Establish her, the family, the tell — then the transgression - Ch 3: She gets up. The turn. First reveal of who she is - Ch 4–5: The hunt begins; first blood; climb the ladder - Ch 6: Midpoint reveal / setback — the truth about *why* they came - Ch 7: The storm — climactic assault - Ch 8: Coda — the kid, the cost, the road Each chapter = roughly one strong scene plus a hook. No fat anywhere. --- # Reflection The prompt's pleasure is **ironic underestimation** — the gap between what "they" think she is and what she actually is. Whatever direction I choose has to maximize that gap and then pay it off with cathartic, escalating violence. The title phrase also implies a *reaction*: the story truly begins when they fuck with her, so the transgression needs to land early, probably end of chapter 1 or mid-chapter 2. Eight thousand words doesn't allow a slow burn. On premise: the **kidnapping engine (option 2)** looks strongest on reflection. It combines rescue and revenge, gives a built-in ticking clock, keeps the kid alive as emotional stakes, and makes "wrong babysitter" literal — they should have made sure she was dead, or never touched her kid. Option 1 (pure home invasion) works but loses the child as a motive and shrinks the plot to a single night. Option 3 (her past comes calling) is tempting because it explains her skills organically, but it risks making the babysitting gig feel like set dressing — though a hybrid is possible: random criminals whose boss turns out to connect to her old life. Option 5 (dead kid) is probably too bleak; it flattens the emotional register to one note and loses the warmth that makes pulp revenge sing. The dead child in her *backstory* can carry that weight instead — motivation without alienating the reader. Option 4 (culpable parents) is a strong *secondary* thread: maybe Dad's debt or secret is *why* the men came, giving the midpoint reveal. On her backstory: the **retired hitwoman who left after a child died on a job** is doing the most thematic work — it makes saving this kid redemption, not just rage, and gives the coda an emotional landing. Ex-special forces is fine but generic; witness protection forces a mob plot that eats word count. On scope: 8k words means ruthless economy. One POV (close third on her), a three-rung villain ladder at most, maybe five speaking roles total. Any conspiracy must be legible in a single paragraph of dialogue. Retro or timeless-leaning setting helps — no cell phones means investigation requires *legs*, which is more pulp anyway. On tone: pure grimdark torture-revenge would be exhausting at this length; pure comedy would undercut the stakes. The sweet spot feels like Leonard-meets-grindhouse — real danger, real grief, but criminals with personality and a babysitter with dry one-liners. The violence should be abrupt and consequential rather than lingered on. Open questions for the outline phase: exactly who "they" are and why they came; whether the parents are culpable or merely negligent; how much of her backstory to reveal versus imply; whether the villain ever learns her name (the "do you know who she is?" beat); and the ending's temperature — clean triumph, or victory with a cost (she has to disappear, leaving the kid behind, which could be the gut-punch that elevates it above pastiche).
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core premise:** Armed men take the eight-year-old boy a babysitter is watching and leave her for dead on the kitchen floor. They should have made sure. What follows is rescue *and* revenge braided into one relentless through-line: she hunts up a ladder of lowlifes to get the kid back before the deadline expires, leaving a body count behind her. **The protagonist:** **Ruth Calloway**, late twenties, quiet, polite, forgettable — deliberately so. Ten years ago she was "the Lark," a syndicate fixer. She walked away after a job went wrong and a child died — a boy about the same age as the one she now babysits. That buried guilt is the engine of the story: saving this kid isn't just rage, it's redemption she doesn't believe she deserves. Her backstory is *implied through action and scars* more than explained — a couple of loaded lines, one old contact who knows the name, and one moment where the villain hears "the Lark" and goes pale. No extended flashbacks. The mystery of her competence is part of the pulp pleasure. **The transgression and the ladder:** The crew didn't come for her — they came because **Danny's father, Frank** (weak, indebted, gambling) fed the syndicate a lie to buy time, and the syndicate took his son as leverage. The parents are therefore *negligent-complicit*: Dad culpable, Mom (away on a business trip) unaware. This gives the midpoint reveal its bite — Ruth isn't just fighting strangers, she's fighting the consequences of the family's own rot, and there will be a reckoning with Frank too. Villain ladder, three rungs: **the street crew** (led by a jumpy, chatty thug — Leonard-style banter) → **the middle man**, a bail-bonds front operator who knows where the kid is held → **Marlowe**, the syndicate man at the top, who once knew the Lark by reputation. **Setting:** Timeless-leaning, deliberately retro-adjacent — landlines, payphones, cash, a chop shop, a roadhouse. No cell phones: investigation requires legs and nerve, which is more pulp. Small industrial city, summer heat. **The kid:** **Danny, eight**, precocious, bonded with Ruth over horror movies and contraband comics. He gets one or two beats from his side of the captivity (kept brief, filtered through Ruth's POV via imagination or a phone call) — enough to keep him a person, not a parcel. He lives. He is brave in a small, believable way that matters at the climax. **Voice and style:** Close third person on Ruth, present-tense-leaning immediacy in prose rhythm (though written in past tense), short declarative sentences in action, longer breath in quiet scenes. Tone: **Leonard-meets-grindhouse** — real danger, real grief, criminals with personality, and Ruth armed with dry, economical one-liners. Violence is abrupt, consequential, and unglamorous — *felt*, not lingered on. No torture porn. Chapter endings hook hard. One POV only. Aim for ~1000 words per chapter: essentially one strong scene plus a hook per chapter, no fat. **The "do you know who she is?" beat:** Delivered twice in miniature — once when a lowlife laughs and won't believe it, once when Marlowe hears the name and stops laughing. That reversal is the prompt's pleasure and must land. **The ending's temperature:** Victory with a cost. Danny comes home safe. Frank faces his reckoning (Ruth's doing, not the law's — a quieter, colder punishment). But Ruth's name is spoken aloud again in the wrong rooms, and the life she built is burned. Final image: she slips away before dawn — taillights, a note, and Danny keeping her secret — triumph shadowed by departure. Elevates it above pastiche and honors the pulp road-coda tradition. **Things to avoid:** the dead kid (off the table — he lives); long backstory dumps; a sprawling conspiracy (the whole plot must be legible in one paragraph of dialogue); quipping that undercuts menace; lingering gore; cartoonish invincibility (Ruth gets hurt, tired, and scared — she wins on will and tradecraft, not superpowers). --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 — "Friday Night" (~1000 words)** Cold open in motion: Ruth arrives at the Delgado house, does her quiet ritual — clocks the street, notes a car idling too long, files it. Establish her economy and politeness; establish the bond with Danny (horror-movie debate, popcorn, an in-joke about babysitters in slasher films — light irony). Small tell for the reader: she catches a falling knife by the blade, reflexes she covers with a joke. Frank leaves with a nervous phone call he takes in the driveway — first whisper of his trouble. *Hook:* the idling car is still there at dark. Then headlights in the driveway that shouldn't be. Ruth moves Danny to the bathroom, tells him the game: *quiet like hide-and-seek, no matter what you hear.* **Chapter 2 — "They Should Have Made Sure" (~1000 words)** The home invasion, fast and ugly. Three men, masks, professional enough to be scary, sloppy enough to be beatable. Ruth fights — kills one with the knife from the drying rack, nearly wins — but the crew leader (**Boone**, chatty, jumpy) gets the drop and puts her down: a blow, a gunshot graze, left bleeding on the kitchen tile. They take Danny. Boone's parting line establishes the motive in pulp shorthand: *"Tell Frank the clock's running."* Ruth, bleeding out, uses the last of her strength to do two things: stop the bleeding with a dish towel and a belt, and memorize — a tattoo, a nickname spoken, the van's plates. *Hook:* she pulls the family photos off the fridge, finds Frank's number for his "book club," and drags herself up. The babysitter is gone. Something else stands up. **Chapter 3 — "The Lark Gets Up" (~1000 words)** Ruth's night of triage and transformation. She stitches herself in the bathroom mirror (economy, no melodrama — she's done this before). She opens the hall closet's false bottom / her trunk: the duffel — cash, a .38 with the serial filed, a burner, a jacket that hides both. One phone call to an old contact, **Sal**, a bartender who knew her in the old life; his silence when she says her name tells the reader everything. He gives her the tattoo: it belongs to a crew that runs collections for **Marlowe**. Ruth's single backstory line, delivered to no one: *"Ten years I was somebody else. Somebody better."* Also: she calls 911 anonymously from a payphone to keep cops off her back a few hours. *Hook:* Frank comes home mid-chapter-end, sees the blood, and lies badly — and Ruth watches him lie, and files it. **Chapter 4 — "First Rung" (~1000 words)** Ruth leans on the street crew. A bar at the industrial edge, then a follow to a flophouse. Leonard-style scene: she lets a lowlife (**Dooley**, the third man from the crew) underestimate her completely — the prompt's pleasure in miniature — before the abrupt turn. She gets Boone's name and the structure: collections crew, snatch ordered from above, kid held "at the shop" until Frank pays what he can't. Dooley laughs at the idea anyone's coming for them. She makes him stop laughing. Cost: Ruth is hurt worse than she admits (the graze, cracked ribs) and the clock is ticking — Marlowe's people don't keep leverage alive past its usefulness. *Hook:* Frank calls her burner, panicking — the syndicate moved the deadline up. Dawn. **Chapter 5 — "The Middle Man" (~1000 words)** Ruth works up the ladder: **the shop** is a chop shop / bail-bonds front run by **Eddie Vale**, Marlowe's middle man. Infiltration over assault this time — she comes in as something she's not (a buyer? a desperate girlfriend?), lets Eddie talk, because men like Eddie talk. He clocks her too late. The scene turns: short, brutal, decisive. She learns two things: Danny is held at **Marlowe's roadhouse** out on the county line, moved there tonight — and that Frank didn't just owe money. *Frank offered the syndicate something else.* The truth curdles: Frank told them the house would be empty Friday night. He didn't know about the babysitter… or did he? *Hook:* Sal calls back with a warning in a changed voice: *"Ruth — Marlowe knows the Lark's name. He just heard it from a dying man. He smiled."* **Chapter 6 — "What Frank Did" (~1000 words)** The midpoint reckoning. Ruth confronts Frank at the house at 3 a.m., .38 on the table between them. The full reveal, legible in one breath: Frank's gambling debt, the lies, and the worst of it — he gave them the night, and when they asked who'd be home, he said *nobody that matters.* Ruth's restraint is the scene's violence: she could kill him and doesn't, because Danny still needs a father — but she makes Frank an instrument of the rescue instead. He'll make the drop call Marlowe demands, say the money's coming, buy her two hours. Also in this chapter: one phone call with Danny (Marlowe allows it, to prove the kid's alive — pulp tradition). Danny, brave in a small way, passes her a coded piece of info using their horror-movie in-joke. *Hook:* Ruth drives toward the county line as the sky goes gray, and we learn what Marlowe's smile meant: he's not waiting for money. He's waiting for *her.* **Chapter 7 — "The Roadhouse" (~1000 words)** The storm. Marlowe has arranged a reception — he wants the Lark alive, to use her or to collect the old bounty on her. The assault: Ruth uses tradecraft over firepower — the lights, the gravel lot, the trucks, the panic of men who can't see what's killing them. Abrupt violence, mounting cost: she's cut, exhausted, running on will. Danny does his small brave thing (the in-joke payoff — he does exactly what a Final Girl does, because she taught him). Boone dies mid-chaos, badly. Final corridor: Marlowe, calm, offers her the old life back — and the "do you know who she is?" beat lands in reverse: *he* knew all along, and it didn't save him. She finishes it without ceremony. *Hook/beat:* silence, dawn through the roadhouse windows, Danny's hand in hers: *"Can we go home now?"* **Chapter 8 — "Before Sunrise" (~1000 words)** The coda, three movements. **One:** the drive home — pancakes at a diner, Danny asleep against her shoulder, the warmth that makes the cost legible. **Two:** Frank's reckoning — Ruth delivers him an envelope: his confession, written in his own hand, and a choice (tell his wife everything, get help, or the envelope goes to people worse than Marlowe). Cold, quiet, done. **Three:** Sal's last call — Marlowe's death means the name "the Lark" is traveling again; the life is burned. Final scene: Ruth slips away before dawn, leaves Danny a note and the contraband comic she promised him; Danny wakes, reads it, and keeps her secret — tells his mother the babysitter was *brave*. Last image: taillights on the county road, and Ruth's dry closing line, to no one, in the spirit of her character: *"Wrong house, boys."*
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Would Approach This Piece First, notice what the prompt *is*: not a premise but a **voice**. "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" is a sentence someone *says* — over a beer, in a squad car, at a barbeque years later. A human's first instinct is likely tonal: match that swagger or deadpan against it. My plan did neither — I chose a neutral close third and never asked who is telling this story or why. That's a common human starting point too, granted, but a lot of humans would hear that line and immediately write first-person confession ("I babysat for the Delgados for two years. This is what happened Friday") or a barroom-teller third ("You hear about the babysitter?"). The prompt hands you an attitude for free; I banked it instead of spending it. Second, humans approach through **idiosyncrasy, not coverage**. A human has seen *Adventures in Babysitting* and *When a Stranger Calls* and the Netflix *Babysitter*; they remember an actual babysitter from childhood; they have a grudge or a crush or a specific town. They fixate. They overwrite the scene they were excited about and rush the connective tissue. They fall in love with a minor character and give him too much page time. They plant something and forget to pay it off. The result is *unevenness* — and unevenness is a texture readers read as human. My plan covers everything on schedule. It has zero sprawl, zero waste, zero obsession. That's not a compliment. Third, a human genre writer would probably channel a specific ancestor — Stark, Leonard, *The Housemaid*-style pulp — and that constraint shapes their sentences in ways I can't reverse-engineer from "Leonard-meets-grindhouse," which is a mood board, not a lineage. # Telltale LLM Moves — and Why They'd Poison This Specific Piece **Safety and sanitization.** LLMs write PG-13 by default. The prompt literally contains "fucked" — the title's register is R-rated vernacular — and my plan contains no profanity, no transgression, violence that's explicitly "consequential but not lingered on." Pulp's charge *comes* from transgression. Not gore — but at least one moment that makes a reader flinch or laugh inappropriately. If the story is more polite than its title, the title is lying. **Bow-tying.** LLMs resolve everything and give every character their "moment." Pulp has a mean streak: threads dangle, the wrong person dies, justice arrives crooked or not at all. A plan that assigns every payoff in advance (mine literally schedules the horror-movie setup for payoff in ch6 and ch7) is building a machine, and machines produce satisfaction, not surprise. **Impressiveness over readability.** Kicker-line chapter endings, trailer-friendly one-liners, "delivered in miniature" beat management. The kicker habit is especially dangerous at 8 chapters: eight mic-drops in a row stops being rhythm and becomes a tic. Humans end chapters flat sometimes, or mid-motion, because the scene just stops. **Competence fantasy with decorative flaws.** LLM protagonists' "flaws" are physical costs (a graze, cracked ribs) that never impair judgment. They never make the consequential error. Real crime fiction runs on culpability — Parker walks into traps, trusts wrong, and people die *because of his choices*. An unerring protagonist isn't a character; she's a power fantasy in a wound costume. **Theme announcement and legibility.** LLMs state the theme in the plan ("the gap between what they think she is and what she actually is") and then dramatize it exactly on schedule. Human writers often discover the theme mid-draft, bury it, or let the story contradict it. "Legible" is an LLM value; humans tolerate residue. **Mystery-box reveals instead of irony.** My plot escalates through withheld information — why they came, what Frank did, what Marlowe knows. But all three reveals are guessable by a genre-literate reader by chapter 3. Humans often get more tension from the opposite: tell the reader early, let the dread come from Ruth not knowing. **Mechanical prose rhythms.** The drafting itself will betray me if I'm not careful: triads ("hurt, tired, and scared" — I wrote that exact rule-of-three in my intention), "She didn't X. She Y'd." constructions, weather-as-punctuation at scene ends (my plan has *both* "the sky goes gray" *and* "dawn through the roadhouse windows"), and the Sherlock-scan where the protagonist catalogs "exits, weapons, hands" to signal competence. # Deep Dive: Where the Plan Is Already Guilty Going through my own intention and chapter plan with a cold eye: **Ruth is a closed system.** The dead-child backstory is the LLM's favorite shortcut: guilt as character depth. It explains her so completely that nothing can surprise us about her. Worse, my intention says she seeks "redemption she doesn't believe she deserves" — that's therapy-speak, a very LLM register for a pulp killer. And notice the name: *Ruth*. *Calloway*. I hid "ruthless" in her name. That's exactly the kind of pleased-with-itself cleverness that reads generated. **"Delivered twice in miniature."** I portioned out the "do you know who she is?" beat like a pharmacist. The prompt's entire pleasure is that reversal, and I've turned it into a dosing schedule. **Boone "dies badly."** I assigned him a karmic death because he's the chatty one. Karmic death-assignment is the LLM making sure the universe stays fair. A human lets the wrong person die at the wrong time, mid-sentence, undignified. **Frank's reckoning is justice-with-dignity.** An envelope, a choice, cold and quiet and *merciful*. And worse — he successfully completes his buy-time task offscreen, so the machine proceeds unimpeded. A weak man doing his one job correctly because the plot needs him to is a convenience, not a character. **The coda is five endings stacked.** Pancakes, Frank's envelope, Sal's warning, the note and comic, Danny's secret, *and* a closing quip. I counted: that's at least five terminal gestures. It's the LLM making sure every emotion is delivered with tracking numbers. "The babysitter was *brave*" — the story literally ends with a witness certifying the protagonist's virtue. Maximum bow. **"Wrong house, boys."** A context-free screenplay quip, chosen because it sounds cool in a plan. It might work. But the *instinct* to end on the callback is mechanical, and I should be suspicious of it on the page. **Ruth makes no consequential mistakes.** I catalogued: after she stands up in ch2, she never misjudges anyone, never acts on bad info, never hurts the wrong person, never arrives late because of her own error. The costs are all recoverable. Also — the syndicate apparently just *let her walk away* ten years ago, because that's what syndicates do in these stories. **Danny is a function.** He exists to motivate Ruth, pass coded info via the in-joke (twee, and an 8-year-old doing Final Girl moves risks preciousness), and validate her at the end. He never does the thing a real kid might: blame her. She fought and they took him anyway — kids absolutely blame. **"The Storm," "First Rung," "The Lark Gets Up."** Trailer-title chapter headings. Packaging instinct. **The retro-no-cellphones choice might be a dodge.** I justified it as craft, but it also frees me from writing convincing modern texture. A human might set it now and let a doorbell cam or a kid's smartwatch create fresh pulp friction. My version risks running on genre signifiers instead of observed world — speaking of which, the plan mentions "summer heat" once and then *nothing uses it*. No smells, no specific brands, no texture. All mechanics and dialogue. **The gender question is unaddressed.** "Babysitter" is gendered labor; three masked men alone with a young woman carries a threat register any human writer would have to consciously handle — engage it or deliberately sidestep it. My plan doesn't even name the decision. The sanitized version (professional crew, no grossness) can read as evasive if it happens by default rather than by choice. **The backstory is the most ready-made option.** Retired syndicate fixer is the single most common action-heroine origin. Alternatives I never seriously entertained: ER nurse (violence-adjacent competence
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Premise (unchanged at its core):** Armed men take eight-year-old Danny Delgado from his babysitter on a Friday night and leave her for dead. She comes after them. But the telling gets rebuilt from the ground up. **The single biggest choice — voice:** The prompt is a spoken sentence, so the story is spoken. **First-person, retrospective:** Ruth tells this years later, to someone, over a drink, starting with the prompt itself as her opening line: *"They fucked with the wrong babysitter. That's not bragging. It's just what happened."* This does several jobs at once. It licenses digression, flat chapter endings, admitted gaps ("I never found out why the dog didn't bark"), and self-deprecating humor instead of trailer quips. It lets her *refuse* to narrate things, which is more compelling than mystery-box plotting. And it solves the backstory problem: she tells us the old life in one flat paragraph because she's past caring, not because the plot needs a reveal. She gives her name as a handle — *"Call me Ruth"* — which converts the too-cute name into an honest narrator's lie. **De-mythologizing her:** No codename. No "the Lark." She wasn't an elite hitwoman — she ran collections for a crew in the eighties, the *same kind of work* the men
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Ruth Calloway (narrator — "call me Ruth") She ran collections for a crew in the eighties. Not the movie version — no black turtlenecks, no codenames. She drove a Buick with a baby seat she never removed in the back because it made traffic stops shorter, and she collected money from people who didn't want to pay it, mostly by being patient and eventually being believed. The work was boring, which was the point; the men she worked with were boring too, in the way that dangerous men often are — all grievance and TV. She quit at thirty-one, not over a moral awakening but because her boss's brother-in-law got promoted over her and she realized she'd been doing volunteer work for a family she'd never be in. There is a body count — she says the number, flatly, and doesn't itemize, and the reader has to live with that. She got out with a discipline she calls "the program," and the program is pettily specific: no forged papers (fake ID is a debt someone can call in), no guns (a gun makes every problem look solvable the same way), new city every four years, jobs where nobody writes your name down — night stocking, line cook, dog groomer. Babysitting is new, and it has one purpose: Danny. She's not seeking redemption — she's contemptuous of the word — she's seeking an exit, and she's noticed she can't find the door. Years of clean living have left her as vigilant and joyless as the day she quit. Danny is the only thing in ten years that makes her laugh, and she is quietly aware that an eight-year-old is doing all the work in their friendship. Her flaw is not guilt; it's *contempt* — for Frank, for Sal, for the men who took Danny, and most of all for the version of herself she catches in mirrors. Contempt makes her fast and makes her wrong: she misreads people she respects less than herself, which is nearly everyone. She also has a tell she doesn't know about: she hums when she's deciding to do something terrible. Danny noticed it. He never told her what tune. Voice: flat, dry, digressive, profane in a working rather than performative way, constantly undercutting her own legend. She narrates years later, to someone unnamed, over a drink she doesn't finish. Physicality: late thirties now (late twenties then). Middling height, strong hands with a groomer's nicks and old breaks, a face people forget — she has cultivated this and is vain about it the way ugly-beautiful people are vain. Moves like someone conserving something. Smiles rarely and is annoyed when she does. > "People say *hitwoman* like it's a job with business cards. I collected money. Sometimes the collection was a promise. You're thinking that's a euphemism. It is and it isn't — mostly it was Tuesday." > "I want to tell you I thought about calling the police. I did think about it. I thought about it the whole time I was doing something else." > "Ten years of the program. Four cities, nine apartments, one dog that lived. And what got me wasn't the life catching up. It was a Friday and a bowl of popcorn. That's how it gets everybody, by the way. Nobody's ever taken out during the part they were worried about." # Danny Delgado Eight years old, fourth grade, one front tooth grown in crooked so his smile lists to port. He is not precocious in the movie way — he doesn't explain themes or dispense wisdom — he's *observant* in the way of kids whose homes run on tension, fluent in adult weather: he can tell from the driveway gravel how much trouble his father is in. He loves horror movies not for the gore but for the rules — he's a rules kid, the kind who corrects Monopoly — and his passion is ranking, categorizing, and litigating the Final Girl canon with Ruth, who he has decided is the only adult who argues back honestly. His relationship with Ruth is real and slightly conspiratorial: she lets him watch things his parents wouldn't, and he, in return, never tells them she cried once at a dog food commercial (she hadn't slept in two years, roughly). He notices things about her nobody else does — the humming, the way she checks the street the way his grandmother checked for rain — and files them the way kids do, without alarm, as simply *who she is*. After the kidnapping he is not a parcel. He's scared, carsick, furious — and furious partly at Ruth, because she promised hide-and-seek would work and it didn't. His courage at the roadhouse is small, realistic, and partly accidental: he does the Final Girl thing not because he's brave but because it's the only script he's got. Afterwards he has nightmares he hides, because he learns fast that telling adults the truth makes them worse. His arc is the quiet one: the story ends with him keeping two secrets — Ruth's, and his father's — and understanding, in a way that will take him twenty years to articulate, that the two secrets are different weights. As an adult he sometimes passes the roadhouse site. It's a self-storage place now. Physicality: small for eight, elbows like tools, a Dodgers cap he wears indoors until told, a voice still high and going shrill when he argues, which is often. > "That's not a rule, that's a *suggestion*. The rule is you never go in the basement. If she went in the basement it's not a real movie." > (in the van, remembered later) "I didn't cry because crying is what they want. I read that somewhere. Or Ruth said it. It might have been about dogs." # Frank Delgado Danny's father, forty-one, a regional salesman for a restaurant-supply company who peaked at twenty-seven and has been quietly refinancing the peak ever since. He bets — not compulsively in the gutter way, but in the *smart guy* way, which is worse: systems, hunches, a guy he knows. He owes Marlowe's crew thirty-one thousand and change, and what makes him contemptible isn't the debt, it's the *performance of coping*: the jacket still pressed, the jokes at the door, the phone calls taken in the driveway with his body angled so the kitchen window can't read his face. He gave them Friday. This is the thing the story turns on, and it's human in the worst way: he didn't sell his son, he *borrowed against him*, convinced in the marrow-deep way of gamblers that the money was coming Monday, that the leverage would never need to be real. When they asked who'd be home, he said nobody that matters — and he has since rewritten that sentence so many times in his head that he almost remembers saying something else. He half-forgot the babysitter existed. That's the version he'll carry. His flaw is that he experiences his own cowardice as *circumstance* — things happen to Frank; he is always at the mercy of a run of bad luck that somehow knows his address. Under pressure he doesn't rise; he *functions*, which is more damning — he makes the buy-time call competently, even well, and part of him is proud of it afterward, and he hates that part, briefly, before refinancing that too. His reckoning is not death and not quite forgiveness. Ruth makes him live with the knowledge, in his house, next to his wife, doing better — the punishment is the performance, maintained for decades. He does, mostly, do better. He never forgives Ruth for being the reason. Physicality: going soft in a way he dresses against, good hair he touches too much, a laugh that arrives a half-second early. Stands too close when lying. Smells faintly of the cologne sample inserts in men's magazines. > "You don't know what it's like to be *due*. When you're due, it's not gambling anymore, it's math. I was so due it was basically *owed* to me." > (on the phone, to the driveway) "Yeah, no, Friday's fine, Friday's — the house is empty, it's just — yeah. Nobody. Friday." # Sal Sixty, owns a bar that opens at noon and closes when Sal feels like it, which is early more often since the stent. He's the last person from the old life Ruth still talks to — or rather, the only one she hasn't lost, because Sal outlived everyone by being, in his own words, "too boring to kill." He was never a made anything. He fenced a little, tended bar a lot, and kept confidences the way some men keep tools — well-oiled, rarely used, loaned to nobody. His role in the story is small and load-bearing: the two phone calls, the warning about Marlowe, and the last conversation Ruth has in the city. What makes him human is that he is *tired of the genre he's in*. He's seen this movie — the old name spoken again, the young men smiling — and his response isn't gravitas, it's irritation and heartburn. He doesn't warn Ruth to be careful; he warns her that careful isn't the point anymore, and then he complains about his grandson. He loves Ruth in the unspoken, slightly unrequited way of old men who watched someone survive — he gave her, ten years ago, one piece of advice she didn't take ("get a boring man and let him make you boring") and he has never mentioned it since, which is its own sentence. He doesn't know about Danny until she tells him, and when she does, he does the math instantly — a kid — and the reader sees him decide, in one beat, that she's already gone from his life and this call is a goodbye she won't admit to. Physicality: big man gone slack, white forearm hair, reading glasses on a sports lanyard, wipes glasses when he's about to say something true. Polishes the same spot of bar through entire conversations. > "You know what the difference is between you and them? Nothing. That's the problem. You were never the special one, you were just the one who left, and they *hate* a deserter worse than a cop." > "Kid, I got a stent, a dachshund, and a grandson who texts me in *emojis*. You think I'm built for your second act? Do what you're doing and lose the number." # Marlowe The man at the top of a ladder that turns out to be shorter than advertised — this is intentional. Marlowe is not a criminal mastermind; he's a *mid-level guy with good branding*, running collections, a chop shop, and a roadhouse bar out on the county line, and he has survived twenty years on one insight: *reputation is cheaper than violence and spends better*. He is therefore the inverse of Ruth — she has spent ten years erasing herself, he has spent twenty inflating himself, and when he hears her old name (the boring truth of it — a collector, not an assassin) what excites him isn't fear, it's *marketing*: the story of Marlowe's crew taking down a boogeyman is worth more than the thirty-one thousand. He is dangerous the way vain men are dangerous — unpredictably, over slights, past the point of sense — and he makes the fatal error the prompt demands, but for a human reason: he can't resist *witnesses*. He wants the story told. He lets Ruth live long enough to be an audience for his version of events, because an unwitnessed victory, to Marlowe, is just expenses. His dialogue is the one place the story allows itself some Leonard juice: he talks like a man who has been interviewed — quotes himself, refers to his own reputation in third person, corrects the record. He's genuinely funny twice, which is worse than if he weren't. He dies mid-anecdote, mid-plea, mid-sentence — no completion, no last-word summation. The story denies him the thing he wanted most: a good ending to his own story. Ruth takes his instead. Physicality: fifty-five, tan in a way that suggests a boat he doesn't own, turquoise ring, soft voice he makes people lean into. Gestures small and precise, like a man dealing cards. > "Everybody's somebody, sweetheart. That's the business. You used to be somebody who *collected*. Now you're somebody's *babysitter*. I don't see the promotion." > "You know what I heard about you? Doesn't matter. What matters is what *they* heard. I'm basically in the story business." # Boone Crew leader on the Friday job, twenty-nine, all forward momentum and borrowed confidence. He's the story's source of lowlife banter, but the banter has a function: Boone talks when he's *unsure*, which means the reader can hear the crew's sloppiness in real time — they're collections guys who got promoted to kidnapping, and they're not enjoying it. He shot Ruth (a graze, but he believes, and tells Marlowe, that he killed her) and the uncertainty of that eats him for the rest of the story; when she surfaces alive, his fear curdles into a weird, aggrieved fatalism, like a man whose lottery ticket was misprinted. He is not given a grand karmic death. He dies in the roadhouse dark the way these men actually die — scared, loud, and not understanding the sequence of events, having made exactly one more bad decision than he had time for. His last exchange with Ruth is deliberately *not* the satisfaction scene; she barely speaks to him. The story reserves its contempt for men with more power than Boone, which he would find insulting, if he had time. Small human details: he's squeamish about the kid (brings Danny fast food, lies that it's "what kids eat," watches him eat it with something like homesickness). He has a girlfriend pregnant and a plan to go legit that's as real as Frank's systems. Physicality: tall, raw-boned, a neck tattoo he got at nineteen that identifies him (Ruth's one solid lead — she describes it: a panther that looks, Danny later tells her, "like a mad horse"). Cracks his neck constantly. Can't stand still. > "This is a *collections* gig, okay? Nobody's supposed to get — look, the kid eats, the kid sleeps, everybody's a *professional* —" > (to Ruth, at the end, betrayed) "I *shot* you. I shot you, that's — you don't get to just *walk around*." # Linda Delgado Danny's mother, appears on-page only at the end, but the story's moral weight lands on her. Thirty-nine, a pharmaceutical rep whose travel schedule Frank has quietly expanded in the telling — how much she *needed* to be gone, how much she chose to be gone, is one of the questions Danny will spend adulthood not asking. She comes home Sunday to a husband with a story, a son with new silences, and a babysitter who has vanished leaving a note and cash for the broken door. She believes Frank. This is the cruelest thing the story does: not a villain exposed, but a family resealing itself. Danny watches her believe him and learns the difference between the secrets he keeps. Ruth, narrating years later, is ambivalent — she gave Frank the chance partly *for* Linda, and has never decided if that was mercy or cowardice of her own, one more person protected from the truth at the price of living next to it. She gets one scene — the doorstep, the casserole-into-freezer competence of a woman metabolizing crisis — and one line that proves she noticed more than the story assumes. What she does with it, Ruth never learns, and says so. Physicality: quick, contained, expensive casual clothes worn without vanity; a hand that rests on Danny's head like a lighthouse beam. Looks at adults the way Danny looks at movies: for the rules. > (the line) "The police report says three men. Danny says there was a *lady*. So you can tell me again, Frank, slower." # Minor presences **Dooley** — third man on the Friday crew; the one Ruth turns first. Sweaty, garrulous, keeps snacks in his car, dies off-page as a consequence rather than a set piece (Ruth learns it secondhand and is briefly, uselessly sorry — he was the one who thought to bring the kid a comic). **Eddie Vale** — bail bondsman, Marlowe's middle man, runs the chop shop. A talker with a systems mind — he explains his own operation to Ruth partly out of professional pride, the way bores do, which is how men like Eddie actually get caught. Lives — another deliberate asymmetry — and is last seen doing the arithmetic of flipping on Marlowe, which he does, incompetently, later. **The neighbor, Mrs. Abernathy** — seventy-something, across the street, saw the van, told the police the wrong color with total confidence. Ruth likes her anyway. She is the story's one representative of ordinary life, and her casserole, delivered during chapter six's reckoning scene like a farce beat, is the only thing in that house nobody deserves.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One They fucked with the wrong babysitter. That's the whole story in one line, and I know it's the line you came for, so I'll give it to you up front, the way I've learned to. You're buying, so you get the long version. But understand that the short one is the true one, and the long one is mostly me telling you how nobody in it knew what they were doing. Least of all me. Call me Ruth. It isn't my name, but it's near enough, and it's what Danny called me, and he's the only person in this I'd lie for, so his name for me is the one you get. Friday I got to the Delgados' at six, same as every Friday that summer. Little ranch house on a street of little ranch houses, the kind of block where the lawns are mowed by retirees and judged by everybody else. Mrs. Abernathy across the street was in her front window when I pulled up, same as always. She watched that street the way other people watch television, and with about the same critical standards. I want to tell you I clocked the street out of professional instinct. The truth is cheaper. I checked the street because I'd been checking streets for years and couldn't stop, the way you keep an accent you can't hear anymore. It wasn't vigilance. It was a tic. There was a car. I'll tell you about it now so you don't sit there waiting for it. Dark sedan, idling at the curb four houses down, nobody behind the wheel that I could see. I noticed it the way you notice a wasp — not scared, just aware — and I filed it under Frank. Frank owed somebody. Frank always owed somebody; I'd worked for the man long enough to know the flavor of his nervous. A strange car outside Frank's house didn't crack the five most interesting things about that evening. You can go ahead and feel superior about that. I did, for years. Danny met me at the door already arguing. He was eight, small for it, one front tooth in sideways so his whole smile listed to port, Dodgers cap on indoors like his mother wasn't gone three days a week specifically so somebody could tell him to take it off. He had a movie in each hand and a position prepared. "Before you say anything," he said, "the second one isn't scary, it's just gross, and gross isn't against the rules." "The rules," I said, "are your mother's." "You're my mother's representative." I let him have the gross one. That was our arrangement, built over a year of Fridays: he got movies his parents wouldn't have allowed, and I got a kid who argued about them like a tiny lawyer, which was the only thing in my life at that point that made me laugh out loud. He had a whole system of horror-movie law. It had precedents. It had a supreme court, which was him. We made popcorn on the stove, the foil kind with the dome that swells up, and he walked me through the rulings while it burned a little, because it always burned a little. "The rule is you don't go in the basement," he said. "Going in the basement isn't brave, it's *wrong*, and if she goes in the basement then whatever happens is her fault and it's not a real movie." "And the closet?" "Closet's different." He got very serious, which on Danny meant his ears went red. "If she's in the closet she doesn't come out. No matter what she hears. Even if it sounds like somebody she knows. *Especially* if it sounds like somebody she knows. That's the closet rule." I told him that was sound doctrine. He said he knew. Frank came through around seven in a shirt pressed hard enough to stand on its own, smelling of magazine cologne, laughing half a second before anything was funny. Linda was in Phoenix until Sunday, moving pills for a company with a bird in the logo. Frank told me not to let the kid stay up past nine, in the tone of a man reciting a line from a play he'd never attended. Then he went out to the driveway and stood with the cordless phone angled away from the kitchen window, and I caught one piece of it through the screen door — *the house is empty, yeah, nobody* — and I want to be honest with you here, because honesty is the whole point of telling it this way. I heard a man say *the house is empty* on a Friday night, with his wife in Phoenix, and I thought: girlfriend. I stood there with all my supposed instincts, ten years of learning to read men like Frank, and I thought the man was planning a date. The sedan was gone by dark. I mention that because it matters and because I didn't, at the time. Danny sacked out on the couch somewhere in the second movie, cap over his eyes, and I stood at the window a while with the lights off behind me, looking at the street not being anything. Danny told me years later that I hummed when I did that. He said I hummed that night, standing at his window, and it was a tune he knew. He wouldn't tell me what it was. He said I'd be mad. The headlights came around ten. I knew it was wrong before the engine cut. Wrong speed up the drive, too many doors. Mrs. Abernathy's window was dark by then, for which I have never once blamed her. Danny came up out of sleep when I touched his shoulder, and here's the thing about that kid, the thing this whole story hangs on: he didn't ask a single question. He looked at my face and got up. I put him in the downstairs bathroom, the inside one, no window worth the name, and I crouched down and told him we were playing hide-and-seek, and the rule was he didn't come out. No matter what he heard. Even if it sounded like me. *Especially* if it sounded like me, and I heard the words come out of my mouth and knew exactly whose law I was reciting. He looked at me with the cap still in his hand and his ears went red. "Like the closet rule," he said. "Like the closet rule." He said it back to me, and he went, and he minded me. It's about the only thing in this whole story that worked the way it was supposed to. Then the doorbell rang, which was decent of them, considering.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of pulp fiction writing that demonstrates sophisticated control of voice, pacing, and character. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Narration** The retrospective first-person voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Ruth's narration is genuinely distinctive: flat, dry, self-undermining, and profane in a working rather than performative way. Lines like "I checked the street because I'd been checking streets for years and couldn't stop, the way you keep an accent you can't hear anymore. It wasn't vigilance. It was a tic" demonstrate real craft — the metaphor earns its place, it illuminates character, and it refuses the easy heroic reading. The narrator actively dismantles her own legend in real time, which is exactly what the plan called for and what makes the voice feel lived-in rather than constructed. The self-aware meta-commentary ("You can go ahead and feel superior about that. I did, for years") walks a fine line between clever and too-clever, and mostly stays on the right side. The narrator's admission that she misread the phone call as evidence of a girlfriend — "I stood there with all my supposed instincts, ten years of learning to read men like Frank, and I thought the man was planning a date" — is a genuinely good beat. It humanizes her, establishes her flaw (contempt leading to misreading), and advances the plot economically. **Character Work** Danny is rendered beautifully in a short space. The horror-movie jurisprudence is not just charming — it's load-bearing. The "closet rule" becomes the chapter's structural spine, paying off when Ruth uses Danny's own law to hide him. This is elegant plotting. Danny's characterization matches the profile precisely: observant, rules-oriented, not precociously wise but genuinely sharp in a child's way. His ears going red when he gets serious is a small, specific physical detail that does real work. Frank's brief appearance is efficiently damning: "the tone of a man reciting a line from a play he'd never attended" is a strong line. The phone call fragment — "the house is empty, yeah, nobody" — and Ruth's misreading of it is the chapter's central dramatic irony, handled with restraint. **The Closet Rule Payoff** The structural decision to have Ruth recite Danny's own horror-movie law back to him when hiding him is the chapter's best moment. It's thematically resonant (the rules Danny loves become real), emotionally affecting (the child recognizes it immediately), and it's earned rather than imposed. "I heard the words come out of my mouth and knew exactly whose law I was reciting" — this is the kind of sentence that justifies the whole enterprise. **Pacing and Plot** The chapter establishes threat, character, and situation with economy. The sedan detail is planted and acknowledged ("I mention that because it matters and because I didn't, at the time") — this is the retrospective narrator doing exactly what the plan intended: admitting gaps and failures without mystery-box coyness. The ending on "Then the doorbell rang, which was decent of them, considering" is a perfect chapter-closing line: dark humor, understatement, and forward momentum. **Potential Weaknesses** The "wasp" simile ("I noticed it the way you notice a wasp — not scared, just aware") is functional but not particularly fresh. The description of Mrs. Abernathy watching the street "the way other people watch television, and with about the same critical standards" is a slightly overworked simile — it's trying a bit hard for wit. These are minor complaints. The chapter is perhaps slightly front-loaded with voice-establishment before the plot kicks in, but given that voice is the story's primary engine, this is defensible. The line "Frank owed somebody. Frank always owed somebody; I'd worked for the man long enough to know the flavor of his nervous" — "the flavor of his nervous" is a slightly strained construction, though it fits the voice. **Dialogue** The dialogue is excellent. Danny's horror-movie arguments feel genuinely childlike without being cloying. The closet rule exchange is crisp and purposeful. Frank's single line is perfectly calibrated. Nothing feels written-for-effect in a hollow way. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter executes the plan with high fidelity: the opening line delivered as promised, the retrospective voice established, Ruth's self-deprecation and contempt-flaw demonstrated, Danny's character rendered through the horror-movie law, the closet rule as structural device, and the chapter ending on a flat, dark-humorous note. The plan's intention to "de-mythologize" Ruth is present throughout. **Overall Assessment** This is genuinely strong literary pulp fiction — it achieves the tonal balance the plan aimed for, the voice is consistent and distinctive, the character work is efficient and specific, and the structural payoff of the closet rule is genuinely satisfying. It's not quite a masterpiece — a few metaphors strain slightly, and the chapter is primarily setup — but it's well above average work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Model Output:
# Chapter Two They came in polite. That's a detail I've never seen in a movie — the bell, and then a young voice through the door asking if Frank's home, doing a bit about a car breakdown and a dead battery, and it's so ordinary it almost works. Almost is doing a lot of lifting in that sentence, because people like me don't open doors after ten, and people like the Delgados didn't have anything a stranger would come calling about. Three of them. I got that from the shadows on the porch through the sidelight — one at the door doing the talking, two hanging back off the light, and the hang-back is how you know. Nobody who's actually lost stands in the dark on purpose. I want to tell you I had a plan. What I had was a kitchen and about four seconds, and the ten years in between didn't leave me much to work with. No gun — that was the program, and I'd kept it, and standing there in Frank Delgado's kitchen I had time to think one clear thought about that, which was: *tonight, Ruth, the program's just going to have to carry you.* I had a knife block and a dish towel and a baby gate on the stairs that weighed about fifteen pounds. I took the long knife and I stood to the hinge side of the door, and I said Frank wasn't home and could I take a message, and the talking man said sure, and then the door came in off its latch because he'd had a shoulder on it the whole time and my voice had just told him where I was. The first one through was a tall kid, raw-boned, cracking his neck, and he was talking before he was all the way inside, which I'd learn was just what he did — he talked the way other men sweat, it came out of him whether he wanted it to or not. "We're not here for you, lady, we're not — where's the kid, it's a collections thing, nobody's supposed to get—" The second one came in behind him and the first one never finished the sentence, because I put the knife into the meat of the second one's shoulder, high, where it pins the arm, and I did it fast and without any anger, the way you'd break down a chicken, and that's a sentence I've never said out loud before tonight, so enjoy it. You want to know what it was like, I can see it. It was like work. There's a reason nobody writes songs about us, and it's that the thing itself is quick and ugly and mostly about footing, and any man who tells you different is selling something. I had four good seconds, and I used them the way you use anything scarce. I got the second man down and the knife back, and the tall one — Boone, his name was Boone, I'd hear it in a minute — Boone grabbed the baby gate off the stairs like it was nothing and swung it. That was the four seconds spent. I got an arm up and the gate took me anyway, edge across the forearm, and the floor came up, and then it was all the bad parts. There's a boot in it, and the corner of the cabinet, and I was down in the space between the island and the fridge, which is where you don't want to be, curled around my own ribs, and I heard the third man pounding up the stairs and I heard Boone yelling no, down, he's *down*, check the doors, and I understood the whole shape of it then, understood it like reading my own handwriting. They didn't want Frank. They wanted Danny. And they knew the layout of the house. The third man found the bathroom in about a minute. It wasn't a mystery — an eight-year-old behind a locked interior door is an eight-year-old behind a locked interior door, and there's only so many doors a house keeps. I heard Danny not make a sound. You have to understand, that kid went quiet the way he promised, and he stayed quiet while they worked the knob, and he stayed quiet through most of what came after, and he was better at the worst night of his life than I have ever been at anything. I got up off the tile.
