We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Serving the prompt, first.** The sentence is an understatey shrug — a person surprised by their own expertise, mid-learning, slightly abashed. So the story must be about *learning, quickly, badly, visibly*: torn sleeves, lost shoes, a down-climb that terrifies her. "One" gets kept, ambidextrous: it's both the impersonal English pronoun and one particular woman. The prompt sentence opens the story as its first line. The story does **not** end with an inversion of it. **Premise (revised).** Clara Danby grew up at Wrentham Hall; her father lost it through a crooked solicitor, Fitch; the new owner is Nicholas Thorne, railway money, awake at night. She breaks in to retrieve things her father left — which are mostly worthless — and her practice improves while her morals drift: from *mine* to *valuable* to *dangerous*. Thorne notices, oils the hasp, plays the game — because he's lonely and because, a little, he enjoys that it pleases him to know. The worst thing she does is also the best thing she does: the false betrothal, which she weaponizes to save herself, and which costs a household servant her position. The ledger is found, the fraud exposed — off-page, no courtroom. Thorne tells Clara the house is a bad investment and she mustn't marry it by marrying him. She makes an honest offer anyway; the refusal is left unread. The story ends on the cost paid and the door not yet opened. **Characters (revised).** - **Clara:** dry, proud, decent — *less decent than she believes*, which she discovers only after it costs someone else. She is good at climbing and still visibly bad at it: good at walls, terrible at landings and descent. Her grief is unprocessed, and *angry* — her father's disgrace, as much as his death. The chapter-one humiliation that drives her to climb is not suffered nobly; she flinches from it all story. Edmund exists as a handhold she calls "Edmund's grip" and the trousers; his death is never explained on-page. - **Thorne:** attentive, yes — but his noticing is also *possession-adjacent*. He buys things. He bought her house. The game pleases him partly because she is a secret in a house he owns, and he knows the exact hour of her comings before he tells her anything. He is funnier than she expects, which disarms her and should slightly disarm the reader. His complicity in Fitch is real: he suspected for months and did nothing because the ledgers were so beautifully kept. - **Fitch:** once genuinely liked Clara's father; the first forgery was one forgery, a bridge-loan that failed; thirty years of being trapped inside his own accounts. When caught, he apologizes to Clara for her father — sincerely, uselessly. Never twirls, never threatens her person; the man-trap is housekeeping, not murder. - **Mrs. Bell:** decent, careful, not "secretly on Clara's side." She covers for the "ghost" because a servant who reports ghosts gets replaced. Her dismissal is the story's irreversible wound; Fitch deflects suspicion onto her; Clara cannot confess without destroying the case and lets her go with money and a letter instead of the truth. - **Phoebe:** underused, a daylight thread; not a rescue-device. - **Fifth character, new: Constable Unwin,** a former Danby footman now village constable — wryly believes in ghosts, advises Clara on ghosts, and knows exactly what she is doing and has silently chosen not to know. He must have very few scenes. - **Aunt Dorothea:** deaf, so heavy sleepers — played once, never milked. **Voice & style.** Close third on Clara, present-tense-dry, modern-readable with period texture, no dialect. One ruling discipline: **bannings.** Ban: the sentence "both were window-people" and all paraphrases of it; door/window statements of theme; any epigraph that accurately predicts its chapter; more than three uses of the word "wren" in the text; any milking of Edmund (his name may appear in dialogue zero times, in narration twice, max); any scene where someone explains their feelings. Thorne's interiority never enters the narration — acts and notes only. **The manual, revised.** Epigraphs stay but are demoted from a device to a voice: excerpts from a real failing book, *Practical Hints on Window Entry* by "A Late Resident," which Thorne's grandmother apparently wrote and which is mostly wrong. Rules are bad advice the chapter quietly disproves ("Rule the First: Never trust ivy. Ivy is a liar" — followed by a chapter in which the ivy is the only thing that holds). One chapter has no epigraph (Ch. 5). The last is a torn fragment with no rule on it at all. This inverts the pattern-matching: the epigraph becomes unreliable, so it stops being a spoiler and starts being a joke at the manual's — and the plan's — expense. **Wren, demoted.** The name Wrentham stays (the prompt made the house). The carved newel-post wren exists and *contains nothing*. "The wren keeps what the crows would steal" is the father's note, but the interpretation is wrong twice: first the newel wren (empty), then the study's wren paperweight (just a paperweight). The ledger is in the **cut pages of *Tristram Shandy*** — because Clara's father hid the one thing that mattered inside the thing that made him laugh, and because nobody in that house had ever opened a book. It is found by accident, by Phoebe, looking for a fan. This is the plan's biggest risk and its biggest humanization: the puzzle dissolves into life. **Falling.** Chapters 1–8 each contain a fall or a near-fall of some kind — physical, social, moral. This is the quiet structure, never announced, replacing the explicit "every chapter ends on a turn" spec with something less mechanical. **Aims:** the prompt's shrug; comedy that bruises; competence undercut by terror; romance conducted in acts, not speeches; one irreversible cost; an ending with a door unopened. **Avoids:** the bow; the thesis line; mirrored wounds stated aloud; fair-play puzzle elegance; rescue plotting; courtroom mechanics; the Jane Eyre shadow (no attic, no madwoman, no gothic weather; the house is mundane — the ghost is a rumor, the horror is Fitch's handwriting); eight identical chapters (Ch. 5 is deliberately flat and short-feeling; Ch. 3 is mostly a social scene). **Ending, fixed:** No door-line. The final image is Clara at the back of Mrs. Bell's carrier cart, watching Wrentham's front door recede — the door she has still never entered. One earlier line is allowed to land at the very end, quietly, in dialogue or not at all: *"The door will keep."* Unresolved, un-opened, un-thesised. # Chapter Planning **Ch. 1 — "The Pear Tree."** *Epigraph (mock-manual): "On entering a house: it is the same as leaving it, backwards."* Open on the prompt sentence, literally: "One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. Clara learned it in a fortnight, and badly." Then the humiliation that drives her: a village tea where the rector's wife returns, *as charity*, a box of Danby things Fitch sold her — including Clara's mother's thimble, handled in front of everyone. Clara's pride cracks in public; she is pitied, and the pity is the wound. That night, first climb — pear tree, drainpipe, her old bedroom now a lumber room. She is visibly bad: rips her sleeve, loses a shoe in the pear tree, nearly cries on the drainpipe not from fear but from being *furious* (her father's disgrace, not his death). She retrieves from the baseboard only the portrait miniature and a folded note: *"The wren keeps what the crows would steal."* She has no idea what it means. Descending, she is seen — a lamp, a man at the dark library window, very still. She gets down by falling the last six feet into the laurels, losing the shoe a second time, and limping home. **Fall:** literal, off the wall. **Cost:** the shoe, left behind in the laurels. **Ch. 2 — "The Oiled Hasp."** *Epigraph: "A lit window is an eye. Climb the blind ones." (The chapter will disprove this: the blind ones are where the traps are.)* Daylight first — the village; Aunt Dorothea asleep mid-sentence (played once, gently); Clara buys boot-blacking and, on a mad impulse, a penny notebook, because the note needs thinking about and she thinks on paper now. Night: second climb, better planned, worse executed. The study window hasp has been **oiled**. The newel wren is there; she pries it — nothing inside. She finds her father's old desk, and in it nothing but a tradesman's bill she steals anyway (drift: worthless, but *his*). At the library door she hears breathing, stops, and does the thing she is worst at: she waits. The man inside is asleep over a drawing of a bridge. She watches him too long — long enough that the reader understands this is not reconnaissance — then leaves, and on the sill finds, tucked under the hasp, her **lost shoe, cleaned**. **Fall:** social — she is no longer a trespasser but a *guest*, and she didn't agree to be one. **Turn:** she doesn't take the shoe. She leaves it on the sill and climbs down without it, barefoot in her stockings, which is the first thing she's done that's actually graceful. **Ch. 3 — "The Wrong Wren."** *Epigraph: "Always know your room. A stranger's bedroom is the commonest catastrophe." (Disproved: the catastrophe is her own old bedroom.)* Mostly a social chapter, deliberately. A village at-home; Thorne attends, with Phoebe, and is clearly there to find out who the climber is — he has the shoe. Clara is introduced to him. He is funnier than she can afford, and his attention is a physical thing, like being measured. He says, of Wrentham, "I bought it for the bookshelves," and she understands that he has never opened a book in it. She hates him cleanly and is drawn to him uncleanly, and the chapter lets both be true without resolving either. That night she climbs to the study and breaks the wren paperweight — her second wrong wren — and it is only a paperweight, and she sits on the floor of her father's study and does not cry, which is worse. Fitch is mentioned for the first time on-page: he was at the at-home, and he said her father's name gently, and she nearly broke. **Fall:** emotional — the wrong wren, the floor of the study. **Cost:** she leaves the broken paperweight; she has started breaking things. **Ch. 4 — "The Threshold."** *Epigraph: "If you are discovered, be a ghost. Everyone believes in ghosts." (Disproved: Thorne does not believe in ghosts.)* The midpoint conversation — but wrong-footed, not consummated. She climbs; the study lamp is lit; Thorne is *in her father's chair*, waiting, with the cleaned shoe on the desk beside him. He is not angry. That is the problem. He is interested, and his interest is a kind of enjoyment, and she can see that he knows exactly the hour of her comings and has for a fortnight. They talk — briefly, badly, honestly in patches. He does not offer to help. She does not ask. What passes between them is not alliance but *recognition of the game*, and under it the thing neither says: he owns the room they're standing in. She leaves by the door — he lets her — which is the first time she's used one, and it feels like a defeat, not a kindness. **Fall:** she used the door. **Turn:** on the step he says, "There's a constable coming Friday. Fitch's idea. I'd use the window." He is warning her — but he is also choosing her over his own solicitor, and he hasn't decided why yet, and she knows it. **Ch. 5 — "The Quiet Chapter."** *No epigraph.* Deliberately flat, deliberately short-feeling. No climb. Rain for three days. Clara's hands are torn; her aunt notices and says nothing, which is its own sentence. She thinks about the note. She thinks about the shoe. Phoebe calls, and in the drawing-room, reaching for a fan, knocks over a fat old volume of *Tristram Shandy* — it falls open *hollow*, cut out, and inside is a folded packet: **the ledger**. Clara's heart stops; she cannot take it; Phoebe, chattering, closes it and puts it back on the shelf, and Clara spends the rest of the chapter making conversation about nothing while her father's proof sits four feet away in a book no one will ever open again. It is the cruelest and funniest chapter. **Fall:** none — and that's the fall. The quest is solved by accident, in daylight, by a girl looking for a fan, and Clara can't touch it. **Cost:** she now knows where it is and can't go back for it without climbing again, and she's sworn off, and the swearing-off lasts four days. **Ch. 6 — "The Man-Trap."** *Epigraph: "A fall from the first floor kills one in six. A fall from the second kills four in six. The upper windows are therefore safest." (The manual's logic, exactly as bad as it sounds.)* She climbs for the book. This time the trap is real: Fitch, suspecting the "ghost," has set a man-trap inside the study hearth — legal at night, indoors, technically, which is the whole horror of it. She comes down the chimney-breast side, her old route, and the trap takes her skirt and **not** her leg — Edmund's grip, the wrong route, saves her by inches. She screams once, short. Thorne comes; the constable comes; it is Fitch's constable and Fitch's trap, and Clara is caught, in her dead brother's trousers, in her father's study, with a packet in her hand. And here she does the worst and best thing: she looks at Thorne and says, steadily, "Tell them I'm your betrothed." It is not a proposal. It is a weapon. Thorne — and this is his moral hinge — *hesitates*, and in the hesitation she sees him decide that possessing this, even falsely, is worth lying for, and she sees him see himself, and then he says, "She's my betrothed." **Fall:** literal (the trap) and moral (the lie, hers; the hesitation, his). **Cost:** Fitch, cornered, does the arithmetic of servants — and Mrs. Bell, who knew about the ghost, is dismissed at dawn for the breach in his house's security. **Ch. 7 — "The Ledger."** *Epigraph: a torn fragment, no rule, just the words "—the upper windows, which are—"* The aftermath, deliberately un-climactic. The ledger is real and damning; the fraud is exposed **off-page** — a lawyer comes from London; Fitch apologizes to Clara, sincerely and uselessly, for her father; he is taken away in a fly, not a police van, and Clara watches him go and feels *nothing*, which frightens her more than the trap did. No courtroom. Thorne avoids her — his complicity is now plain to her: he suspected Fitch for months and did nothing because the ledgers were beautifully kept. Clara goes to Mrs. Bell's cottage with money and a letter of recommendation and cannot say *it was me, and I let you take it* — she lets Mrs. Bell believe the household story, and Mrs. Bell, who is not a fool, lets her. It is the story's unhealed wound and neither of them mentions it. **Fall:** Clara's, and it's the one that matters — she paid a debt with someone else's money. **Cost:** Mrs. Bell's position, gone, not restored. There is no scene where it comes back. **Ch. 8 — "The Door Will Keep."** *Epigraph: none — or the manual's last page, blank.* The denouement, refusing the bow. Autumn. The money is being restored — slowly, in the way of these things. Thorne calls on Clara, properly, at her aunt's cottage, through the front door, and it is awkward and he is bad at it and that helps. He tells her two things, plainly: that Wrentham is a bad investment he never wanted, and that she must not marry it by marrying him — that he has been possessed of things before, and she is not a thing, and he will not be the door she walks through to get her house back. It is the most honest speech in the story and it is also, she realizes, a proposal declined before it was made, which is either the proudest or the most cowardly thing a man has ever done in front of her. She does not answer it. The story ends not with her answer but earlier in the day: she has ridden out to Mrs. Bell's new situation, twenty miles, to bring her the one thing from the lumber room that was hers — and coming home in the carrier's cart, she passes Wrentham, and looks at the front door she has still, after everything, never entered. **Final line, not the thesis:** *"The door will keep," Clara said, to no one; and the cart went on.* **Fall:** none — the first chapter without one. **Open:** the door un-opened, the offer un-answered, the debt to Mrs. Bell un-paid. The prompt's "one" has learned the art; what she hasn't learned yet is whether to want the door.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Clara Danby Twenty-eight, which in this village is a verdict, not an age. She grew up at Wrentham Hall as the younger child, the girl, the afterthought — Edmund was the heir and the joy, and Clara was the one who followed him up walls because it was the only way to stay in the room. Her father, Henry Danby, was a gentle, unworldly man who loved bad jokes and *Tristram Shandy* and trusted the wrong people with the right documents. He died four years ago, six months after the fall, of what Clara privately calls *the shame* — not apoplexy, not a decline, *shame* — and she is angrier at him for being disgraced than she is at Fitch for disgracing him. This is the wound she will not look at. She has spent four years being the daughter of a ruined man: first as a sort of poor relation, now as companion to her deaf aunt Dorothea, which means she has spent four years being *good* — patient, quiet, grateful, invisible — and the goodness has curdled in her like milk left out. The climbing is the first selfish thing she has done since the fall, and it frightens her how much she needs it. She is intelligent in a specific, unshowy way: she notices systems — how a house is run, how a conversation is steered, how a man's attention moves around a room — and she is usually the cleverest person present, which she has learned to conceal because a clever poor relation is an unemployed one. Her wit is dry and mostly silent; the things she doesn't say are funnier than the things she does, and this has made her, over four years, a slightly *dangerous* person — someone who is laughing at you, kindly, from very far away. She is brave in the way of people who have already lost the thing they were afraid of losing: she is not afraid of heights, or gossip, or poverty, or even Fitch, particularly. What she is afraid of is being pitied, and of wanting things, and of her own anger, and of the possibility that she is not actually good, that the goodness was just powerlessness with better manners. The story proves this fear correct, and she survives it, which is the point. Her great flaw is that she treats her own life as a debt to be serviced — to her father's memory, to her aunt, to some idea of the Danbys — and so she cannot let herself *want* anything outright. She can only want things sideways, through duty: she doesn't break into Wrentham to get *her* things back, she breaks in to get *her father's* things back, and it takes her eight chapters to admit the difference is a fiction. She is also, and she would be appalled to know it, a little bit of a snob: she has the genteel poor's contempt for the vulgar rich, and her first clean hatred of Thorne is not because he bought her house but because he bought it for the *bookshelves* and has never opened a book. She is wrong about him, and being wrong about him is one of the few pleasures she allows herself. Physically: tall for a woman, which she disguises by a slight, habitual stoop she's had since sixteen — Edmund was tall and she made herself smaller so he'd seem taller, and the habit outlived him. Dark hair, going slightly mousey at the temples, worn in the style of a woman who has stopped expecting to be looked at. Her hands are the tell: strong, scarred now, bitten nails, a climber's hands, always in motion — she is one of those people who is *still* in company and *restless* alone, the reverse of the usual. She is good at walls and bad at landings; she climbs like a person who learned young and never practiced, all instinct and no technique, and she descends badly, with her eyes shut, which is where the falls come from. In company she is watchful and a little too quiet, and when she does speak it's usually one sentence too few, which people read as shyness and is actually editing. Motives and allegiances: to her father's name, absolutely and pathologically; to her aunt, genuinely and resentfully; to Edmund, in the way you are loyal to the dead, which is to say uselessly and completely. She wants the ledger, she tells herself, to clear her father. She actually wants it because it is the only thing left in the world that is *his*, and she wants her house back, and she wants to stop being pitied, and she wants, increasingly and against her will, the man who is awake at two in the morning in her library. She is allegiant, in the end, to a family that no longer exists, and the story is about the slow, costly transfer of that allegiance to the living. Voice: > *The rector's wife held the thimble up to the light, as if checking it for silver, and Clara stood in her best dress and her worst smile and thought: I could come back for the rest. At night. Through the window. The thought was so calm and so complete that it took her a moment to understand it was a plan.* > *"I'm not proud," she told her aunt, who couldn't hear her, which was the only person she ever told the truth.* > *She had been good for four years. Goodness, she was discovering, was a muscle; and hers had wasted; and the climbing hurt like the first day of something.* --- # Nicholas Thorne Thirty-six. Railway money — not the romantic kind, not the tycoon-with-a-top-hat kind; his father was an engineer who patented a coupling, and Nicholas inherited the patent, the works, and the exact social position of a man who is too rich to ignore and too new to like. He has been rich for fifteen years and has never once felt *safe* in it, which is why he buys things: houses, horses, bookshelves, people's time. He is not acquisitive out of greed — he is acquisitive out of a deep, unexamined belief that safety is a thing you can *purchase*, and that if he just owns enough of the world it will stop being able to hurt him. He bought Wrentham to launch Phoebe; he has been in it eight months and has used four rooms. He does not sleep. This is the truest thing about him: he has not slept properly since he was twenty-one and his father died in his arms in the middle of a sentence, and now he works at night, on bridge designs he will never build, because bridges are the opposite of the thing that happened to his father — bridges are a way of saying *you can get from here to there, there is a way across, I have checked the load.* He is a good man, and the story's business is to find the precise edge where his goodness stops. He is honest in his dealings, generous without being asked, kind to servants in the way of a man who was once, briefly, not far from being one. He is also — and he knows this about himself, which makes it worse, not better — a man who *enjoys* knowing things. He is a noticer. He notices the ghost on his stair before the servants do; he notices the exact hour of her comings; he notices that she takes nothing of value and he oils the hasp and he *waits*, and the waiting is the sin, because the waiting is a kind of possession. He could have stopped her in a night. He let it go on for a fortnight because the house was less empty with a secret in it, and he has not yet decided whether that makes him a romantic or a predator, and the honest answer is that it makes him both, a little, like everyone. His great flaw is that he treats intimacy as a problem of *access* — he believes that if he can just get close enough to a person, quietly enough, they will not be able to leave. His father left mid-sentence. He has been trying to finish the sentence ever since, with other people, who keep not being his father. His second flaw, and the one the story punishes, is the beautiful ledgers: Fitch's accounts were so *well-kept* that Nicholas suspected for three months and did nothing, because doing nothing was easier than admitting he'd hired a crook, because the ledgers balanced, because he is an engineer and he trusts a thing that balances. He let Clara's ruin continue for a season because the paperwork was tidy. He has to live with that, and the story makes him. Physically: big, and carries it awkwardly — he is one of those large men who has never learned to be graceful in rooms, only in fields, and so in drawing-rooms he stands a little too still, like furniture that apologizes. Dark, weathered, going grey at the temples in a way that makes women forgive him his origins. He has an engineer's hands, stained faintly with ink and oil no matter how he scrubs, and he is always faintly too warm — he takes his coat off indoors, which the village reads as vulgarity and is actually just that he runs hot. His attention is his most physical attribute: when he looks at you, you feel *surveyed*, measured, load-tested, and it is intensely flattering and faintly alarming, and Clara hates it for exactly those reasons. He is funnier than his size suggests, dry and self-deprecating in the way of men who are actually quite proud and have learned to hide it as a courtesy. Motives and allegiances: to Phoebe, completely — she is the only person he has never tried to possess, and the only one who loves him without being afraid of him. To his dead father, whose unfinished sentence he is still trying to finish. To the idea of *safety*, which he pursues through ownership and which keeps not working. He wants, in order: Phoebe launched; the house to feel like a home; to sleep; and then, increasingly, the woman who climbs his walls, whom he wants in exactly the way he wants everything, which is to say *to keep her*, and the story is about him learning that she is the one thing that cannot be kept by being bought. His final speech — "you must not marry it by marrying me" — is the hardest thing he has ever done, because it is the first time he has ever let go of something he could have held. Voice: > *He had not slept in fifteen years. It was not, he thought, a tragedy; it was a schedule. The night was the only time the house stopped reminding him that he'd bought it.* > *"I know what time you come," he said, and immediately wished he hadn't, because it was the truth, and the truth, he'd noticed, was the thing women ran from.* > *She was the only thing in the house he hadn't paid for. He kept catching himself trying to work out what she'd cost, and then stopping, because he already knew: everything, and nothing, and the nothing was the point.* --- # Mr. Fitch Fifty-eight, and looks it, and has earned it badly. He came to the Danbys thirty years ago as a young solicitor with one client and a talent for accounts, and he was, at first, exactly what he seemed: careful, honest, fond. He *liked* Henry Danby — this is the thing the story must not lose — he liked him the way you like a man who is everything you aren't: gentle, funny, unworldly, kind. The first forgery was not a scheme; it was a bridge-loan, a single one, to cover a client's emergency, and the client failed, and the loan had to be covered, and the covering had to be covered, and thirty years later Fitch is a man who has spent his entire professional life inside a structure of paper he built in one bad week in 1838. He did not set out to ruin Henry Danby. He set out to survive Tuesday, and then Wednesday, and then thirty years of Wednesdays, and Henry Danby was simply the largest, softest, most trusting account on his books, and so Henry Danby's ruin was not a crime of hatred but of *inertia*, which is worse. He is not a villain in any way he would recognize. He is punctual, soft-spoken, kind to clerks, generous at Christmas. He has a horror of scenes, of raised voices, of anything *unseemly* — his whole personality is a wall built to keep the unseemly out, because the unseemly is where the accounts are. He is genuinely, terribly fond of Clara, whom he has known since she was eight, and his apology to her in Chapter 7 is not a performance: it is the first true thing he has said in thirty years, and it is completely useless, and he knows it, and the knowledge has been killing him by inches for decades. He set the man-trap not to hurt anyone — the thought of *hurting* Clara is genuinely unbearable to him — but to *stop* her, to make the ghost be a servant, a poacher, anyone but who he half-knows it is, because if it is who he thinks it is, then the thirty years are up. He is a man trying to hold a door shut against the truth, and the tragedy is that the door is made of paper and he knows it. Physically: small, neat, grey, dried-out — a man who has been pickled in his own caution. He dresses a decade out of fashion, in the manner of someone who stopped expecting to be seen. His hands are inky and tremulous, and he has a habit of washing them — not literally, he is not *that* obvious, but he rubs them together, constantly, a dry washing motion, when he is thinking. He never raises his voice; he gets *quieter* when threatened, so that you have to lean in to hear him, which is a kind of power. He smells faintly of lavender water and fear. Motives and allegiances: to survival, first and only; to the memory of a version of himself that existed before 1838; and, genuinely, to Clara, whom he would save if saving her did not require destroying himself. He is allegiant to the lie, because the lie is load-bearing now — remove it and the whole structure comes down on everyone, not just him. He does not want to hurt Clara. He wants Clara to *stop*, and the unbearable thing is that he cannot tell her why without confessing, and he cannot confess, and so he sets a trap for her, and hates himself, and does it anyway. Voice: > *"Your father," he said, and stopped, and rubbed his hands together, and Clara watched him do it and thought, with a coldness that surprised her: he's washing them. He's been washing them for thirty years.* > *He had not slept well since 1838. It was a kind of distinction, he supposed; he and Mr. Thorne had that in common, at least.* > *"I'm sorry," he said, and meant it, and it changed nothing, and he had known it would change nothing, and he said it anyway, because it was the only coin he had left.* --- # Mrs. Bell Fifty, housekeeper at Wrentham under three regimes now — old Mr. Danby, then the brief awful interregnum, then Thorne — and she has survived all of them by the same principle: *she sees everything and says nothing and the house runs*. She is not warm. This is important — the LLM temptation is to make her a secret ally, a twinkling accomplice, and she is not that. She is a professional. She has been in service since she was thirteen, and she has the servant's absolute, unsentimental clarity about the family upstairs: they are the weather. You don't hate the weather. You *dress* for it. She liked the Danbys as one likes a mild winter; she finds Thorne an improvement because he pays on time and doesn't drink; she covers for the ghost not out of loyalty to Clara but because a housekeeper who reports ghosts is a housekeeper who gets *replaced*, and Mrs. Bell does not get replaced. Her great quality is that she is *not a fool*, and her great tragedy is that being not-a-fool is no protection at all. She knows within a week that the ghost is Clara Danby — she recognized the footprint in the flowerbed, a woman's print, small, with the heel turned out, and she remembered Miss Clara had always walked like that, toe-out, like a duck, bless her. She says nothing. She oils nothing, leaves nothing, helps *no one* — she simply declines to see, which is the maximum degree of complicity her professionalism allows. And it doesn't matter. When the trap is sprung and the betrothal is announced and Fitch needs a body to explain the breach, it is Mrs. Bell who goes, because she is the most expendable person who knew the most, and her dismissal is the story's cold, correct injustice: the one person who did *exactly the right amount of wrong* is the one who pays. Physically: a woman built like the house she runs — solid, upright, no nonsense. Iron-grey hair screwed back so tight it pulls her face into a permanent expression of mild skepticism. She has a housekeeper's walk, silent and purposeful, and a housekeeper's hands, red and capable, and she carries a ring of keys she never, ever puts down. She looks at everyone — masters, servants, ghosts — with the same flat, assessing gaze, a woman checking for dust. Motives and allegiances: to the house, first — Wrentham itself, the actual building, which she has kept running through three owners and which she loves more than any of them. To her own position, second, which is not greed but the rational self-interest of a woman with no other capital. To Clara, a distant third, in the form of a memory of a duck-footed little girl she was briefly fond of. She is not on anyone's side. She is on the side of *the house standing*, and the house stands, and she is dismissed anyway, and that is the story's coldest sentence. Voice: > *She'd seen the footprint Tuesday. Small, turned out, a woman's. She'd raked the bed smooth before the gardener saw it, and thought no more about it. Houses had ghosts. You didn't poke them.* > *"I've kept this house for thirty years," she said, not as a boast, as a fact, the way you'd say water's wet.* > *She took the money Miss Clara brought, and the letter, and she looked at the girl's face — all that guilt, sitting on it like a hat — and she thought: she wants me to forgive her. Well. She can want.* --- # Phoebe Thorne Nineteen, and the only entirely uncorrupted person in the story, which the story must handle *carefully*, because uncorrupted people are boring. Phoebe is not boring because she is not stupid — she is exactly as intelligent as Clara, and entirely unarmored, so her intelligence goes *outward*, into the world, in a continuous, unedited stream. She talks. She talks constantly, brilliantly, about whatever is in front of her — the fan, the book, the weather, the unspeakable dullness of the village, her brother's tragic elbows — and her chatter is not vacuousness, it is *generosity*: she cannot bear a silence, because a silence might mean someone is uncomfortable, and so she fills every silence with the first thing that comes to hand, and the first thing that comes to hand, one fatal afternoon, is a fat old volume of *Tristram Shandy*. She is the story's accident, its dea ex machina, its pure uncalculating engine: she finds the ledger because she has no guile, and she gives it to Clara without knowing what it is, and her guilelessness is the moral mirror the story holds up to everyone else's maneuvering. She adores her brother with the clear-eyed adoration of a much younger sibling — she thinks he is a great sad elephant of a man, tragic and ridiculous and *hers* — and she is the only person who is not afraid of him, and the only one who can make him laugh. She knows, by Chapter 6, that something is happening between him and the climber, and she handles it the way she handles everything: by talking about it, endlessly, wrongly, to the wrong people, and somehow, in the talking, making it come true. Physically: small, quick, birdlike — she is the actual wren of the story, though the story should never say so. Dark like her brother but without his weathering; pretty in a way that will be beautiful in five years and doesn't know it yet. She cannot sit still; she perches, she fidgets, she knocks things over, and her hands are always reaching for the next object — the fan, the book — and it is this, her beautiful fatal restlessness, that solves the plot. Motives and allegiances: to her brother, completely; to *life*, which she finds endlessly, exhaustingly interesting; to Clara, instantly and with no justification, because Phoebe collects interesting people the way her brother collects objects. She wants to be launched, but only because Nicholas wants it; left alone she would live in a cottage with six dogs and be perfectly happy, and the story's sad secret is that everyone is maneuvering for a future Phoebe doesn't actually want. Voice: > *"You're the one who climbs in the window," she said, delighted, the moment they were alone. "Nicholas thinks he's being subtle. He leaves your shoe on the sill like a great tragic slipper. It's *agonizing*, I have to watch it, I can't look away—"* > *She knocked the book off the shelf reaching for the fan, and it fell open hollow, and Phoebe said "oh, *sneaky*" with the simple admiration of a girl who had just learned the world contained secret compartments.* --- # Constable Unwin Forty-five, village constable, and — this is the detail that matters — *formerly the Danbys' footman*, twenty years ago, before the fall, before everything. He is the story's one walking reminder that Wrentham had a before. He is a big, slow, weathered man, deliberately so — he has cultivated slowness the way other men cultivate authority, because a slow constable is a constable nobody bothers to lie to. He is not clever, but he is *deep*, and he has the old servant's absolute loyalty to the Danby name, transmuted into a constable's absolute discretion: he knows exactly what Clara is doing from the first broken flowerbed, and he chooses, silently, completely, to not-know it. His scenes must be very few — two, maybe three — and each one must do the work of a whole subplot: he advises Clara on ghosts, with a perfectly straight face, as a way of telling her he knows; he is *not* the constable who comes on the night of the trap (that's Fitch's man, brought in from the next parish, precisely because Unwin can't be trusted to catch her); and he is the one who, in the end, does not arrest anyone, because the arrest happens off-page, in London, where constables like Unwin don't go. He is the story's quiet conscience, the man who decided twenty years ago whose side he was on and has never had to say so out loud. Physically: enormous, gone comfortably to seed, with a footman's ramrod posture slowly collapsing into a grandfather's stoop. He has a great walrus mustache and kind, shrewd, heavy-lidded eyes, and he moves with the exaggerated care of a big man in a small village, as if the whole place were a parlor full of breakable things. Motives and allegiances: to the Danbys, forever; to the village, which he keeps the way Mrs. Bell keeps the house; to a private, twenty-year-old idea of what is right that has nothing to do with the law he nominally serves. He is the story's one uncomplicated allegiance, and the plan must resist the temptation to give him a scene where he explains himself. He never explains himself. Voice: > *"Ghosts," he said, scratching his mustache, looking at the flowerbed with the small turned-out print in it. "No law against 'em. I'd leave 'em be, miss. They mostly go away on their own." He touched his hat, and left, and did not come back.* > *He'd carried Miss Clara on his shoulder once, when she was five and the harvest was in. Some things you didn't forget, and some things you didn't say, and in his experience they were the same things.* --- # Aunt Dorothea Seventy-one, Clara's late father's elder sister, deaf as a post and sharp as a tack, and the story must treat her deafness as a *fact*, not a device — she is not "the deaf aunt who lets Clara climb," she is a woman who has been deaf for thirty years and has built an entire rich, silent, slightly tyrannical inner life around it. She reads lips badly and pretends to read them perfectly, which means she lives in a state of continuous, serene misunderstanding, answering questions no one asked and ignoring ones they did. She is not sweet. She is impatient, opinionated, and entirely self-absorbed in the way of old women who have stopped performing, and Clara's four years of caring for her have been four years of being talked *at* by a woman who cannot hear the answers. Her one great scene — and she should only get one — is the moment in Chapter 5 when she looks at Clara's torn hands, and Clara braces for a question, and Dorothea simply turns her head a quarter-inch, the tiniest, most deliberate act of not-seeing in the story, and says nothing, and goes back to her knitting. It is the only kindness she performs in eight chapters, and it costs her nothing, and it is perfect. Physically: tiny, erect, draped in shawls, with a face like a disapproving hawk and hands that are never, ever still — knitting, always knitting, garments for people who died years ago. She moves through the cottage like a small, deaf storm, knocking things over, entirely unaware of the noise she makes. Motives and allegiances: to her own comfort; to the past; to Clara, in a fierce, inarticulate, entirely undemonstrative way that Clara will not recognize as love until long after the story ends. Voice: > *"You're out at night," she said, not looking up from her knitting. Clara froze. "I can tell," said her aunt, to the silence, to herself. "You come in with your stockings wet. A girl with wet stockings is up to no good or up to something, and you're my brother's daughter, so." She turned the heel of the sock. "Lock the door when you come in. There's no sense letting the no-good in after you."*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One — The Pear Tree *On entering a house: it is the same as leaving it, backwards.* — *Practical Hints on Window Entry*, by A Late Resident One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. Clara learned it in a fortnight, and badly, and the first thing she learned was that she had no choice in the matter, because the choice had been made for her on a Tuesday afternoon in the rector's drawing room, over seed cake. Mrs. Wren — that was her name, and Clara had once found it funny — set the box on the table between them with the particular gentleness of a woman performing charity and *knowing* it showed. "We've had it in the attic since the sale," she said. "And I thought — well. Some things ought to come home." The box held eleven things. Clara counted them, because counting was something to do with her face. A hairbrush with half its bristles gone. A psalter with her grandmother's name in it. Three napkin rings. A doorstop in the shape of a pug. And, at the bottom, wrapped in a scrap of flannel, her mother's thimble, which Mrs. Wren lifted out and held up to the light as though checking it for silver. "Mr. Fitch let me have the lot for a song at the auction," she said. "I always meant to return the little things. The *personal* little things." The room was full. That was the architecture of the cruelty — not the thimble itself, but the Misses Hepworth by the window, and old Mrs. Judson with her ear trumpet angled like a cannon, all of them watching Clara Danby receive her own mother's thimble back as an act of mercy. Clara took it. She said thank you. She said it quite steadily, and she heard how steady it was, and she hated Mrs. Wren for making her steady, and hated her father for dying disgraced and leaving her to be steady in drawing rooms, and then hated herself for the second hatred, which was the larger one. She walked home with the box on her hip, past the lane that led up to Wrentham Hall, and did not let herself look at it. She had stopped looking at it four years ago. It was a discipline, like not scratching. Her aunt was asleep in her chair by the fire, knitting on her lap, mouth open, deaf to the door and the world. Clara set the box down. She looked at the thimble. And the thought arrived whole and calm and fully dressed, like a guest who has been waiting in the hall: *I could go back for the rest. At night. Through the window.* It took her a moment to understand it was a plan. --- She went that night, because a plan deferred is a plan reasoned with, and she did not trust her reasons. She wore Edmund's trousers. She had kept them for no reason she could name — they had come home in a trunk of his things from school, years before the fall — and they fit her closely enough if she rolled the cuffs, and climbing in a skirt was how girls in novels died. Edmund had taught her the route himself, one summer when she was twelve and he was fifteen and their parents were at dinner: the pear tree against the kitchen garden wall, the branch to the scullery roof, the scullery roof to the drainpipe, the drainpipe to her own bedroom window, which had a loose hasp she'd never told anyone about because every child needs one thing that is hers. *That's Edmund's grip*, he used to say, of a knot in the drainpipe's bracket where your right foot went. *Miss that and you're dead.* The pear tree was older. She was older. These facts argued with each other for some time. The branch that had held her at twelve held her at twenty-eight but *commented* on it, a slow creak she felt through her palms, and she hung there for a moment with her cheek against the bark, breathing, her heart going like a fiddler's elbow, and thought: *four years of being good, and this is what the goodness was storing up.* The scullery roof was slick with moss. She crossed it on all fours, slowly, and her sleeve caught on the gutter's edge and tore — a clean rip from elbow to shoulder, astonishingly loud in the dark, and she froze over the sleeping house like a stain. Nothing stirred. The house stood black against the stars, her house, the only roof that had ever meant *home* to her body rather than her address, and someone else was asleep in it, and the unfairness of that came up through her chest so suddenly and so hard that she had to wait for it to pass before she could reach the drainpipe. Edmund's grip held. Her right foot found the knot by memory, and her body remembered the rest better than her mind did — hands, feet, the rhythm of it — but the drainpipe had not been climbed in four years and had opinions, flakes of rust coming away under her fingers, and she climbed with her eyes half shut, which was her way, and which was also, she knew perfectly well, the way of people who fall. The hasp on her old bedroom window was still loose. It opened like a mouth. Her room was a lumber room now. That undid her more than the climb had. Her bed was gone; her bookshelf was gone; sheeted furniture stood about like a congregation of ghosts, and the wallpaper — sprigged, faded, hers — was peeling at the seam where she had once measured her height against Edmund's in pencil. The marks were still there. Someone had papered the room and sold the house and bought the house and nobody had thought the pencil marks worth scrubbing out, and Clara stood in the middle of it in her dead brother's trousers with her torn sleeve and did not cry, because crying was not what she'd come for, and because she was discovering that grief and rage, at sufficient pressure, were the same substance. She went to the baseboard by the window. The night they were turned out — the *actual* night, the last one — she had hidden her mother's portrait miniature there, prised up the board with a nail scissors while her father sat downstairs signing things. It was the only useful thing she had done that night, and she had never told anyone, and some part of her had believed, insanely, for four years, that it had probably been found, been sold, been gone. The board lifted. The miniature was there. And under it, folded into a square the size of a postage stamp, was a scrap of paper that had not been there when she was twelve. Her father's hand. She would have known it upside down and underwater. *The wren keeps what the crows would steal.* She read it four times. It meant nothing to her. It was his joke-writing, his riddle-writing, the hand of a man annotating a sermon he wasn't listening to — and that was all she had time to think, because there was light in the corridor. Not passing. *Moving.* A lamp, low, unhurried, and beneath the door the shadow of it slid along the floorboards and stopped, and Clara stopped breathing with the particular completeness of a rabbit under a hedge. Someone was awake in her house at two o'clock in the morning. Someone was *walking about* in it. The shadow moved on. She counted to a hundred, then to a hundred again because the first hundred had gone too fast to be honest, and then she was out the window with the miniature inside her shirt and the paper in her fist, and down the drainpipe, eyes shut, the rust coming away in her palms. She was three feet from the scullery roof when she looked back. She didn't mean to. It was the last thing she meant. A man stood at the library window, dark, very still, holding a lamp at his side so that the light fell down and not on his face. He was not moving. He was not calling out. He was simply standing at the window of her father's library at two in the morning, looking out into the garden where she was, and whether he could see her or only the shaking of the ivy, she would never know, because she did not stay to find out. She went down the last of it the fast way, which is to say she fell — six feet, off the drainpipe, into the laurels, tearing her other sleeve and losing her left shoe somewhere in the branches. She lay in the laurels with the wind knocked out of her, staring up at the lit window, and the man did not appear in it, and the lamp did not move, and after a moment she understood that he was letting her go, or had seen nothing at all, and she could not decide which of those was worse. She limped home barefoot on one side, the miniature against her ribs, the paper in her fist, and her aunt did not wake, and the house did not wake, and only the shoe stayed behind, in the laurels, as evidence that any of it had happened at all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of opening chapter writing. Let me work through its qualities and failings systematically. **Voice and Style** The prose voice is the chapter's greatest strength. It achieves something genuinely difficult: a period-appropriate texture without pastiche, a dry wit without archness, and emotional weight without sentimentality. The line "counting was something to do with her face" is excellent — it shows Clara's self-management under social pressure without explaining it. "The architecture of the cruelty" is a strong phrase that earns its abstraction because it's immediately grounded in the specific geometry of the room: the Misses Hepworth, the ear trumpet angled like a cannon. These details do real work. The rhythm of the prose is controlled. Sentences vary in length with purpose — the short declarative "The box held eleven things" followed by the inventory, then the longer, more tangled sentences when Clara's emotions become more complex. This is craft, not accident. **Character Execution** Clara is rendered with precision. The plan called for her to be "dry, proud, decent — less decent than she believes," and the chapter delivers this: her hatred of her father for dying disgraced, immediately followed by self-recrimination for that hatred, is exactly the right psychological texture. The detail of Edmund's trousers, the pencil height marks on the wallpaper, the baseboard hiding place — these accumulate into a character who is defined by what she has lost and how she has organized her grief into practical action. The plan specified that Edmund's name may appear in narration twice maximum, and the chapter uses it twice (the trousers introduction and "Edmund's grip"), which is disciplined. The grip detail — "Miss that and you're dead" — is doing double work as both practical climbing instruction and elegiac echo, and it lands without being milked. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The opening sentence is deployed exactly as specified. The humiliation at the tea is rendered with the thimble held up to the light (matching the plan's "handled in front of everyone"). The climb includes the torn sleeve, the lost shoe, the near-cry that is actually fury. The note is found. The man at the library window is present. The fall into the laurels happens. The shoe is left behind. Every beat is hit. The epigraph ("On entering a house: it is the same as leaving it, backwards") is appropriately oblique — it doesn't spoil the chapter, and the chapter doesn't straightforwardly disprove it either, which is correct for Chapter 1 (the disproof pattern is established later). **Weaknesses and Concerns** The phrase "grief and rage, at sufficient pressure, were the same substance" is the chapter's most exposed moment — it's the closest thing to a thesis statement, and the plan explicitly bans scenes where someone explains their feelings. This is Clara explaining her own feelings to the reader, and while it's not egregious, it's the one place where the prose tells rather than shows. The chapter has otherwise been so good at showing that this stands out. "Her heart going like a fiddler's elbow" is a slightly strained simile — it's reaching for period color and lands awkwardly. A fiddler's elbow is not a particularly vivid image of rapid heartbeat; it's more about sawing motion than speed. "Like a guest who has been waiting in the hall" — the thought arriving "whole and calm and fully dressed" — is a good metaphor that earns its elaboration. The man at the window is handled with appropriate restraint. The chapter resists explaining him, which is correct. His stillness and the ambiguity of whether he saw her is well-managed. **Dialogue** There is very little dialogue, and what exists is functional and period-appropriate. Mrs. Wren's lines are exactly right — the particular gentleness of performed charity. The aunt is not present in this chapter. Edmund's voice exists only in reported speech ("That's Edmund's grip"), which is the right choice. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is genuinely moving without being manipulative. The pencil marks on the wallpaper is the chapter's emotional peak, and it's earned by the accumulation of specific detail before it. The restraint — "did not cry, because crying was not what she'd come for" — is exactly right and consistent with the character profile. **Plot** The plot is simple and clear: humiliation drives Clara to climb; she retrieves the miniature and finds the note; she is seen. This is sufficient for a first chapter. The note ("The wren keeps what the crows would steal") is planted without fanfare, which is correct — it should mean nothing yet. **Overall Assessment** This is a high-quality piece of literary fiction writing. It executes a complex plan with discipline, renders a nuanced protagonist in a single chapter, and maintains a consistent, accomplished voice throughout. Its failings are minor: one moment of telling-not-showing, one slightly strained simile. It is not a masterpiece — it is a very good opening chapter that does its job with skill and restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
