Like watching a till do sums. "Well," he said. "Well. This is — I like it here. This is nice." "You've been here twenty seconds." "Good twenty seconds." He looked around, up into the dark of the gallery, the rose window running with streetlamp and rain. "Is this a chapel? It's a chapel. Somebody made a chapel into a — that's fantastic. Whose idea was that?" "My grandmother's." "She a genius?" "She's dead." "God, I'm sorry, I—" He stopped. Recalibrated. "That's a very good straight line and I've stepped in it. Sorry." The rain hammered. Outside, the voice had gone quiet, which Elin didn't trust for a second. She picked up the card machine off the counter and put it in the drawer, then picked it up again because the drawer was the wrong drawer. "You can stop doing that," she said. "Doing what?" "The thing with the face. You've done it four times. It hasn't improved with repetition." He blinked. And then, for a second, something happened that she would think about later, in the shower, against her will: he dropped it. The whole apparatus just — stopped, like an engine cutting, and what was left was a wet tired man about her brother's age with grey at his temples and ink-coloured eyes, standing in a bookshop in Porthdwyn at six o'clock on a Friday. "Christ, that's a relief," he said, and the voice had changed underneath — flatter vowels, something south and east in it, valleys. "Can I just—" the polish came back halfway through the sentence, she heard him catch it, heard him let it go, "—stand here. A minute. I'm not asking for anything. I just want to be somewhere nobody's pointing anything at me." Elin looked at the door. The shadow of a head was still in the glass. "He's still there," she said. "He's paid to be." "He'll get wet." "He's waterproof. They're bred waterproof." She should send him out. She'd sent out a man with a sleeping bag once, and a man with no shoes, and she'd felt terrible both times and done it anyway, because the shop was the shop and six o'clock was six o'clock. The till was counted. *Un, dau, tri.* "You're from somewhere," she said. He'd been looking at the candles. "Everyone's from somewhere." "Somewhere with those vowels. You've got an accent trying to get out." His hand stopped on a candle. It was only a second — less, maybe — and then the face came back on, all of it, rueful and charming and twelve feet tall. "I've been told I sound very classless. It's a compliment in my line. Means you can be cast as anything." "Pontypool," she said. She didn't know where it came from. She'd had a auntie in Sebastopol. "Cwmbran," he said. "Same difference." "Christ, don't say that in either." And then, quieter, like a man confessing to something: "Pontypool." The shadow moved off the glass. They both watched it go, down towards the harbour, hunched. "He'll be in The Griffin," Elin said. "Warm in there. Rhodri'll have him bought and sold by eight." "The Griffin." He said it carefully. "There's a pub. Called The Griffin." "It's older than you." "Everyone keeps telling me that about everything lately." She almost smiled. She didn't; she had a rule about rewarding them, and then she felt stupid for having a rule at all, standing in her own shop in the half-dark with a wet film star and eleven unsold candles. The sentences came out longer than she meant. "Right, well — he'll wait, though, that's the thing, they wait, my brother had one outside his office in Swansea for a week over a VAT thing, not that — you're not VAT — he'll be there at seven in the morning with a flask, and it's a small street, so if you've a car coming you'll want—" She stopped. Breathed. "There's a back way." "There is?" "Through the vestry. Comes out on Crown Lane." "The vestry," he said, delighted, and the face did the thing again, and this time she let it pass, because he was pointing it at the building and not at her. "But you're not going like that," she said, and took a book off the new-in table and held it out. "Twenty percent off. Thursdays." He took it. Read the cover. *Selected Poems*, R. S. Thomas. "Why this?" "Because you need a reason to have been in here, and I'm not selling you a candle." She rang it up with one hand. "And because you ought to read something that isn't a script, I imagine. And it's nine pounds and we've had eleven candles and one book all day, so." He paid. He was quiet a moment, turning the book over, and she had the sudden, unwelcome thought that nobody gave him things to *do*, that his whole life was people taking things away from him to be dealt with. "Elin," he said. He'd read her badge. He said it right, first go. "You've been—" and he stopped, and she watched him decline, visibly, to say something charming. "Thanks." "Back door," she said. "Mind the boiler." She locked up after him, and the shop was quiet, and the rain was only rain. She put the R. S. Thomas display straight. It was already straight. She put it straight again, and looked at her hands like they belonged to somebody else.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished piece of creative writing that demonstrates rare command of craft. Let me work through it systematically. **Nuanced Characters** Both characters arrive fully formed and distinct. Griffin's charm-as-reflex is rendered with precision — the "tiny recalculation behind his eyes, the smile reseating itself a degree dimmer" when Elin refuses to perform recognition. The plan called for the ego to need feeding, and we see it: he watches for the flicker, he does "a whole bit" that gets pre-empted, he's "pleased with himself, like a boy who'd found the right wardrobe." But crucially, the plan also called for genuine warmth, and that's here too — the laugh that "arrived before the joke deserved it," the moment he drops the apparatus entirely. Elin's precision-as-defence is equally well-executed: the rule about not rewarding them, the self-disgust at having the rule, the sentences getting longer when rattled (the Swansea brother speech is perfect), the hands re-shelving what's already straight. **Emotionally Engaging** The chapter earns its emotional weight through restraint. The shower-thought she'll have against her will, the hands that "belonged to somebody else" — these land because they're not announced. The Pontypool exchange is the chapter's best moment: "Pontypool," he said, like a man confessing to something. That's a complete emotional arc in eight words. The reader feels the chemistry without being told to. **Compelling Plot** For a first chapter that is essentially one scene in a closed space, the plot is as compelling as it needs to be. The paparazzo siege creates genuine stakes, the back-door resolution is satisfying, and the R.S. Thomas gambit (selling him a book to make him leave) is both plot-functional and character-revealing. The chapter doesn't overstay. **Coherent** Entirely coherent. The physical space is clear, the timeline is clear, the emotional logic is clear. No false notes in the sequencing. **Weak Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. Each character has a genuinely distinct voice. Griffin's "I read it as come in. Common mistake" establishes him immediately. Elin's "You've done it four times. It hasn't improved with repetition" is exactly the voice described in the profile — dry, precise, ending conversations. The Pontypool/Cwmbran exchange is beautifully handled: "Same difference." / "Christ, don't say that in either." The dialogue does not over-explain, does not editorialize, and the silences are load-bearing as planned. One minor quibble: "You've been—" and the visible declining of the charming thing is slightly over-telegraphed, but it's a small fault. **Tell-Don't-Show** Almost entirely show. The one borderline moment is "she had the sudden, unwelcome thought that nobody gave him things to do, that his whole life was people taking things away from him to be dealt with" — this is interior observation rather than pure showing, but it's Elin's perception, not authorial intrusion, and it's earned by the moment. The plan called for close third on Elin, so this is appropriate. **Unsurprising or Uncreative** The chapter consistently subverts expectations. The "I know who you are" repeated flat is a perfect deflation of the expected recognition beat. The R.S. Thomas as exit strategy is unexpected and character-true. The Pontypool confession arrives without setup and lands hard. The ending — hands re-shelving what's already straight — is the right image and not the obvious one. **Amateurish** Nothing amateurish here. The prose is controlled throughout. The pacing is confident. There are no clichés that aren't earned, no sentiment that isn't undercut. **Purple Prose** The prose is clean and precise. "The rain had been going since Tuesday and it was Friday now" is a perfect opening sentence — functional, rhythmic, characterful. "The rain was only rain" at the end is the right kind of restraint. No purple passages. **Forced Poetry or Metaphor** The metaphors that appear are earned: "like watching a till do sums" for the recalculation behind his eyes is fresh and character-appropriate (she's a shopkeeper). "Like a man holding the sea out with his shoulder blades" is slightly more literary but not strained. "Like a boy who'd found the right wardrobe" is precise. None feel imposed. **Well-earned Lightness or Darkness** The lightness is consistently earned. The comedy of the paparazzo, the Pontypool exchange, the candles — all land because they're grounded in character. The darker undertone (his exhaustion, her loneliness, the shop's precarity) is present without being announced. **Characters Consistent with Profile** Extremely faithful. Griffin's laugh arriving "before the joke deserved it," his standing in ways that add an inch, his noticing the light — all from the profile. Elin's counting in Welsh, her sentences getting longer when rattled, her hands as the tell, the rule about rewarding them — all present and correctly deployed. The "Pontypool" confession matches the profile's note about it being a class wound. **Followed Chapter Plan** The chapter follows the plan with impressive fidelity: the filthy evening, Elin cashing up, the door crashing in, the siege forcing proximity, the charm offensive with some dying and some landing, the buried accent moment ("You've got an accent trying to get out"), the R.S. Thomas sale, the back-door exit, the coda of re-shelving with annoyed hands. The plan's instruction to ration banter so charged moments register is followed. The plan's note that "the bell and the rain are a bell and rain" is honored — no over-symbolism. **Faithful to Writing Prompt** Fully faithful. Wales setting (Pembrokeshire coast, Welsh counting, Welsh pub name), famous actor ducking into bookshop to escape paparazzi, conversation charged with unspoken chemistry, actor as charming rogue with fragile ego, bookstore owner superficially immune but inwardly flustered, primarily in dialogue with distinct voices. The prompt's specific beats — the charm offensive, the immunity that provokes genuine attraction, the derailing of the charm — are all present. **Summary** This is a genuinely excellent piece of writing. The craft is consistent, the voices are distinct and alive, the restraint is disciplined, and the emotional intelligence is high. The chapter does exactly what a first chapter should: establishes character, creates chemistry, and ends with the reader wanting more — without any of the machinery showing. Minor deductions only for one slightly over-telegraphed interior moment and one metaphor ("holding the sea out with his shoulder blades") that reaches slightly further than the prose's general register. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 19 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two: The Back Way Mair came over on Saturday morning with the bara brith that hadn't sold, which was most of it, and set it on the counter like evidence. "Well," she said. "Well what." "Rhodri had your photographer in The Griffin till eleven. Kept him in pints, got the lot. They're up at the castle eight weeks, it's the dragon one, and the wife's done an interview." She unwrapped a loaf and cut herself a slice with the knife Elin kept for string. "Also a man was seen leaving your shop by the back way on Friday, wet through, carrying poetry." "Crown Lane. Someone watches Crown Lane now." "Everyone watches Crown Lane. It's where the recycling goes." Mair chewed. "So?" "So nothing. He came in out of the rain. I sold him a book. He went." "Which book?" "Does it matter which book?" "It always matters which book." Mair pointed the knife at her. "You're telling it wrong, anyway. You're telling it like a witness statement. What was he like?" "Wet." "Elin." "Tallish. Wet. Chatty." Elin took the cling film off the tray they used for window display and rewrapped the till float, which did not need rewrapping. "He had a good jacket. Leather. Ruined by the rain, you'd think, although I'm no expert, and there was a sort of — he does a thing with his face—" "There it is," Mair said. "What." "You've told me what his coat was like twice." "I mentioned it once." "*Good jacket* and then *leather*. That's twice. You never tell me what anybody's wearing." "You never ask what anybody's wearing." "I'm asking now." Elin gave her the look that had ended conversations in three counties. Mair ate her slice, unhurried. She was the only person in Porthdwyn immune to the look, and she knew it, and it was the foundation of their friendship. "They're saying he's difficult," Mair said. "She doesn't say it in the interview, but you can tell she's saying it. *Sources close to.*" She said it like a curse, which it was. "I've a niece with him on her wall. I'll not have her disappointed." That night, in the flat, Elin looked him up. She did it standing at the counter eating toast, as if standing made it not count. The coverage was ugly in the modern way, all of it written by people who had never met anyone involved. *GRIN AND BEAR IT: why everyone's Team Immy.* A source close to the actress Imogen Dacre described life with Griffin Wye as "like living with a mirror that needs feeding." There was a photo of him at an awards thing, jaw first, and a list of his films with marks out of ten, and an old one of him young in a play she'd never heard of, looking like a different and poorer man. She shut the laptop. She opened it again to check the name of the play. She shut it again and went to bed feeling like she'd been through someone's bins. He came back on Monday, which was absurd. She knew it was absurd because the bell made her look up with her whole face before she could arrange it. Different jacket. The same jacket. Its brother. "It's faulty," he said, and laid the R. S. Thomas on the counter. "It's poetry. It's all faulty." "I read it. Some of it. I read the last one first — habit. Scripts. You check it lands before you learn it." "You read the last poem first?" "You've gone a colour." She had a whole position on reading the ends of things. She read the first fifty pages of everything and trusted them, like a handshake; he read endings, like counting the change before you'd paid. "Which was the last one, then?" A pause. "The field one." "They're all field ones. He's famous for it." "The bright one, then." He was caught, and knew it, and stood there in it, grinning, not reaching for anything. "All right. I read four. And I came back because—" he looked around, at the gallery, the light coming down through the rose window in coloured pieces, "—I don't know. I was driving past." "Your castle's forty minutes up the coast." "Long detour," he agreed. The bell went and Mair came in with the weather still on her shoulders, stopped dead, and said "Oh," in a way that carried. Elin watched him turn it on. That was the thing she'd think about later: she could *see* it, the whole warm apparatus swinging round like a lamp on a ship, and it worked anyway, that was the obscenity of it. The hand out. "Mair. Is that Welsh for Mary?" "It's Welsh for Mair, thanks." He laughed — the too-loud one, the whole face. He took a slice of bara brith when it was offered and said it was the best thing he'd eaten in Wales, and Mair went like a switched torch, gone, utterly, visibly. Elin stood behind her own till with a feeling she identified, after a moment's honest search, as irritation, and underneath the irritation something pettier she declined to identify at all. "The bakery's over the road, Mair," she said. "I know where it is. I work there." Then the drip started, into Glenys's mixing bowl, where it always started when the rain came round off the sea. *Plink.* He looked up. "Your window's leaking." "It does that." He crossed the shop and stood under the rose window with his head back, and the light came through the glass onto his face in red and blue pieces, and for once he didn't seem to know where the light was. "That's not the window," he said. "Window's just where it shows. That's the roof. Roof's had it." "We have a man." "What did the man say?" "Eighteen thousand," Elin said, and it was out before she'd decided to let it. "I could—" "No." He looked at her. "You don't know what I was going to say." "Yes I do." The shop went quiet but for the drip. Mair stood holding the bara brith like a woman at the tennis. "This is better than telly," said Mair. "Right," he said. "Right." As if being told no was a flavour he hadn't had in a while. He put his hands in the brother jacket's pockets. "Books are twenty percent off Thursdays." "They are." "Thursday, then." He went. The bell swung and settled. "Eighteen thousand," said Mair. "Don't." "I'm saying nothing. I'm a professional." "You're a gossip." "I'm a gossip pretending to be a professional. There's a difference. I read it in a book." Elin picked up the R. S. Thomas and put it back on the new-in table, face out. Then she turned it spine out, because face out was doing too much.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of creative writing that demonstrates genuine craft across nearly every dimension. Let me work through it carefully. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. Both characters speak in genuinely distinct registers. Elin's voice is exactly as specified: dry, precise, deflecting with economy. "Wet." as a complete answer to "What was he like?" is perfect — it's funny, it's defensive, and it's a lie by omission. Griffin's "The bright one, then" when caught not knowing the poem title is beautifully observed: he's caught, knows it, and doesn't reach for a recovery, which is itself a kind of charm. The Mair scenes crackle — "This is better than telly" lands without being too cute. The dialogue is load-bearing throughout; it does character work, plot work, and tonal work simultaneously. The exchange about the jacket — Mair catching Elin saying "good jacket" and then "leather" — is a small masterpiece of indirect characterization. It shows Elin's fluster through Mair's observation rather than through Elin's interiority, which is exactly right. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan with impressive fidelity while not feeling mechanical. The plan called for: Mair's dissection (present, sharp, Mair "sharper than Elin would like"), Elin googling him and hating herself (the "bins" line is perfect — "like she'd been through someone's bins"), the return visit with a transparent pretext, the charm working on Mair irritating Elin disproportionately, the roof revelation, and the refusal of his offer. All present. The plan's instruction to let the chapter "run quiet" is honored — there's no forced topper at the end, just Elin turning the book spine-out, which is the right kind of small. **Character Consistency** Elin's physical tells are deployed correctly: she rewraps the till float (handling objects when rattled), the blush is implied rather than stated, her sentences don't get longer here but her deflections do. The detail about her reading first pages and his reading last pages is a genuinely good character collision — it's not over-explained, just placed and left. Griffin's laugh ("too-loud one, the whole face") is consistent with his profile. His "Right. Right." when refused — "as if being told no was a flavour he hadn't had in a while" — is one of the chapter's best lines, doing exactly what the profile demands. **Showing vs. Telling** The chapter is almost entirely show. The one moment that edges toward tell is "she identified, after a moment's honest search, as irritation, and underneath the irritation something pettier she declined to identify at all" — but this is close third person doing exactly what close third person should do, and the self-deception is the point. It's not a failure; it's a technique. **Metaphor and Prose** The prose is controlled. "The coverage was ugly in the modern way" is a good line. "Mair went like a switched torch, gone, utterly, visibly" is excellent — the three adverbs after "gone" are doing real work, each one adding something. The light through the rose window onto Griffin's face is handled with restraint: "for once he didn't seem to know where the light was" — this pays off the profile detail about him always knowing where the light is, and it's not over-explained. The bara brith as "evidence" in the opening is a small, good touch. There are no forced metaphors, no purple passages. The prose is clean and purposeful throughout. **Weaknesses** The chapter is nearly without significant flaw, which makes criticism difficult but also raises the question of whether it's playing it slightly safe. The plan called for Elin to be "cold past the point of wit at least once" — this doesn't quite happen here; her coldness is always witty. The "eighteen thousand" revelation is slightly convenient as a plot device, though it's handled naturally enough. The ending — spine-out rather than face-out — is good but perhaps the most "writerly" gesture in the piece, the one moment where the author's hand is most visible. The Mair character is very well-drawn for a supporting role, perhaps slightly too well-drawn — she threatens to steal scenes from the principals. **Overall** This is professional-quality literary fiction. It honors its plan, its characters, its prompt, and its own aesthetic commitments. The dialogue is genuinely excellent. The restraint is earned. The chemistry between the characters is conveyed through behavior and deflection rather than declaration. The Welsh setting is present without being performed. This is the kind of chapter that makes you want to read the next one. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: Pretexts He came on the Thursday, and then again the Tuesday after, and then on a day that wasn't either, and she stopped keeping count because keeping count was its own confession. Rhodri put a sign in The Griffin's window: *AS SEEN ON TELLY — THE GRIFFIN WELCOMES GRIFFIN*. "It's good for the whole street," he told Elin, meeting her eyes like a man challenging her to say otherwise, and she didn't, because it was true, and that was the irritating thing about Rhodri. The customers split three ways. Some came over faint and had to be handed paper bags. One woman from Cardigan told him, to his face, in the queue, that the last film was the worst two hours of her life and she'd brought her mother for her birthday. "And your mother?" "She cried at the horse." "Everyone cries at the horse," he said, signing the woman's copy of the tie-in paperback, which she held out before he'd finished asking. "The horse does the best work." And old Mr Vaughan came in for his crossword book and found a film star by the till and addressed him in Welsh, the normal Porthdwyn way, half-greeting, half-weather. Griffin smiled. It was a lovely smile. "Sorry — English, if you would. I'm out of practice." Mr Vaughan nodded and said it all again in English, no worse, but Elin saw it land. Saw Griffin stand there in the shape of a man who'd been offered something and fumbled it. It sat in the shop a moment, unexplained. Mr Vaughan paid in coins and went, and Griffin rang up the next customer — he did that now, he just *did* things — and nobody mentioned it. "You understood him," Elin said, later, when the shop was empty. "Some." "You could have answered." "It's been twenty years of it going. You don't know what that's like. It goes like—" he moved his hand, water through fingers. "I used to dream in it. Now it's like hearing the telly through a wall." He said it lightly, like an anecdote, and then didn't do the thing where he checked whether it had landed, which was how she knew it wasn't an anecdote. The roof man came back with a second quote, higher, on account of the first one's optimism. Elin did the figures at the kitchen table over coffee and then did them again to make them say something else. The shop would do till spring. Then it would be a wetter shop. She told Griffin none of this. He arrived with things instead — a tray of Mair's coffee, a horror story about a stunt horse, once a daffodil bulb in a paper bag, no explanation, which she planted in a pot on the vestry step and watered with a fury that surprised her. Then on the Friday she went too far. He'd been telling the room — a room of four — about an awards dinner, names falling out of him like he'd swallowed a magazine, and the light was doing something unkind and he looked, suddenly, exactly his age and twice as hungry. "Do you hear yourself?" she said. "You name people like you're dropping litter." The shop went very quiet. Mrs Rees from the cottages put down a thriller and backed away from it. Griffin went still. Not the charm-still — she'd learned the difference by now; this was the other thing, the cold room under the house. "Careful, Miss Pryce," he said, quietly. "I'm extremely convincing when I want to be liked." A beat. "You don't want to find out how convincing." Mrs Rees left without the thriller. The bell was very loud. Elin should have let it stand. He deserved it standing. But his face had gone somewhere she recognised from mirrors, and she heard herself say, "Well, now I know what you were like in the crime one," and it wasn't enough, it was a joke in a graveyard— "No," he said. "Sorry. That was — Christ. That's not — I don't talk like that. I don't—" He was gripping the counter edge. He did the whole thing with no polish at all, which was the worst of it: he actually seemed to need her to say it was all right, needed it *now*, needed it the way other people need air, and it embarrassed everyone, most of all him. "Tell me it's all right." "It's all right." "You don't mean it." "No," she said. "But I will by six." He laughed — a raw, wrong-sounding version of the laugh. He stayed quiet after that, which she'd never seen him do, and in the quiet, while she priced a box of garden birds, he said, to nobody, to the till: "It's a reflex. The liking thing. Like a knee. You hit it and it goes. I've had it so long I don't know what's—" he stopped. "Twenty years I've been at it. I'm good at it. I'm not sure I ever decided to be." Elin priced garden birds. "Miss Hale," he said. "Drama teacher. Pont-y-pŵl. First applause of my life, I was seventeen, I played a tree with one line. Something in my chest went—" he made a fist, opened it. "I've been chasing it since. There you are. That's the whole diagnosis and I'd like it noted that I offered it voluntarily." "Noted," she said, gently, and then, because gentle felt dangerous: "Fourteen garden birds at two-fifty." "Robbery." "It's a small shop. Somebody has to fund the chandelier." She nodded up at it — Glenys's joke, the one fixture the chapel had never needed. Then she heard herself, and where it was going, and it was too late to stop: "There's an evening you could do. If you were serious about—" she hadn't meant to say it at all. "Reading. In here. We charge at the door. The whole town would come and pretend they hadn't." He looked at her a long moment, and she watched him work through charm, past it, to something underneath, and choose, carefully: "Are you asking for my help, Miss Pryce?" "I'm asking you to read poetry aloud. There's a difference." "Noted," he said. Outside, it had started raining again, the ordinary way, and the drip came on in the mixing bowl, *plink, plink*, steady as a clock that was running out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. It has the "dirt in the engine" the plan called for — exchanges that don't resolve cleanly, questions that go unanswered, the flat functional lines ("Fourteen garden birds at two-fifty") doing real emotional work. The Welsh language moment with Mr Vaughan is handled with exactly the restraint the plan demanded: "It sat in the shop a moment, unexplained." No one articulates what it means. The subsequent conversation about the language going "like water through fingers" is Griffin's most unguarded moment so far, and the writing earns it by not underlining it. The snap and apology sequence is the chapter's centrepiece and it works. The cold flash ("Careful, Miss Pryce. I'm extremely convincing when I want to be liked") lands with genuine menace, and the immediate collapse into naked need — "Tell me it's all right" / "You don't mean it" — is both psychologically precise and uncomfortable in the right way. The plan called for the apology to be "so desperate it embarrasses everyone, including the reader," and it delivers. Elin's response ("I will by six") is perfectly calibrated: dry, not cruel, a small mercy that costs her something. The Miss Hale monologue is well-handled. Griffin's self-diagnosis is offered "voluntarily" and he notes this, which is itself a performance of vulnerability — the plan's insight that even his genuine moments are contaminated by the ego's need to be credited for them. The fist-opening gesture for the applause is concrete and unforced. The fundraising reading proposal emerges organically from the scene rather than being engineered. Elin's "I hadn't meant to say it at all" is shown through the sentence structure itself — the trailing off, the self-interruption — rather than stated. **Weaknesses and concerns:** The opening paragraph ("she stopped keeping count because keeping count was its own confession") is the chapter's weakest line — it's the kind of polished aphorism that the plan explicitly warned against, a moment where the narrator steps in to do thematic work the scene should do itself. It's not terrible, but it's the one place where the writing reaches for a literary effect rather than earning it. The "cold room under the house" metaphor for Griffin's stillness is slightly strained — it's evocative but the "under the house" extension pushes it past what the moment needs. The closing image — the drip in the mixing bowl, "steady as a clock that was running out" — is the chapter's most overtly symbolic moment. The plan explicitly demoted motifs and warned against engineered endings. This simile does the thematic work too explicitly: the roof's financial precarity, time running out, the relationship's uncertain future. It's not purple, but it's the one moment where the writing tells us what to feel rather than trusting the scene. The woman from Cardigan who says the film was the worst two hours of her life is a nice touch — the plan called for "at least one customer finds Griffin tedious and says so" — and the exchange about the horse is economical and funny. However, the horse callback ("Everyone cries at the horse") feels slightly too neat, a callback that ties up too cleanly. **Character consistency:** Both characters are entirely consistent with their profiles. Griffin's name-dropping followed by visible regret, the cold flash, the naked apology, the Miss Hale confession — all precisely calibrated to the profile. Elin's "gentle felt dangerous" is a perfect interior beat, and her retreat into "Fourteen garden birds at two-fifty" after a moment of genuine warmth is exactly right. The daffodil bulb "watered with a fury that surprised her" is a lovely small detail. **Plan adherence:** The chapter follows its plan closely: the Welsh language fumble, the ego snap and desperate apology, the quiet after with something true said by accident, the fundraising reading proposal emerging from both of them pretending it's only about the roof. The plan said "let this chapter run quiet; don't force a topper ending" — the ending is not quite a topper but the mixing bowl simile does reach for resonance in a way that slightly violates the spirit of that instruction. **Overall:** This is high-quality literary fiction with a strong command of dialogue, character, and restraint. Its flaws are minor and mostly involve the occasional reach for a poetic effect that the plan itself warned against. The chapter does what it sets out to do with skill and genuine feeling. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four: The Reading Mair did posters, Rhodri did a barrel in the vestry and called it culture, and by seven the chapel was fuller than Elin had ever seen it, including the christening. Sixty people. She'd never sold sixty tickets to anything because she'd never charged for anything, and now they came in shaking umbrellas and shyness, Mr Vaughan and the Cardigan woman and Mrs Rees — returned for the thriller she'd abandoned, bought it at the door like a toll — and half the sixth form, and three young women from the caravan site who screamed, once, when he came down the vestry stairs, and then apologised to the room for it. He was good. Elin had prepared herself for competent, for famous, for an evening she'd enjoy *as a fundraiser*. She had not prepared for this. He read the way the shop deserved and no one had ever given it — poems she'd stocked for years, out of stubbornness, at two copies a decade, and he stood under the rose window in its one dry hour and gave them back at sixty copies a minute. No microphone. He knew exactly how the chapel carried; of course he did; he was built for rooms, and this one rose to him like bread. "You know this one," he told the room. "Your man wrote it up the road." And he read Thomas in English and said the poet's name carefully, the Welsh way, and old Mr Vaughan sat forward in his pew — in Glenys's pew — and Elin had to look at the till drawer for a bit. At the interval he took questions instead of wine, and answered the sixth form properly, seriously, about drama school and being scared, and one of the caravan girls asked about the horse and he did not do the horse line, she noticed; he said the horse's name was Biscuit and that Biscuit had a better agent than he did, and the room went, warm and huge. It worked on everyone. It worked on Mair, who stood at the back with her arms folded pretending to run security, torch-bright. It worked, Elin realised, because he meant it. That was the unbearable part. Cynicism she could have watched all evening. This was worse. She stood behind her till with the door money in a biscuit tin and felt, with complete clarity, that she had built this evening like a bonfire and then complained about the heat. She looked away at the wrong moment — at Mrs Rees, about nothing — and when she looked back he was mid-poem and watching her over the heads of the room, no change in his voice, nothing anyone else would catch, and the line was about a field going luminous while the man's back was turned. The biscuit tin lid went down on her thumb. Afterwards, in the vestry, with the barrel sounds and Mair's laugh coming through the wall, the room suddenly very small. "Thumb," he said. "It's a professional injury. Books are sharp. It's a war." He'd taken her hand before she finished — forearm first, then hand, charm-tech or not, she couldn't tell, maybe nobody could, that was the thing about him — and turned the thumb over. His own hands were warm. The room went very quiet inside her. "You're shaking," he said. "I'm cold." "It's stifling in here." "Then I'm shaking *and* it's stifling. People contain multitudes. It's a bookshop, there's a whole shelf—" and the sentences were going long again, she could hear them going, and she could not find the brakes, and then she could, because he was looking at her mouth, and the quiet changed, and she understood with her whole body that this was it, this was the moment, it was arriving— His phone lit on the table between them. Face up, for once. *Cass*. The moment didn't shatter. It did something worse: it stepped aside politely and let real life through. "Your publicist," Elin said. "Nobody calls a landline any more. Nobody has—" he looked at the phone like it had betrayed him personally. "She has a sense for it. It's a gift." "You should get it." "She'll ring again." "Then you should definitely get it." He got it. She listened to his side of it while she made tea in Glenys's pot, the good one, the one with the lid that didn't match. *Yes. No. I know. I know it was — Cass. Cass, it was a bookshop reading in Wales, nobody—* A silence. He looked at the ceiling. "What do you mean, already?" Elin put the milk down very carefully. He ended the call and sat with the phone in his hands, and the man from under the rose window was gone and the other one was there, the tired one, the one she'd only seen in glitches. "Photos," he said. "From tonight. They're already—" He stopped. "Somebody in the room. Somebody in this room, tonight, sold—" He laughed, once, no humour anywhere near it. "I asked for one evening. She'd say that's naive. That's what she *says*, she says Griffin, that's naive, like naive is a place I keep summering in." "It might not be—" "It's always somebody. It's a business. There's a rate card." He got up. The vestry was too small for him standing; the whole flat was too small, she'd never noticed before, it had been too small for one woman and now it was grotesque. "You should know. It's going to be ugly for a bit. There's a way it goes, it has phases, like a flu. They'll find the shop. They'll find you." "Me." She said it flat, because flat was the only tool in the box. "What is there to find?" He looked at her then, and the look was not charming at all, it was sorry, genuinely and helplessly sorry, like a man watching a wave come in on somebody else's sandcastle. "Nothing," he said. "That's the problem. They bring their own." The rain came back before the barrel was done. The drip went in the mixing bowl all night, and Elin lay in bed in the room behind and listened to it counting money she didn't have.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of creative writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter's central achievement is the reading scene itself. The prose earns its lyricism here: "he stood under the rose window in its one dry hour and gave them back at sixty copies a minute" is a striking image that works because it's grounded in the specific (two copies a decade vs. sixty copies a minute), and the chapel-as-acoustic-instrument detail ("he knew exactly how the chapel carried; of course he did; he was built for rooms") is precise and revealing of character simultaneously. The bonfire metaphor — "she had built this evening like a bonfire and then complained about the heat" — is one of the chapter's best lines: it's self-aware, character-specific, and earns its figurative weight. The interrupted almost-kiss is handled with real skill. The phone interruption doesn't shatter the moment but "stepped aside politely and let real life through" — this is the kind of writing that justifies the plan's ambition. The chapter correctly identifies that the interruption should feel worse than a dramatic rupture, and it does. Griffin's phone call is excellent. The half-heard conversation, the ceiling-staring, the "naive is a place I keep summering in" — this is his voice, and it's good. The final exchange about "they bring their own" is genuinely chilling and lands the chapter's emotional note without over-explaining. The biscuit tin lid on the thumb is a perfect piece of physical comedy-as-revelation. Elin's long sentences when rattled ("People contain multitudes. It's a bookshop, there's a whole shelf—") are consistent with her profile and feel earned rather than performed. **Weaknesses:** The chapter occasionally tips into telling rather than showing. "It worked on everyone. It worked on Mair... It worked, Elin realised, because he meant it. That was the unbearable part." This is close to editorializing — the narrator is doing work the scene should do. The plan specifically warned against this kind of summing-up, and the chapter partially ignores that warning here. The "luminous field" poem moment — him watching her over the heads of the room while reading a line about a field going luminous while the man's back was turned — is a little too neat, a little too engineered. The plan explicitly banned "mirrored image" moments and "no margin-note confession, no misquote-correction ritual." This doesn't quite violate that ban, but it's in the neighborhood of the kind of thematic tidiness the plan was trying to avoid. The opening paragraph's "including the christening" is a nice touch, but the chapter's opening pages are slightly more expository than the rest — we're being told about the evening rather than placed in it. The chapter finds its feet once we're in the vestry. The "shaking" exchange is good but the "People contain multitudes" line, while consistent with Elin's voice, is slightly too knowing — it risks being a wink at the reader. The plan called for "at least one customer finds Griffin tedious and says so" and "the town reacts unevenly" — this chapter doesn't deliver that friction, though it's not strictly required in this chapter. The plan also called for the almost-kiss to "misfire into awkwardness rather than yearning" — the chapter delivers something closer to yearning interrupted, which is slightly different and slightly less interesting than the plan's intention. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is largely excellent. Griffin's voice is consistent and specific. Elin's voice is consistent. The phone call scene is particularly well-handled. The "thumb" exchange is economical and good. The final exchange is strong. There are no false notes in the dialogue, though there are also no moments of the flat functional lines the plan called for — every exchange is doing emotional work, which slightly over-pressurizes the chapter. **Overall:** This is a high-quality chapter that mostly delivers on its plan, with some minor violations of the plan's own stated principles (the thematic tidiness of the poem moment, some telling-not-showing in the reading scene). The characters are consistent with their profiles. The emotional arc is well-managed. The prose is controlled and mostly earns its figurative moments. A few lines tip into over-engineering, but the chapter's best moments — the bonfire line, the phone call, "they bring their own" — are genuinely excellent. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Five: Mystery Woman The photo was grainy and it was unmistakably her: thumb in mouth, biscuit tin under the other arm, head tipped back to say something, and him looking down at her like the room was on fire and he was warm for the first time in years. *MYSTERY WOMAN: the Welsh bookseller who's tamed the Raven.* They'd found her name by Tuesday. By Wednesday they knew about the candles. Rhodri was in the paper, quoted, *a local publican*. "I said the town was delighted," he told her, on her own doorstep, holding a bag of apples like a hostage's offering. "Which it is." "You sold them a photo, Rhodri." "It was a good photo. You look—" he searched, "—spirited." He shifted the apples. "They gave a price. I'd have been a mug. I'd have been a mug, Elin, it's a business." He looked at his boots. "There's a cut in it for the roof, if that's—" "Take your apples." "It's a *business*, Elin," he said, to the door she'd already shut. They came the way he'd said they would, in phases, like a flu. First the photographers, two of them, damp and bored, photographing the till, the candles, the kettle steam, her hands, her coat on its peg. Then the ones who came in as customers and asked about his favourite section. Then the phone: a woman from a morning show saying *human interest* four times in one sentence until it stopped meaning anything. Elin put the phone down and it rang again before her hand had left it. Mair ran interference with a tray. "Cup of tea while you wait, love? No? Pity." She moved between the photographers and the till like a goalkeeper in a hairnet, and Elin caught her, on the Thursday, enjoying it — just for a second, flushed, eyes bright — and then catching herself and looking at Elin with something close to shame. "Don't," Mair said. "I didn't say anything." "You looked it." "I'm allowed to look things." It was Cass who called, in the end, not him. A voice like a hotel lobby. "Elin, hi — Griffin's asked me to reach out. We're so sorry about the noise, it's a cycle, it genuinely does burn off. The advice is don't engage, don't post, don't correct anything, and let it breathe. Honestly, for the film, it's—" a pause, the smallest recalibration, "—well. It doesn't hurt." "For the film." "I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But I'm also not wrong about the cycle." "I have a shop." "I know," Cass said, and for the first time the voice had a person in it. "That's the bit he's shouting about. If that helps." It didn't, particularly. He came himself on the Sunday, through Crown Lane, hood up like a man in a film about informants, and she let him in and locked the door and they stood in the dark shop not looking at each other. "Sixty tickets," he said. "It was a good night." "It was a good night." "The barrel helped. Rhodri—" "Rhodri sold them the photo." He shut his eyes. "Of course he did." "He offered me a cut. For the roof." She heard her own voice, how level it was keeping, the levelness a wall she'd been laying brick by brick all week. "I said no. In case you were wondering what kind of week it's been." "I can make it stop. There's a statement we could—" "A statement." She turned round at that. "A statement from who, exactly? From us? Is there an *us* in your machine? I'd love to know what I've been booked as." "Don't." "It's a fair question. I've had a week to think of questions. Was it a good night, or was it content? Because I priced it as a good night and I've been re-shelved." "It was a good night," he said, and the polish was gone, he was just a man in a hood in a dark shop. "It's the only good night I've—" and then, instead of that, something worse came out of him, she watched it come out and watched him let it: "You knew what I was. You knew the first night. You read it on the side of a bus." The drip went in the bowl. *Plink.* "That's unfair," she said. "Is it." "It's *also true*," she said, and her voice went up for the first time, cracked open on it, "which is the worst kind of unfair, and you know it, you know exactly which kind you're being—" "I'm being followed home, Elin." Flat. Tired. "I don't have kinds. I have one speed and it's this." "You have about five hundred speeds. I've been watching them all month. That one's the coward one." He looked at her for a long moment. Somewhere outside, a car went through the ford too fast. "They've called the reshoots forward," he said at last. "Press week. I fly tomorrow. It's three weeks, maybe—" "Fine." "I'll fix this." "Fine." "I mean it. I'll—" he stopped, and she watched him decline, visibly, to promise something he didn't understand how to deliver. He just stood there with his hood up and his hands in the brother jacket, and then he said, very quietly, in a voice with the Gwent all through it, "I don't know how to leave places without an exit line. There's always an exit line. You've no idea how strange this is." "Goodbye, Gareth," she said. He flinched like she'd thrown something. Then he nodded, once, like a man accepting a fair price, and went out through the vestry. She stood in the dark shop for a while. The bell over the door didn't ring, because he hadn't used the door, and somehow that was the loneliest thing of all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of creative writing that demonstrates sophisticated control over tone, dialogue, and character interiority. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Nuanced Characters** Both characters are rendered with genuine complexity. Griffin's "coward one" speed, his inability to leave without an exit line, and the devastating moment where he declines to promise something he doesn't understand how to deliver — these are precise psychological observations. Elin's "levelness a wall she'd been laying brick by brick all week" is shown through behavior rather than stated. The Rhodri scene opens the chapter with a minor character who is neither villain nor ally but something more uncomfortable: a man who did a mercenary thing and knows it and offers apples. Mair's moment of being caught enjoying the drama, then ashamed — "Don't. / I didn't say anything. / You looked it." — is economical and true. The characters are not performing their complexity; they inhabit it. **Emotionally Engaging** The chapter builds to a genuine emotional rupture. The exchange "You knew what I was" / "It's also true, which is the worst kind of unfair" is the chapter's emotional core, and it earns its weight because both positions are defensible. The final image — the bell not ringing because he used the vestry door — is genuinely affecting without being engineered. "Goodbye, Gareth" landing like a thrown object is the chapter's best moment: it uses the established detail of his real name as a weapon, and his flinch "like a man accepting a fair price" is exactly right. **Compelling Plot** The chapter does what it needs to: it ruptures the relationship through external pressure (the tabloids) and internal pressure (the argument), and it does so without melodrama. The Cass phone call is a smart structural choice — it gives us Griffin's response (he's "shouting about" the shop) without letting him off the hook. The reshoots as the mechanism of departure is clean and unforced. **Coherent** The chapter is tightly structured: the tabloid siege, Rhodri's non-apology, Mair's interference, Cass's call, the Sunday confrontation. Each section flows logically. The timeline (Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Sunday) is clear without being labored. **Weak Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It is genuinely distinct in voice — Rhodri's defensive repetition of "It's a business," Cass's hotel-lobby professionalism cracking slightly, Griffin's "I don't know how to leave places without an exit line." The argument between Elin and Griffin is particularly good: it doesn't resolve cleanly, both characters say things that are true and unfair simultaneously, and the rhythm of the exchange feels like actual speech under pressure rather than scripted sparring. The plan called for "questions go unanswered; interruptions and dead air are load-bearing" and this is delivered: "I'll fix this. / Fine. / I mean it. I'll—" and then the decline. **Tell-Don't-Show** Almost entirely shown. The one slight exception is "the polish was gone, he was just a man in a hood in a dark shop" — this is a mild tell, but it's brief and earns its place as a transition marker. The Mair scene is pure show: we see her enjoyment, her shame, her preemptive "Don't" before Elin can say anything. **Unsurprising or Uncreative** The chapter avoids the obvious beats. The rupture doesn't come from a grand confrontation but from accumulated pressure and one honest, ugly thing Griffin says. The Rhodri non-apology (offering a cut for the roof) is a genuinely surprising and uncomfortable detail. The Cass call is an unexpected structural choice that works. The final image of the bell not ringing is creative without being showy. **Amateurish** Nothing here reads as amateurish. The prose is controlled, the pacing is deliberate, the dialogue is confident. There are no clichés that aren't being used knowingly. **Purple Prose** The prose is lean. "A voice like a hotel lobby" is the most figurative line and it earns its place. The drip in the bowl — "Plink" — is used as punctuation rather than decoration. No purple passages. **Forced Poetry or Metaphor** "The levelness a wall she'd been laying brick by brick all week" is the most extended metaphor and it works because it's consistent with Elin's character (precision, construction, defense). "Like a man accepting a fair price" for Griffin's nod is apt given the commercial language that runs through the chapter (Rhodri's business logic, Cass's film-benefit calculation). The metaphors are earned and sparse. **Well-earned Lightness or Darkness** The darkness is well-earned. The chapter has been building to this rupture across four chapters, and the plan's instruction to let "you knew what I was" be "unfair and also not" is executed precisely. The small moment of Mair's guilty enjoyment provides the only lightness, and it's calibrated correctly — it doesn't undercut the chapter's weight. **Characters Consistent with Profile** Griffin's cold flash ("You knew what I was"), his immediate awareness that it was unfair, his inability to promise what he can't deliver, the Gwent vowels surfacing under stress — all consistent with the profile. Elin's voice cracking on "also true," her brick-by-brick levelness, her use of "Gareth" as a precision weapon — consistent. Rhodri's mercenary logic and guilty apples, Mair's sharp warmth — consistent. **Followed Chapter Plan** The plan called for: the tabloid siege, Mair running interference and being caught enjoying it, Cass's rules relayed secondhand, the quarrel in half-sentences with "you knew what I was," reshoots summoning him, no reconciliation beat, ending on the shop feeling emptied. All of this is delivered. The plan also specified "she accuses him of feeding on it; he can't deny it; then he says something worse and more true" — this is executed with "You knew what I was." The plan's instruction to "resist any summing-up" is honored: the chapter ends on the bell not ringing, not on Elin's feelings about it. **Faithful to Writing Prompt** The prompt established the bookstore, the Welsh setting, the chemistry, the rogue actor with the fragile ego, the immune-but-not-immune bookstore owner. This chapter is a natural and faithful extension of those premises, deepening rather than departing from them. **Minor criticisms:** The "phases, like a flu" simile is slightly generic. "A voice like a hotel lobby" is good but the most obviously "writerly" line in an otherwise restrained chapter. The chapter is perhaps slightly less surprising in its overall arc than in its individual moments — we knew a rupture was coming — but the execution is sufficiently inventive that this is a minor complaint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Six: "She Sells Me Books" Mair brought it to her on the Thursday, phone first, the way you'd carry a dead bird. "You don't have to watch it." "Then why did you bring it?" "Because if you watch it with me I can say things during." Mair set the phone on the kitchen table between the teapot and the butter. "And if I don't bring it, Rhodri shows you at the pub with commentary." It was thirty seconds of it. A red carpet, London rain — even the rain looked expensive — and a presenter with a microphone shaped like a weapon. And him. He looked wonderful, that was the first thing, the thing she'd carry around for days like a stone in a shoe: he looked rested and golden and entirely unbothered, the brother jacket's smarter cousin, the face doing the face. "—and Griffin, everyone's asking — Wales! The bookshop! Is there a mystery woman?" The laugh. The easy one, the one that cost nothing. "There is a bookshop." A pause the size of a heartbeat. "She sells me books." And the wink. And the interviewers laughing, and the rain glittering, and him moving down the line to the next microphone, already somewhere else. Mair's face was a careful, terrible thing. "Well," Elin said. "He's not wrong." "Elin." "It's literally true. It's the only true thing he's said in public in a month, probably. She sells him books." She picked up the phone and handed it back, and her hand was so steady she hated it. "Did he say anything else?" "He did a bit about the horse." "Everyone loves the horse." She did not cry. She noted this with a kind of clerical interest: no tears arrived, nothing dramatic, just a low hum, like a fridge, that she carried through the rest of the day, the rest of the week. She sold books. She said *twenty percent off Thursdays* in a voice a robot could have rented. She was short with Mrs Rees, who had only wanted to know if the murders in the new one happened on page one or later, and then had to ring Mrs Rees at home to say the murders happened on page four and she was sorry about Tuesday. She waited for the call. This was the part she'd told no one, the part that sat under the clip like a second, worse clip: she understood *she sells me books*. It was reflex, it was cowardice, it was the five hundred speeds and he'd picked the wrong one. She could have got over the wink. But the phone didn't ring, and then it didn't ring again, and on the ninth day Mair read out, from somewhere, that the production had gone into lockdown over a leak, everyone's phones sealed, standard practice, nothing to read into — and Elin understood that silence was the message now, and silence had a rate card too. The hum got louder at night. At 1am on the eleventh night she got up and typed his name and then Imogen Dacre's name, because pain apparently wanted the full menu, and found the actress radiant at a fashion thing, saying nothing about anyone, winning. Then she typed her own name, which she'd sworn not to, and there she was: *mystery woman*, thumb in mouth, spirited. A comments section knew everything about her in three hundred words. She shut the laptop and stood in the dark kitchen and the drip went *plink* in the mixing bowl below, counting. The last man she'd liked had checked his reflection in shop windows while holding her hand. That was the whole story; she'd given it one line years ago and it had stayed at one line, a good economical line, the kind she liked. Only now she found the file had a second page. She'd watched herself disappear in those windows, made a joke of it, kept walking. She'd had a rule since: she would not be scenery in somebody's close-up. And she'd broken it herself, she'd built the bonfire, she'd *charged at the door*. On the twelfth day she took his book off the shelf — the R. S. Thomas, the first one, the one he'd returned with the corners folded down like a dog had been at it — and wrapped it in brown paper. Glenys had taught her wrapping: two turns, diagonal corners, string not tape, the post office respects string. Her handwriting went on the label like a seizure. *G. Wye, c/o*— and she stopped. She didn't know the studio's address. She knew it was findable. Everything was findable, that was the whole problem, the whole industry of the thing. She put the parcel under the counter. She'd post it tomorrow. The parcel sat under the counter for three days, brown and square and addressed to a c/o, like a man at a party nobody had invited him to. On the fifteenth day the till was good — the curious were still coming, buying things out of a shame they'd never name, and she'd learned to take the money without looking up, *thank you, mind the step* — and after closing she counted it in Welsh, and got to *deg* and found she was crying after all, quietly, without theatre, the way the roof leaked: just water finding the way through. Mair came at nine with a bottle and didn't ask anything, and they drank it in the vestry with the rain on the little window, and at some point Elin said, "He had five hundred speeds and I miss all of them. Even the coward one. *Especially* the coward one, what is *wrong* with me—" "Nothing's wrong with you." "Something is *deeply* wrong with me." "All right," Mair said, refilling. "Something's deeply wrong with you. But it's not that." Outside, beyond the glass, the street was dark and empty and photographed by no one, and the rain was only rain, and the parcel sat under the counter in the dark, undelivered.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of literary fiction that executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. Let me examine it critically across the relevant dimensions. **Nuanced Characters** Elin is rendered with exceptional precision here. The chapter plan called for her to be "cold to Mair, googles him at 1am" and to snap at a customer — all of which appears, but without the mechanical checklist feeling. The detail of ringing Mrs Rees back to say "the murders happened on page four" is exactly the kind of specific, humanising gesture that makes a character feel real rather than constructed. Her 1am googling of Imogen Dacre ("pain apparently wanted the full menu") is psychologically acute. The "second page" of the file about the last man — the shop windows — is handled with admirable economy; it doesn't overstay its welcome. Griffin is present only through the clip and its aftermath, but his characterisation holds: the wink, the easy laugh, the pivot to the horse. The plan's note that he'd "picked the wrong one of five hundred speeds" is rendered as Elin's own understanding, which is more sophisticated than simply showing him being cowardly. **Emotionally Engaging** The chapter is quietly devastating in the right way. The fridge-hum metaphor for grief is earned. The parcel under the counter — "addressed to a c/o, like a man at a party nobody had invited him to" — is the chapter's best image, genuinely affecting. The ending with Elin crying while counting the till in Welsh, and Mair's refusal to offer false comfort ("All right. Something's deeply wrong with you. But it's not that."), lands with real warmth. The emotional register is controlled throughout; nothing is oversold. **Compelling Plot** This is the quietest chapter by design, and the plan acknowledged it. The plot is essentially: clip arrives, silence follows, Elin processes. There's no dramatic event. The parcel is the closest thing to a plot object, and it sits undelivered — which is the point. For readers invested in the characters, this works; for readers wanting momentum, it may feel thin. The chapter does what it needs to do structurally, but it's the least propulsive of what we can infer about the sequence. **Coherent** Entirely coherent. The timeline (fifteen days, numbered) is handled cleanly without feeling schematic. The transitions between scenes are smooth. The vestry scene with Mair closes the chapter without forcing resolution. **Weak Dialogue** The dialogue is excellent. Mair's voice is distinct — "Because if you watch it with me I can say things during" is perfect characterisation in one line. The exchange about what's wrong with Elin is funny and true simultaneously. The clip dialogue ("She sells me books") is exactly right: minimal, plausibly real, and devastating in context. No line feels written-for-effect in a bad way. **Tell-Don't-Show** There are moments of telling, but they're largely justified as Elin's interior voice, which is the chapter's chosen mode. "She did not cry. She noted this with a kind of clerical interest" is telling, but it's characterful telling — it shows us how Elin processes emotion by describing her processing of it. The "second page" passage is more expository, but it's brief and earns its place. The chapter is primarily showing through specific detail rather than abstract statement. **Unsurprising or Uncreative** The chapter avoids most obvious moves. The clip itself is handled obliquely — we get it through Mair's phone, not a dramatic confrontation. The parcel-under-the-counter is a genuinely fresh image for unresolved feeling. The counting-in-Welsh-and-crying moment is unexpected and culturally specific in a way that feels earned rather than deployed. The "second page" of the file is a clever structural callback to the character profile's "one line" economy. The Mair scene avoids sentimentality through Mair's dry pragmatism. **Amateurish** Nothing here is amateurish. The prose is controlled, the structure is deliberate, the emotional beats are calibrated. The only potential weakness is that the chapter is so quiet it risks feeling like connective tissue rather than a chapter in its own right — but the plan acknowledged this and the execution is skilled enough to make the quietness feel intentional rather than slack. **Purple Prose** Largely absent. "Even the rain looked expensive" is a touch self-conscious but earns its place. "The face doing the face" is good. The parcel metaphor is the most extended image and it works. There's no purple prose in the pejorative sense — no overwrought nature description, no strained lyricism. **Forced Poetry or Metaphor** The fridge-hum metaphor is sustained across the chapter and works. The parcel image is fresh. "Pain apparently wanted the full menu" is slightly cute but not egregiously so. The "stone in a shoe" for the image of him looking wonderful is a little familiar. The roof-leak comparison for crying ("the way the roof leaked: just water finding the way through") is the chapter's most deliberate metaphor — it connects to the building's established symbolism without being heavy-handed. On balance, the metaphors land more than they miss. **Well-earned Lightness or Darkness** The darkness is entirely earned — fifteen days of silence after a public dismissal is genuinely painful, and the chapter doesn't rush past it. The lightness (Mair's "All right, something's deeply wrong with you. But it's not that.") is earned through the relationship established in prior chapters and through the weight of what precedes it. The balance is right. **Characters Consistent with Profile** Elin is exactly as described: the clerical self-observation, the one-line file on the last man, the counting in Welsh, the inability to write the address. Griffin is consistent in absentia — the wink, the easy laugh, the pivot to the horse. Mair is sharp and warm and "secretly enjoying it" in exactly the way the profile suggests. **Followed Chapter Plan** The plan called for: the clip on Mair's phone, the lockdown silence, snapping at a customer, googling at 1am, the one permitted line about the last charming man, the parcel on the counter, ending small with rain and nothing resolved. All of these are present. The plan said "resist any summing-up" — the chapter resists it. The plan said "end small — the parcel sitting there, rain, nothing resolved" — the chapter ends with exactly this, though it adds the Mair scene which enriches rather than undermines the ending. The chapter follows its plan faithfully while not feeling mechanical. **Faithful to Writing Prompt** The prompt established the bookstore, the chemistry, the characters. This chapter is deep in the story's middle, and it's faithful to the established world and characters. The Welsh setting is present (counting in Welsh, the vestry, the rain). The romance's complications are handled with the sophistication the prompt's spirit demands. **Summary** This is a high-quality chapter that executes a difficult brief — a quiet, interior chapter in a romance — with skill and restraint. Its weaknesses are minor: the plot is thin by design, one or two metaphors are slightly familiar, and the "second page" passage is the most expository moment. But the dialogue is excellent, the characterisation is precise, the emotional register is controlled, and the chapter earns its quietness. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good literary fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Seven: Wet Tuesday The shoot wrapped in the third week of October, and Porthdwyn was sorry to see it go. That was the thing nobody had warned her about: the town missed it. The caravanners left and the chip shop cut its hours back; Rhodri took the sign down from the window and put up *CHRISTMAS MENU — BOOK EARLY*, which was a lie of a different vintage; and for a fortnight, everywhere Elin went, people told her stories about the film people with the particular wistfulness of a town describing weather it had enjoyed. Mr Vaughan had shown a grip where the good pigeons were. The sixth form had been extras in mud for a day and would be dining out on it until their own children were extras. Even Mrs Rees said it was quiet now, said it twice, in the tone of a complaint about God. Elin listened to all of it with her face arranged, and counted tills, and sold the curious what they came for, and the curious thinned, week on week, like the tide going out and taking the strange money with it. The roof math happened on a Sunday, at Glenys's table, with Mair holding the calculator because Elin's handwriting extended to numbers. Reading night: eight hundred and forty, less the barrel. The whip-round Mair had run without asking — an envelope left on the counter that said *THE ROOF (NOT THE MAN)* in Mair's writing, containing eleven hundred and some-odd pounds in notes of every age, including, Elin knew with certainty, five twenties that smelled of apples. The curious weeks' tills. The candle money, God help her. It came to within four thousand of the lower quote. "Four thousand," Mair said. "I can get a loan." "You can get *one* loan." "One is all I need. I've only got the one roof." Mair wrote the total on the back of the envelope, underlined it twice, and then said, carefully, in the voice she used for nothing else: "The town did that." "I know." "I'm saying it because you'll not say it to yourself." It was true of the town and it was true of her and the truth sat there like the envelope, smelling faintly of apples. The trade piece found her by accident. She was looking for the weather — a real storm had a name that week — and the name came up in a sidebar, his name, and her hand moved before her head did, the way it had learned to. It was an industry paper, all grey text, no photographs: *The Raven Crown*'s reshoots had run long; sources described tension; Griffin Wye, "long considered one of the franchise's steadiest hands," had declined — *twice*, the piece stressed, with the faint relish of the trade — to participate in any coverage touching his personal life, and had shut down a junket line of questioning in terms that one publicist described as *unhelpfully final*. The word "difficult" appeared once, in quotes, unattributed, doing its quiet work. She read it twice. She told herself it was a weather report from a country she'd never visit: men were difficult or not difficult, franchises were steady or not steady, none of it meant anything to a shop with a four-thousand-pound hole in it. She would not read into it. She bookmarked it. She unbookmarked it. She was aware, with a cold precision she normally turned on other people, that *he's refused to talk about me* was exactly the sort of proof she could not accept — refusal as performance, silence as another of the five hundred speeds, a man could lose a game by being too proud to play and call it principle — and she was furious, actually angry, jaw-tight angry, that he'd left her nothing she was allowed to trust. She shut the laptop. *Plink*, said the bowl, from the dark shop below, counting. She thought about Glenys that night, properly, the way she'd been rationing: her grandmother at the sink in this room, thirty years gone, washing up after some chapel do, saying *nobody warns you the building's the easy part*, and not saying what the hard part was, just drying a plate and looking out at the dark. Two sentences of memory. Elin dried her own plate and looked out at the same dark and did not finish the thought. The storm missed them. It went up the Irish Sea and gave its name to somebody else's week, and Porthdwyn got only the ordinary weather, the wet grey kind, day after day, the kind with no audience at all. Tuesday was the wettest of them. Rain from breakfast, the ford running, the street empty from noon — even Mair's queue was a delivery van. Elin shelved. She did a stock count nobody had asked for. She sold, in the whole long afternoon, one railway novel to a holiday man from Swindon who was staying a fortnight regardless, he said, he liked it atmospheric, and she gave him the twenty percent even though it wasn't Thursday, because he'd earned it. At four o'clock she was on the step-ladder in the gallery with an armful of bird books when the bell went. She didn't turn. "We're—" *open till six, mind the step*, she was going to say, and the words were there, and then something made her turn anyway, the quality of the quiet maybe, the way the door had shut too gently for weather. He was standing just inside, in the little square of worn floor where people always stood while their eyes adjusted, and he was alone. No entourage. No Cass. No hood, even — the rain had him, the hair down and dark, water coming off him onto her floorboards in a slow spread. He had nothing in his hands. That was the thing she noticed, absurdly, past everything else: no book to return, no parcel, no flowers, no prop of any kind, both hands empty and still at his sides like a man who'd been told to put everything down and had decided, for once, to comply. He didn't smile. He didn't do the face. He looked tired in a way the red-carpet version of him had never once looked tired, and older, and — she hated knowing this, hated that her eye was trained for it now — *unlit*, standing in the wrong part of the room, making no use of the window at all. The shop was very quiet. The drip went in the bowl. The Swindon man's railway novel lay on the counter where she'd wrapped it, the only evidence of the whole day. He opened his mouth. Closed it. Whatever had been in it, he'd left it in the rain somewhere on Crown Lane, and he didn't reach for anything to replace it. Elin stood on the third step of the ladder with an armful of garden birds, and neither of them said a word, and the bell over the door went on swaying, very slightly, long after it should have stopped.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a chapter that operates almost entirely in the register of restraint, and it largely earns that restraint. The plan called for "time passing," the roof math, the trade piece, a memory of Glenys, and then the threshold moment — Griffin in the doorway, alone, unsmiling, nothing prepared, chapter ending before he speaks. The model executes this plan with considerable fidelity and, more importantly, with genuine craft. The opening section on the town's nostalgia is one of the chapter's strongest passages. "The sixth form had been extras in mud for a day and would be dining out on it until their own children were extras" is a genuinely good line — specific, funny, true to how small towns work, and free of sentimentality. The detail of Mrs Rees complaining about the quiet "in the tone of a complaint about God" is similarly precise and earns its place. The plan called for the town to be "briefly, honestly nostalgic for being interesting," and this is delivered without condescension or Llareggub-style whimsy. The roof math scene with Mair is efficient and characterful. The envelope reading "THE ROOF (NOT THE MAN)" is a perfect Mair detail — sharp, warm, slightly guilty, exactly as the character profile describes her. The five twenties smelling of apples is a lovely concrete touch. Mair's line "The town did that" and Elin's acknowledgment of it is handled with appropriate economy; the emotional weight is there without being pressed. The trade piece section is the chapter's most technically demanding passage, and it mostly succeeds. The model captures Elin's self-aware fury at being left "nothing she was allowed to trust" — the recognition that silence-as-performance is still performance, that refusal could be principle or could be another speed of the charm machine. "She bookmarked it. She unbookmarked it." is exactly the right kind of small, undignified, recognizable behavior. The plan specified she should "refuse to read into it" and be "angry at herself for keeping it," and both are present. The jaw-tight anger feels earned. The Glenys memory is handled with admirable discipline — two sentences, as the plan specified, no more. "Nobody warns you the building's the easy part" is a good line that does thematic work without announcing it. The model resists the temptation to expand or explain, which is the right call. The storm that misses them — "It went up the Irish Sea and gave its name to somebody else's week" — is a clean, unforced piece of writing. The weather as non-event, the ordinary wet grey days "with no audience at all," sets up the Tuesday scene without over-engineering it. The arrival scene is where the chapter reaches for its highest register, and it largely succeeds.
